3307228100.PlainText:
MDX: unintuitive error message when using default export as named import

Version
3.0.6
Platform
linux
What steps will reproduce the bug?
clone 
run deno task build
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
always
What is the expected behavior?
MDX works
What do you see instead?
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body:
The problem is you're importing a default export as a named export. This works fine:

```diff
-import { Video } from "./_components/Video.tsx"
+import Video from "./_components/Video.tsx"

Hello, World!

<Video controls />
```

Note that Lume components are automatically loaded, so you can do simply:

```mdx
Hello, World!

<comp.Video controls />
```

This also has the benefit that live reload works

The problem with this approach is Lume components automatically adds the page data as component properties. And your component renders all properties using a spread attribute. You can prevent the inherit behavior with `inheritData` property:

```jsx
export const inheritData = false; // Prevent to inherit all page data

export default async (props) => <video controls {...props} />
```

That being said, I thing SSX library shouldn't fail like it does. I will fix it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Updated [ssx with better error message](https://github.com/oscarotero/ssx/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

3323662301.Title: Lume `dest` absolute paths create relative directory
3323662301.Author: benstigsen
3323662301.PlainText:
Lume dest absolute paths create relative directory

Version
v3.0.6
Platform
Linux
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Let's say I'm at /home/ben/foo/:
This results in the destination folder /home/ben/foo/home/ben/bar, instead of the expected /home/ben/bar.
Making use of relative paths works though:
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
Happens with an absolute dest site path.
What is the expected behavior?
It should output the generated site at the absolute path.
What do you see instead?
It outputs the absolute path relative to the current working directory.
Additional information
No response

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Is there any reason for not using dest: "../bar/" ?

... hmm... no not particularly.

I did figure that it only worked with paths relative to the current directory. But it still surprised me when it didn't work with absolute paths.

718298716.Title: How to generate output in same directory structure as src?
718298716.Author: probins
718298716.Body: I'm wanting to convert a simple metalsmith site to run on deno, and Lume looks like a reasonable fit. One thing I'm stuck on is the structure of the output site. I want to convert `src/abc.md` to `site/abc.html` and `src/xyz.md` to `site/xyz.html`, but it puts each src file in its own directory, which I don't want. I tried using the `permalink` variable, but this makes no difference. Seems to be in `site.js` but I can't see any way to define this.
718298716.PlainText:
How to generate output in same directory structure as src?

I'm wanting to convert a simple metalsmith site to run on deno, and Lume looks like a reasonable fit. One thing I'm stuck on is the structure of the output site. I want to convert src/abc.md to site/abc.html and src/xyz.md to site/xyz.html, but it puts each src file in its own directory, which I don't want. I tried using the permalink variable, but this makes no difference. Seems to be in site.js but I can't see any way to define this.

Comments.Body:
Hi.
Yes, Lume creates pretty urls, so if you export a page as `/about-us`, the real filename is `/about-us/index.html`. But I understand that this is not always desirable. A quick fix is adding the `.html` extension to the permalink variable (ex: `permalink: about-us.html`). Didn't that work for you?

Anyway, I'm going to create a configuration option to disable permalinks, so you don't have to edit the permalink in every page.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Done!
The new version `0.7.0` has an option do disable prettyUrls. Just set `false` to this option in the `_config.js` file:

```js
const site = lume({
  prettyUrls: false
})
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
wow, that was a fast response :-)

Adding the permalink option to the pages makes no difference.

Now I'm having another problem, which is probably due to my limited knowledge of deno. I installed 0.6.0 yesterday with `deno install`. How do I update to the new version? There doesn't seem to be an update or uninstall option, and running install without a release no uses 0.6.0. Using `deno run` without a release no uses 0.6.0, and specifying 0.7.0 loads 0.7.0 `cli.js`, but then uses 0.6.0 for all other files, including `site.js`, which is the one that's changed. 😕
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: PS permalink works in 0.6.0 if I use `.htm`. `.html` is treated in the same way as no permalink.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, I didn't find a good way to manage and update versioning.
I've released `0.7.2` that fixes the problem with permalink.
Updating a package is the same as installing it, but you need to add the `-f` to force the installation (and override the previous installation), and `--reload` to remove the cache and reload all dependencies:

```sh
deno install -f --reload --unstable --allow-read --allow-write --allow-net https://deno.land/x/lume/cli.js
```

Note also that in `_config.js` you can return a lume instance of any version, different to what do you have installed in cli, for example:

```js
import lume from "https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.7.2/mod.js";
  
const site = lume();

export default site;
```

So, other way to update the version is just editing the version in the `_config.js` file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yay, that now works. Thanks for the lesson in using Deno. I see you can also use `-r` instead of `--reload`, so a bit less to type.

Moitas grazas (as you name your software with a Galician word, I assume you are galego :-))
Comments.Author: probins

752342974.Title: Multi-command scripts
752342974.Author: probins
752342974.Body:
I've been trying out the script runner. I have what's probably a common setup, where I convert md files to html, and then want to run other scripts to create other parts of the site. So the script looks something like:
```
mkdir newdir
cd newdir/
deno run xxx
```
I put this in a separate `.sh` file, which works fine. But I would prefer to include this in the Lume config file, and I can't find a way to do this. If I put the cmds in one line separated by `;`, then it makes dirs called `newdir;`, `cd`, etc. The same with separating the cmds with `&&`. I also tried using the sequential option, but it looks like it tries to `cd` before `mkdir` has completed, so that doesn't work either.

Any ideas?
752342974.PlainText:
Multi-command scripts

I've been trying out the script runner. I have what's probably a common setup, where I convert md files to html, and then want to run other scripts to create other parts of the site. So the script looks something like:
I put this in a separate .sh file, which works fine. But I would prefer to include this in the Lume config file, and I can't find a way to do this. If I put the cmds in one line separated by ;, then it makes dirs called newdir;, cd, etc. The same with separating the cmds with &&. I also tried using the sequential option, but it looks like it tries to cd before mkdir has completed, so that doesn't work either.
Any ideas?

Comments.Body:
Mmm, looks like multi commands are not supported by `Deno.run`

```js
Deno.run({ cmd: ["mkdir", "newdir", "&&", "cd", "newdir", "&&", "ls -al"]})
```

And executing them in different subprocesses does not follow the new cwd after the `cd` command:

```js
Deno.run({ cmd: ["mkdir", "newdir"]})
Deno.run({ cmd: ["cd", "newdir"]})
Deno.run({ cmd: ["ls -al"]})
```

I have to investigate how to fix this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've released 0.9.6 that fixes this.
You can upgrade easily with `lume --upgrade`

`cd` won't work when split into multiple processes, as described in https://askubuntu.com/questions/481715/why-doesnt-cd-work-in-a-shell-script - the `cd` only applies to that process, not to the following command/process. So all the commands needed have to be in the same process (presumably macOS handles this differently).

This doesn't explain this particular issue, but I'm sure it's related somehow, and if it's not going to work in my case anyway ...

I prefer to keep the directory tidy and keep the script commands in the config file, so what I could do is change my config file to create a temp file and then execute it. Lume could do this for me, i.e. keep the array of processes for commands that are unrelated, and change the sequential option to create a (executable) file and then run it. And change the docs to explain the difference. What do you think?

This works, but my script is currently set up to use Args, which a simple import won't have. So I'm thinking I will change myScript to export a function, import that into the config file, and then run it in myFunction with the appropriate param. I think that should work. That would remove the need for any subprocesses at all.

https://deno.land/manual/examples/subprocess has an example of using `Deno.run()` to do `deno run`, but then I would be using one Deno script (Lume) to run `Deno.run` to run `deno run` - which gets really confusing. 😕

Ok, new version released (v0.9.8) with the following changes:

- Fixed `cd` commands in linux
- Added support for javascript functions as scripts
- The arguments passed after `--` are stored in `site.flags`, so you can use it in your functions. For example:
```js
site.script("hello", () => 
    site.flags.forEach((flag) => console.log(`hello ${flag}`))
);
```
```
lume --run hello -- world galaxy universe
```

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've found the issue.
The script runner runs the function and then execute a `Deno.exit(status)`. Your function `writeFile` does not returns the promise, so it doesn't know that this is an asyncronous function, so it exits before the promise is resolved.
I've changed the logic so now it's not required to return a promise, so it should work fine.

I've also changed the way `--upgrade` works. Now it fetch the latest version from deno.land (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/cli.js#L83) and install this specific version (for example: `https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.9.9/cli.js`) so I think this fixes the cache problems. (of course, this change will be effective after this upgrade).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yes, that all seems to work now, thanks. I'd suggest adding some docs/examples on how to use flags. `site.flags` seems to always exist, so I can just do `process(site.flags[0])` and it will just use 'undefined' if there are no flags (which is what I want).
Comments.Author: probins

753432280.Title: Replace denopkg?
753432280.Author: probins
753432280.Body: I see you're using denopkg to load your fork of denjucks. I'd suggest using https://www.jsdelivr.com/ instead, as it caches and serves via cdn, which will be more efficient than simply downloading the raw source files from Github as denopkg does. (I tend to use statically.io, as it also handles GitLab as well as Github, but jsdelivr is well-established.)

761798340.Title: Pug support
761798340.Author: jaymakes11
761798340.Body:
Would _love_ to test out and use lume, but not without it supporting [Pug](https://pugjs.org) (the thought of writing actual HTML after using Pug makes me shiver). 

Is this currently possible?
761798340.PlainText:
Pug support

Would love to test out and use lume, but not without it supporting Pug (the thought of writing actual HTML after using Pug makes me shiver).
Is this currently possible?

Comments.Body: You would probably just need to create a plugin like the Eta one. For Deno compatibility you could just use a CDN like [esm.sh](https://esm.sh).
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
Yes, if we are able to run Pug in Deno, it would be easy to create a plugin for lume, as we have for [Eta](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/eta.js), [nunjucks](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/nunjuks.js) or [jsx](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/jsx.js).

I don't think automatic conversion with esm.sh is possible here, because these conversions don't work when we have to use the filesystem (for example to handle includes https://pugjs.org/language/includes.html) and seems like Pug don't have plans to support Deno yet (according with https://github.com/pugjs/pug/issues/3255).

One solution is creating a fork (as we have done with [denjucks/nunjucks](https://github.com/lumeland/denjucks)).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yeah, forking Pug seems the most feasible approach for this in the near future. Wish I had the bandwidth to take on something like that. 

Should any others, many +1s for your efforts. I'll be the first to start your repo :)

> I don't think automatic conversion with esm.sh is possible here, because these conversions don't work when we have to use the filesystem (for example to handle includes https://pugjs.org/language/includes.html) and seems like Pug don't have plans to support Deno yet (according with [pugjs/pug#3255](https://github.com/pugjs/pug/issues/3255)).
> 
> One solution is creating a fork (as we have done with [denjucks/nunjucks](https://github.com/lumeland/denjucks)).

@oscarotero [esm.sh](https://esm.sh) does automatically fill in Node specific libraries with the deno std node compatability library. It may be a little hard to look at [but the pug build](https://cdn.esm.sh/v9/pug@3.0.0/esnext/pug.js) is importing the result of `deno bundle --unstable https://deno.land/std/node/fs.ts` from [here](https://cdn.esm.sh//v9/_node_fs.js).
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000
oscarotero

Comments.Body: from https://github.com/jspm/project/issues/50#issuecomment-733887687 it looks like jspm is currently adding this as well.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: does that mean you can use nunjucks directly as well, rather than the denjucks fork?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins Yeah, probably does mean we can transfer from having to use a fork of Nunjucks. Using [esm.sh](https://esm.sh) really opens us to use many more NodeJS template engines.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: but it's only for those functions covered by Deno's Node compatibility layer, right? So it doesn't include for example net? A pity, as I use an API with my smart home software that I would prefer to run under Deno :-(
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: We should probably add a note for the plugins that says that it may be better to use template engines with native Deno support because those will probably have better support and stuff with errors and other problems.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: v0.10.0 released including the Pug plugin
Comments.Author: oscarotero

765462378.Title: Nunjucks in md files
765462378.Author: probins
765462378.Body: I'm converting an old Jekyll site to Lume, and one issue I'm running into is that the md files contain `{{ ... }}`, but it looks like these are not being parsed. https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/nunjucks/ says "just place the .njk or .html file outside the _includes folder", but in my case these are .md files, not .html. I tried a simple test with a layout which includes an md file with `{{ content | safe }}`: if I put `{{ myData }}` in the layout file, it is parsed correctly, but if I put it in the md file, it is included unparsed in the output html. Is this intentional, or a bug of some sort? Or do I have to define something else to get the Nunjucks variables in md files parsed?
765462378.PlainText:
Nunjucks in md files

I'm converting an old Jekyll site to Lume, and one issue I'm running into is that the md files contain {{ ... }}, but it looks like these are not being parsed.  says "just place the .njk or .html file outside the _includes folder", but in my case these are .md files, not .html. I tried a simple test with a layout which includes an md file with {{ content | safe }}: if I put {{ myData }} in the layout file, it is parsed correctly, but if I put it in the md file, it is included unparsed in the output html. Is this intentional, or a bug of some sort? Or do I have to define something else to get the Nunjucks variables in md files parsed?

Comments.Body:
At this moment, there's no way to mix nunjucks and markdown. Maybe I could create a new file extension, let's say `*.md.njk`, so the file execute nunkjucks first and then render the result as markdown.

Anyway, one way to do this now is by changing the extensions of these files. For example, instead `index.md`, change to `index.njk`. So, even if the content is markdown code, it's transformed by nunjuks. Then, use the wrapper layout of the page to render markdown, for example:

index.njk:
```njk
---
title: Hello world
layout: main.njk
---
# {{ title }}
```

main.njk:
```njk
<html>
    <body>
         {{ content | md | safe }}
    </body>
</html>
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yes, that works, thanks.

Rather than creating a new extension, I'd suggest making the whole process more configurable. Static sites can be built from any number of sources, for example, from cloud-based databases. They aren't useful for data that changes frequently, but they're perfect for publishing other data at little or no cost. Templates can be used in any type of file, for example, in js or css. So, it would be good to have the option to define in the config file which part of the build is run in which order, for example, run this script to fetch data from database, then run Nunjucks on the md files in dirA, then convert md files in dirB to html, and so on. There's currently the ability to run scripts from the command line, but it would also be useful to be able to just run Nunjucks or md conversion on a directory or even a single file, without having the recreate the whole site every time.

No hurry, of course, I can use what's there atm. Just something to think about for future development. 😄
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
1tty has a nice option `templateEngineOverride` to do that: https://www.11ty.dev/docs/languages/#overriding-the-template-language

Having something like that, you could configure how a specific file is rendered or, if you save the config value it in a `_data.*` file, it would affect to all files in that directory.
What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yes, having the ability to specify md only or md+template language both globally and at a directory level would solve this particular problem. I found a further problem here yesterday: I set up a couple of njk files as you suggested, which worked, but I then added some md files in the same directory, and it stopped working. I had to put the md files in a separate subdir for it to work again.

I can put other suggested improvements to the flow in separate issues.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've added support for the `templateEngine` variable.
Starting from v0.10.0 (not released yet) you can do things like this: https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/custom-template.md (run `njk` first and then `md`) and the result is https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/custom-template.html
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yeah, that's much more flexible, thanks. Are you planning on releasing 0.10 soon? If so, I'll wait. Otherwise, I could download the current master and try with that.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Probably today
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: v0.10.0 released.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I can't get this to work. To simplify, if `mydata` consists of a list of `title` and `no`, in my page file `page.html`, I have:
```
---
layout: layout.html
templateEngine: njk,md
---
{% for title, no in mydata %}
* {{ title }}: [{{ no }}](/items/{{ no }}.html){% endfor %}
```
and I'm ending up with lines of:
```
* title: [no](/items/no.html)
```
where title and no are the correct values for each line.

So, Nunjucks is substituting the data correctly, but the result is not being converted from md into html.

Am I missing something?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
I just tested your example and it works fine to me:
https://github.com/lumeland/test/commit/4550c5b2c430930004b3d95627636f96a3618c91

Maybe it is something related with cache (again). Try to clean deno cache and reinstall lume again.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yes, now works; it was indeed the cache. Looks like cache reload does not work properly. ☹️
Comments.Author: probins

766892057.Title: Outward Facing Types
766892057.Author: shadowtime2000
766892057.Body: Deno has builtin Typescript support so I was a little surprised to see this written in JS. I can understand not wanting to use Typescript, but I think this should have files like the plugins and the site written in typescript so people can use ts if they want it without writing there own types. We could then keep the internals of it written in JS. I could do this.
766892057.PlainText:
Outward Facing Types

Deno has builtin Typescript support so I was a little surprised to see this written in JS. I can understand not wanting to use Typescript, but I think this should have files like the plugins and the site written in typescript so people can use ts if they want it without writing there own types. We could then keep the internals of it written in JS. I could do this.

Comments.Body: Alternatively we could do a `.d.ts` for every file that should be used externally, though that could be a little harder to maintain with new features and stuff.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
I'm not a big fan of Typescript but understand that for some people it's useful. Is required to be lume in Typescript to create 3rd party stuff in Typescript?

All the public api is in the `site.js` file (it has all functions to configure, register plugins, loaders, template engines, etc).
Is the file `site.d.ts` enough? Or we need to create a `.d.ts` for every file?

Is possible to have the types as jsdocs?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero 

> Is possible to have the types as jsdocs?

[Types as JSDocs is available in the Node Typescript version](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/type-checking-javascript-files.html) though I don't believe it works in Deno. Also, I believe the JSDoc typings are only used internally and aren't used to type check when someone imports it outside of the project (possible wrong here).

> All the public api is in the `site.js` file (it has all functions to configure, register plugins, loaders, template engines, etc).
Is the file `site.d.ts` enough? Or we need to create a `.d.ts` for every file?

A `site.d.ts` could work, but it would require you to put a comment like this above the import i believe
```js
//@deno-types="https://cdn.jsdelivr.com/github/lumeland/lume/site.d.ts"
```
Since all the plugins are closures, I am assuming that is so because later options and configuration support would be added to them. In that case there would need to be a `.d.ts` for every plugin file, and they would have to write (in my opinion an ugly) comment above the imports. So if a somewhat advanced static site used all builtin plugins (12 atm), they would have to write 12 of those comments above every plugin import, and another for the site import.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
Ok, understood.
So there are two options to add typings: by creating a bunch of `.d.ts` files or rewriting the whole library to Typescript.
At this moment, I don't want to do any of them. `.d.ts` requires a lot of work and maintenance (and I agree that it's ugly). And rewrite all lume to Typescript requires work too. Note that this library uses some dependencies converted directly from jsdelivr, jspm and esm.sh. I think Typescript can introduce more complexity and I'd like to keep things simple.

Maybe, in a future `1.0` version, when all dependencies are loaded from deno.land, a Typescript version makes more sense.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero Or we could convert the `site.js` and the plugins to `.ts` and put in an `any` or an `unknown` for internal stuff.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: Related #104
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ok, finally lume [0.25.0](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/releases/tag/v0.25.0) is fully written in Typescript.
So I can close this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

770128485.Title: Nunjucks for js files
770128485.Author: probins
770128485.Body:
In #11, I mentioned being able to use templates with js files, so I can for example populate an array from shared data. You can do this In Jekyll (and can define `layout: null` for non-html files). I hoped that if I specified a `_data.json` with `templateEngine: njk` it would work, but it ignores the directory. If I rename the js file to `*.njk`, then it creates an output html, but doesn't parse the templates.

In the particular case I'm working on, I can get around this by including the js in the html file, but that's not very efficient if I have several html pages that use the same js.
770128485.PlainText:
Nunjucks for js files

In #11, I mentioned being able to use templates with js files, so I can for example populate an array from shared data. You can do this In Jekyll (and can define layout: null for non-html files). I hoped that if I specified a _data.json with templateEngine: njk it would work, but it ignores the directory. If I rename the js file to *.njk, then it creates an output html, but doesn't parse the templates.
In the particular case I'm working on, I can get around this by including the js in the html file, but that's not very efficient if I have several html pages that use the same js.

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure to understand your case. Can I see an example?
Note that `.js` files are not interpreted as pages (they could be javascript that you want to execute in frontend). You need to use the extension `.tmpl.js`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins

Comments.Body:
Understood.

lume ignores all files with extensions not recognized by any template engine. So, for example `page.foo`, or `document.doc` are ignored (you can copy them but not render as a page).

In theory, if you save the file as `my.js.njk`, it should be loaded and rendered by njk, remove the `njk` extension and save it as `my.js`. This is not what happens now (it's saved as `my.js.html` because the html extension is added always), but I'm going to release a new version with this fixed.

Other option is include a frontmatter with the permalink value, so you can decide the exact output name of this file. Create a `my.njk` file with this content:

```
---
permalink: my.js
---
let apis = {};
{% for type, key in apikeys %}
apis["{{ type }}"] = "{{ key }}";
{% endfor %}
```

Or even shorted

```
---
permalink: my.js
---
let apis = {{ apiKeys | dump }};
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
ok, thanks - I'll try this out this afternoon. (Thanks for reminding me of `dump`, too; Jekyll's default templating has `jsonify` which is much the same.)

To give you some context, I used to use PHP servers, and used this sort of templating a lot. However, for sites where the data doesn't change very often, static site generators will do just as well. I started off using Github Pages, which was limited to Jekyll, but I switched to GitLab a few years ago, where I can run anything. Deno/Lume is way faster than Jekyll: the site I'm working on took several minutes with Jekyll, but <1 minute with Lume, and most of that is loading the Docker image.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Oh, interesting! Thanks for let me know :)

I did some performance tests some time ago (using this repo: https://github.com/seancdavis/ssg-build-performance-tests) but I think there's plenty room for improvements yet.

FYI, Yesterday I've released a new version that allows to have support for `my.js.njk` file, so you don't need to create a frontmatter with the permalink value (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#0102---2020-12-17).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: this doesn't seem to work. I renamed the file to `xxx.js.njk`, and changed the code to `let apis = {{ apiKeys | dump | safe }};` (it's json, so has to be 'safe', otherwise I get a lot of `"`). It correctly renames the file to `xxx.js`, but outputs `let apis = ;`. This time it isn't a problem with the cache, as I deleted the cache and re-ran - but same problem.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: oh, and another problem is that it wraps it in the default layout file. I could get around this by creating an empty layout. Jekyll has the option `layout: null`.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: I'll take a look.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just tested your use case and it works fine to me.

In this directory: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/pages

There's a `_data.yaml` file with a default layout and some variables. And the `styles.css.njk` file outputs a css file with these variables and override the layout value with `null`. Here you can see the result: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/_expected/pages

So I suspect something is wrong in your side.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yes, sorry, my bad. I had the js in a new dir, and forgot to copy `_data` over. Duh! Works fine, including `layout: null`. 👍
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
> Deno/Lume is way faster than Jekyll: the site I'm working on took several minutes with Jekyll, but <1 minute with Lume, and most of that is loading the Docker image.

just for interest, here's a screenshot of recent commits to my site (>1300 pages). Spot the difference!
![Screenshot 2020-12-20 at 08 58 24](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/283514/102709393-294d5080-42a2-11eb-9a97-2291a6135114.png)
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Wow, impresive! Thanks
Comments.Author: oscarotero

770446146.Title: Compilation of Layouts for Faster Builds
770446146.Author: shadowtime2000
770446146.Body: Many template engines (like Eta) support compilation of a template into a function which can make it faster. What if we allowed compilation with layouts to allow faster builds? Some template engines don't support this, so we could still keep the run every time rendering open as an option.
770446146.PlainText:
Compilation of Layouts for Faster Builds

Many template engines (like Eta) support compilation of a template into a function which can make it faster. What if we allowed compilation with layouts to allow faster builds? Some template engines don't support this, so we could still keep the run every time rendering open as an option.

Comments.Body:
Yep, sure. Performance is super important.
Do you have any idea of how to implement this? If you want to contribute with a PR, that would be great.

Precompiled templates should be updated if we detect changes in the files (with `--serve`). For example, nunjuks has an internal cache that does not update so I had to clean it manually before every build https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/nunjuks.js#L14
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I think I can implement this with a cache based on layout filepaths (possibly expand to just templates everywhere?) to hold the functions. Yeah, I am going to have to look into updating precompiled templates on detecting changes.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
Nice! Thank you.
FYI, this is the function called to perform an update after detecting changes: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L232
This function receives an array of files that have changed, in order to be reloaded again (I know, with Typescript it would be more obvious :) and finally build the site again.
Maybe we can pass this array of files to every template engine used, so they can update its internal cache if they need to.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Now that precompilation and cache was implemented in nunjuks, eta and pug, I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

771354154.Title: Use esm.sh Nunjucks?
771354154.Author: shadowtime2000
771354154.Body: I was wondering if you will switch to use [esm.sh](https://esm.sh) for Nunjucks.
771354154.PlainText:
Use esm.sh Nunjucks?

I was wondering if you will switch to use esm.sh for Nunjucks.

Comments.Body: +1 from me (assuming it works 😄). My experience of forks is that they are easy to create, but not at all easy to maintain (though Nunjucks is probably pretty stable and won't change very much).
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, it's an option.
In fact, I'm using a fork of a fork (because the [original fork](https://github.com/denjucks/denjucks) seems like is not maintained anymore).
I did some changes to that fork, like removing some dependencies that was failing. Compare the [deps file of my fork](https://github.com/lumeland/denjucks/blob/master/src/deps.js) with the [original file](https://github.com/denjucks/denjucks/blob/master/src/deps.js), the only dependency is a Deno std library. So I feel that my fork is pretty stable.

The advantages to switch to esm.sh is that we can update anytime to use new features and improvements of the original nunjucks library. But the cons are that we are not really using the original library, but an autogenerated version that can introduce bugs and load more javascript (due dependencies and stuff).

I think that we can continue using the fork for now but keeping a eye [on the changelog of the original Nunjucks](https://github.com/mozilla/nunjucks/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md). When the new version is released, we can reconsider again if making the change worth it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Can you use the browser version of Nunjucks? There are 2 basic issues here, right? Converting code from CJS to ESM; converting Node-specific functions like fs. The browser version should use standard js with no dependency on Node-specific functions. The current min.js has a UMD wrapper, which would need to be changed to `export default`. The best is for Nunjucks to produce ESM output, but downloading and editing the wrapper on each release should be easier than trying to maintain a fork.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: The browser version uses ajax to load the templates (You can [see the code here](https://github.com/mozilla/nunjucks/blob/master/nunjucks/src/web-loaders.js#L61)). Deno does not support XMLHttpRequest and [won't support it](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/2191) (it support `fetch`, a more modern API). And even if it were suported, it wouldn't be so efficient as using the filesystem API.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: ok. A pity. I suppose the underlying problem is that so much of the JS code out there is out-of-date. I see Nunjucks' build process is still using uglifyjs, which doesn't support ES2015, let alone more modern JS. Ideally, all this code would be updated, but that's a mammoth task.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Automatic conversors like esm.sh do not work always. For example, I had to port Pug to Deno by myself because the conversion from esm.sh conflicts with the Typescript of Eta: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/18
So the most stable/reliable solution is using a fork or port even if they are not updated so often as the original library.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

776201960.Title: Benchmarks
776201960.Author: shadowtime2000
776201960.Body: It would be great to see some build time benchmarks compared to popular SSGs such as Jekyll and 11ty. @probins did share the speed increase in his sites build time compared to Jeykll.
776201960.PlainText:
Benchmarks

It would be great to see some build time benchmarks compared to popular SSGs such as Jekyll and 11ty. @probins did share the speed increase in his sites build time compared to Jeykll.

Comments.Body:
The times I posted were the total time setting up GitLab Pages, where much of the time is spent loading the Docker image and deploying the pages, not just running Lume. Deno programs are slim by design. To run Lume, you just need Deno and not much else. So you can use a small Docker image such as Alpine Linux with Deno already included. For Jekyll, you have to use the Ruby image, which is a lot larger and takes a lot longer to load. Jekyll also has a lot more baggage. Node is much the same: you have to spend a lot of time installing a large number of 'required' packages, much of which you probably don't need. You could of course create your own custom Docker image with everything you need already loaded, which would be faster to run.

I suppose it depends on what you want to compare. In my case, I'm interested in the total time from `git push` to deployment. If you just want comparisons of actual run time, you would install everything first, and then run the different systems one after another.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:

776386306.Title: async filters
776386306.Author: oscarotero
776386306.Body:
Nunjucks allows to use async filters but they need to be specified on register:

`env.addFilter(name, func, [async])`

So, we need to add this argument to lume, with `site.filter(name, func, [async])` and check if it works fine.
776386306.PlainText:
async filters

Nunjucks allows to use async filters but they need to be specified on register:
env.addFilter(name, func, [async])
So, we need to add this argument to lume, with site.filter(name, func, [async]) and check if it works fine.

Comments.Body: Released in `v0.10.7`
Comments.Author: oscarotero

776389331.Title: new filters
776389331.Author: oscarotero
776389331.Body:
There are some filters that I'd like to add to lume:

- [ ] `inline`: to inline content in the html (css, javascript, svg, etc). Example:

```njk
<style>
    {{ "/css/my-styles.css" | inline | safe }}
</style>
```
This will return the content of the css file.


- [ ] `css`: The css plugin could register the css filter, so we can not only inline css but also transform it:

```njk
<style>
    {{ "/css/my-styles.css" | inline | css | safe }}
</style>
```

- [ ] `bundle`: We can do the same with the `bundler` plugin, to transform javascript/typescript.
- [ ] `svg`: We can do the same with the `svg` plugin, to transform svg.
776389331.PlainText:
new filters

There are some filters that I'd like to add to lume:
[ ] inline: to inline content in the html (css, javascript, svg, etc). Example:
This will return the content of the css file.
[ ] css: The css plugin could register the css filter, so we can not only inline css but also transform it:
[ ] bundle: We can do the same with the bundler plugin, to transform javascript/typescript.
[ ] svg: We can do the same with the svg plugin, to transform svg.

Comments.Body: by 'bundler plugin', do you mean having a plugin to do `deno bundle`? swc, on which Deno is built, is working on implementing a port for Terser to add to this. I'm using denopack, which uses the browser version of Rollup/Terser, for bundling/minifying. Their [roadmap](https://github.com/denofn/denopack/issues/8) mentions adding 'static generation', so perhaps there's scope for working together?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
The idea is that existing processors like css (which uses postcss) or svg (which uses svgo) can be used also as template filters, to transform inline sources.

The `bundle` processor uses the built-in `Deno.bundle()` (see here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/bundler.js).

Any other bundler like denopack can be used (it's really easy to create more plugins for Lume). I'll take a look to Denopack.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
ok. Maybe an example in the docs of how to create a plugin? There's a couple of issues with Denopack, but basically it works well. I'll look into creating a Terser plugin, which would be useful for minifying js without bundling.

I'd suggest standardising the docs to use the same terms throughout. For example, the home page talks of "transformers", but I don't think this word is used anywhere else. Does this mean both plugins and filters? https://lumeland.github.io/creating-pages/filters/ says, under `md`, "It's installed by the markdown plugin", which is correct, but at the top it says "New plugins must be registered" which I think should be 'filters'.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, right. "New plugins must be registered" is a typo, it should say "New filters". I'm fixing it.
I have to complete the docs with info about extending Lume with new plugins, transformers, filters, etc.
I just added docs for loaders (https://lumeland.github.io/advanced/loaders/).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I just added some documentation for processors, engines and plugins.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: looks like deno_swc uses Terser (via esm.sh and Wasm) for minification. Seems rather a roundabout way of using Terser :-). https://github.com/swc-project/swc/pull/1302 is swc's current work on adding a Terser port. https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/6900 is Deno's issue for incorporating this in Deno bundle. denopack's using the browser version of Terser looks like the best way forward to me. A Lume plugin could easily do something like the shell script in https://github.com/terser/terser/issues/544#issuecomment-724663556.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Have you tried https://github.com/timreichen/Bundler?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: looks interesting - something else for me to investigate. :-) Also uses Terser from esm.sh.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: AFAICS, Bundler does no tree-shaking. So I'm sceptical how much use it is for browsers. Might be ok for Deno though, where bundle size is not so important.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Instead creating filters for every processor (css, bundle, svg etc), I finally created the `inline` processor, to insert automatically in the html the code of any resouce with the `inline` attribute. For example: ```html ``` Is converted to: ```html ```

Yes, I think I may have misunderstood too. Examples please.

If we're talking about bundling, `deno bundle` is very basic, and currently doesn't minify. Rollup, as used by Denopack, is very mature, does tree-shaking and code splitting, and has a large number of options, such as producing sourcemaps.

Comments.Body:
the standard convention is to have source code in `src` or `lib`, so you could ignore that and have one js file in your site folder that does `import from 'src/whatever.js'`. I'll try that out for running denopack. I think it would probably be better to be able to run a bundler on just one file, rather than everything with a `.js` extension.

I've just tried out `deno bundle`, and find it has a major issue. When you import modules and then run something, it should bundle the imported modules first and then your code, so any initialisation in the imported modules is run first. But, for some reason, it put some of the imported initialisation after my code in the bundle, so my code failed as the initialisation hadn't run. Duh!

If it can be added easily using a plugin, I can consider it (I'm not an expert of React or Vue). Do you have any idea of how implement this?

779281865.Title: Terser processor/plugin
779281865.Author: probins
779281865.PlainText:
Terser processor/plugin

Following the discussion in other issues, I've created a _config.js to run all js files through the Terser minifier:
I've not thorougly tested the output yet, but in principle it works well. It's not a 'static site' in the traditional sense, but is using Lume as a file processor to minify all files in lib/ into outlib/. Like Denopack, it imports the browser version of Terser, which creates the Terser global. Specifying globalThis is not strictly necessary, but makes it clear where this Terser reference is coming from.
So, I can either document this as an example of what can be done, or create a processor and/or plugin for general use. Another issue mentions passing options to plugins, and this would also be useful here, for example, whether to generate sourcemaps (the appropriate module would have to be imported to support this, of course).

Comments.Body: @probins I don't think we have to import the UMD build and use the `globalThis`, Terser exports native browser ES modules [in `/main.js/`](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/terser@5.5.1/main.js).
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: I just tried that, but it fails trying to `import MOZ_SourceMap from "source-map";`. Could get around that with an import map, but ISTM it's simpler to use the browser version.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins You could file a bug report for that, but until that is fixed we could import from [skypack](https://www.skypack.dev/view/terser), I checked it and it maps the `source-map` dependency to an ES module link. It should work, haven't tested it.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: yeah, but you're into the same issues discussed with other plugins. Using skypack, jspm or esm.sh is essentially a workaround, and you can't rely on Node code remaining compatible with Deno. Browser code OTOH should be compatible, even if using globals is not ideal.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Ok. Do you like to create a PR with this plugin? Maybe modify the current bundler plugin to use Terser that seems like it produce better results for browsers.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins

Comments.Body:
To create a new page, just need to create the page and add it with `site.pages.push(page)`.
You can see an example in the `css` plugin: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/css.js#L31
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body: but the processors don't have access to the plugin options - `css` only works because the processor is in the same file as the plugin. Is the aim not for processors and plugins to be independent?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: in any case, `_data` files are already used for configuration: `layout`, for example.
Comments.Author: probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thinking about this a bit more, it's not just the sourcemap option that needs to be defined, it's all the terser options. In principle, these could be different for each file, but if that's what the user wants then they can use Terser directly. In a process like this, you would want the same for all files. A plugin could use sensible defaults, which users could change if they wish. The user could set this in `site.data("terserOptions")` which the plugin would have access to. If `terserOptions.sourceMap` is set, then it could create the extra files. `site` is already used in the processor, so this wouldn't be adding to the closure.
Comments.Author: probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I would call it 'configuration data'. :-) Have to say I don't see the difference between using data to specify whether a file should have a sourcemap, and using data to specify whether a file should be minified, as in https://lumeland.github.io/advanced/processors/

As sourcemap support comes from another module/package, alternative ways to include them would be:
* have 2 separate plugins, one with and one without
* use `import()` to fetch the sourcemap code conditionally:
````js
 if (sourceMap === true) {
  await import('sourcemapfile');
  output sourcemap
}
````
I think I'll raise an issue on Terser to ask them to create an ES-module version for use in Deno as well as browser. This implies they would have to either bundle in the source map code or import it where needed.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
I've just been looking at [the code](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L357), and it looks like the processor function is passed the `site` instance `this`, so there's no need for the closure, and processors can be put in an independent module if wanted without any problem. I don't mind whether the Terser options are put in `site.extraData` or their own property.

One further question: is it possible to chain/pipe processors? It might be useful, for example, to take the output from `bundler` and run it through Terser.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
> it looks like the processor function is passed the site instance this, so there's no need for the closure

Yes, I've added this in the last version. It could be needed if you want to pass options, but I see that you're not convinced about that.

> One further question: is it possible to chain/pipe processors?

Yes, you can register multiple processors for the same extension, so they are executed in that order.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@probins I am testing your Terser plugin and in some cases it returns an empty content.
For example, this nunjucks file: https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/nunjucks/to-javascript.js.njk outputs the following javascript code https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/nunjucks/to-javascript.js.

But after run terser, the output file is empty. Any idea of why this happens?
Other files like these: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/scripts works fine.

A quick `console.log` in the plugin looks like is a bug with Terser library, not the plugin itself. This is the content passed to terser:

```js
const apiKey = {"bar":"foo","foo":"bar"};

const urls = [
  "/subdirectory/bar",
  "/subdirectory/foo",
  "./foo",
  "../foo",
  "https://example-site.com/subdirectory/foo",
  "https://example.com/bar/foo",
];
```
And this is the result (note that the code only has the sourceMappingURL reference.
```
{
  code: "\n//# sourceMappingURL=to-javascript.js.map",
  map: '{"version":3,"sources":[],"names":[],"mappings":"","file":"/nunjucks/to-javascript.js"}'
}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Terser ignores code which doesn't do anything. If you add `console.log(apiKey, urls)` at the end, it will output minified code.

Or, more accurately, it leaves out unreferenced variables.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ah, got it.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero
oscarotero

Comments.Body: ok, that makes sense. But the docs will have to be changed too. I think this should be mentioned in https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/about-plugins/
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: also, I think `sourceMap` is the better name for the option rather than `map`. `map` can mean many things, whereas `sourceMap` is specific.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ok, I'll rename it to `sourceMap` to both.
I want to make Lume very easy to learn and use, so consistency in the API is key here. I think it depends of the type of plugin. For example, plugins that runs a processor like postcss, terser or svgo will use similar options structure. Other plugins to add support for template engines (nunjuks, eta, pug) don't need the same options (like `sourceMap`) but they should be consistent with each other.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

789860523.Title: Deno.bundle() removed in Deno 1.7.0

See https://deno.land/manual@v1.7.0/typescript/runtime for replacement

Comments.Body: Looks like `Deno.emit()` (like the old `Deno.bundle()`) cannot load a js/ts file passing the content, and resolve the imported modules automatically. If `sources` option is used, all imported scripts must be there. This makes this plugin a bit useless for lume. [question](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/689420767620104201/801548227429138522)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Looks like `Deno.emit()` (like the old `Deno.bundle()`) cannot load a js/ts file passing the content, and resolve the imported modules automatically. If `sources` option is used, all imported scripts must be there. This makes this plugin a bit useless for lume.

Bundlers usually work from an entry point, or 'rootSpecifier', as Deno calls it. They then fetch all the imported modules, and all the modules they import ... Not sure how that fits with Lume. I think it would only be useful if all the imports were external.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Yeah, the problem is that the main entry point may be a nunjuks file that generate a js file, or something like that. This is why I want to provide the content of the entry point and Deno.emit() resolve the other imports (something similar to what the postcss plugin does).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: ok, I see what you mean. With rollup, you can pipe the entry point contents from stdin. Don't know whether that works with `deno bundle`.
Comments.Author: probins

795671080.Title: Refactor CLI to use "commands"
795671080.Author: JordanShurmer
795671080.Body:
First, this project is great. Keep up the good work.

This is a proposal for a redesign of the CLI interface. I'd be happy to put in a PR for if it seems like a good thing to do.

# A Proposal

Redesign the CLI interface to use "commands" instead of options.

```
$ lume --help

  🔥lume v0.14.0
  A static site generator for Deno

  Docs:

    https://lumeland.github.io

  Usage:

    lume [OPTIONS] COMMAND

  Options:

    -h, --help     - Show this help.
    -V, --version  - Show the version number for this program.

  Commands:

    build [config]  - build the site
    init            - create a _config.js file for a new site
    serve           - Build the site and start a web server that refreshes automatically for every change
    upgrade         - Upgrade local lume install to the latest version
    update          - Update the lume version imported in _config.js to the latest
```

Each command would have its own `--help` output as well, specifying the relevant options for that command

---

If this looks like a good direction for **lume** I can get a PR ready sometime this week
795671080.PlainText:
Refactor CLI to use "commands"

First, this project is great. Keep up the good work.
This is a proposal for a redesign of the CLI interface. I'd be happy to put in a PR for if it seems like a good thing to do.
A Proposal
Redesign the CLI interface to use "commands" instead of options.
Each command would have its own --help output as well, specifying the relevant options for that command
If this looks like a good direction for lume I can get a PR ready sometime this week

Comments.Body: I really like this idea! 11ty is a lot simpler than Lume, and Lume is a lot more pluggable and flexible and configurable so I think it is fit that we adopt a more "powerful" CLI system. I think we could use [yargs](https://yargs.js.org) for this.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
I agree this would be better but, f you're going to do this, I think `lume COMMAND [OPTIONS]` would be more logical. The options only apply to some commands, and the help should make this clear. There is also the `run` command, and `dev`, though I'm not sure what effect this has.

I prefer `-v` to `-V` (and I wish there was consistency in this across all scripts/systems :-( ). I also think it would be useful if this command (or is it an option?) let the user know if a later version is available. Creating a separate `update` command raises another issue. The configuration docs use `import lume from "https://deno.land/x/lume/mod.js";`, i.e. always use the latest version. This means that if you have installed `lume` and you run it when a newer version is available, it uses the new version, but the installed script is not updated. So, `-v` gives you a different version from what is actually used. This needs redesigning, I think.

And one more thing: I think `build` should remain the default command.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Hi.
Thank you very much to bring this up. Casually, I have been working on a similar change a couple of days ago, in a different branch (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/feature/cli-refactor/cli/help.js). I like the idea of having a `--help` option for every command, I don't know if yargs can do that automatically.

I also agree that `build` should be the default command, so running `lume` or `lume build` are equivalent. To me, `--serve` is an option of `build`.

And about the `update`, this is an undocument command that I'm testing because Deno caches the modules forever, so no matter if you install the latest version of lume, if your `_config.js` file imports lume without version, it will use the cached version. Until now, I've fixed it by running [`deno cache` here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/cli.js#L111) but this should be used also with all plugins that are not loaded by default (`postcss`, `terser`, etc), so it's not the ideal situation. Maybe `update` is not the most apropiate name, and `fix-version` or something like that, is better. In addition to that, I found that deno checks always the types before runing:

```
lume
Check https://deno.land/x/lume/cli.js
Check file:///Users/oscarotero/my-site/_config.js
...
```

And using fixed versions, this check is done only the first time, then it's cached, so it's a lot faster.

If you want to contribute to the work done in this branch, PR are welcome.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Awesome. Thanks for the quick feedback. Good to see work already being done on this front @oscarotero.

I will check out that branch and contribute where I can.
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body:
> if your `_config.js` file imports lume without version, it will use the cached version

I just retested this and you're right. A couple of weeks ago, I ran `lume` (the previously upgraded version, no longer the latest) and it fetched the latest version. I thought this was caused by the config file, but it must have been something else. So what's the use case for having both update and upgrade? Both the config file and the cli are importing `mod.js`, so these should surely always use the same version.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins Yes, correct. It's a way to ensure that cli.js and mod.js and plugins are using the same version. This is also critical because all these modules [share the same cache system](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/mod.js#L16). And if they use different versions, they use also different caches.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
an alternative is to have not a config file but a build file. I just created a copy of `_config.js` with `site.build()` at the end instead of `export default site`. Then `deno run -A --unstable ./_build.js`. Seems to produce the same result. I can set the version in the import of `mod.js`, and if I want to use a new or other version of lume, I just change the import in the build file.

It's quite common in Node to have a JS API version as well as cli, and this is similar. It removes the need for an installed script, and the problem of updating it.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
The cli api has also `lume --serve` that not only runs a build but also watch changes and update the site accordingly. Or `lume --run` to run a script registered in the site. So this is why `_config.js` export the site instance, in order to perform different commands with the same site instance.

Btw, you don't need to copy `_config.js`, just import it with `import site from "./_config.js"`.

Anyway, why do you think this is an alternative for the caching issue?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: if I no longer use the installed script, I don't have to upgrade it. I specify directly in the build script which version I want to use, so the dependencies should all be loaded from that version. If I also define the same version for the plugin imports I'm using, any cache problems should go away, right?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, correct. In fact, Deno recommends to use always versioned imports (I read it, don't remember where). This is what `lume --update` does, normalize the imported lume modules in the _config.js file to the same version (it uses the same version of cli but is not required).

Because the problem is not that the installed version (cli.js) is a different version as the imported modules in _config.js, because the cli code only runs the site instance returned by _config.js. The problem is when the _config.js imports several lume modules from different versions (for example: `mod.js` is v0.8.1 and `plugins/terser.js` is v0.9.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
but that's precisely my point: if both the _config.js and the cli define the version no, and the 2 are updated by 2 separate commands, then there is always the chance they will be out of sync. It's also more work for the user. Perhaps `upgrade` could always run `update`.

Anyway, I'm changing all my configs to include `site.build()`, and will see if I encounter any problems. I'm also using this kind of process in other Deno systems I'm using, as ISTM it's simpler than maintaining installed scripts.

I think this issue can be closed.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, if you don't need any extra functionality that cli provides (like run scripts, watch, server and live-reload features), it's perfectly fine don't install it.

The cli interface is similar to what other node libraries provide in a different package ([postcss-cli](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-cli), [webpack-cli](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-cli), etc). But in Deno, thanks to the ES module, we don't need to create different packages, because if you don't use it, this code won't be downloaded.

> Perhaps upgrade could always run update.

Maybe, but `update` updates the current _config.js file and you can have several projects with lume.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Closed by #34
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

801358004.Title: index.pug file changes not being written on index.html in --serve mode
801358004.Author: silviajoy
801358004.Body:
I have this folder structure:

```
_dist
_includes
	head.pug
config.js
index.pug
pug.pug
```

I noticed that changes made to the pug.pug file are detected and also written on _dest/pug/index.html, while changes made to index.pug file are detected, but not actually written on _dest/index.html file

_config.js:
```
import lume from "https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.14.0/mod.js";
import pug from "https://deno.land/x/lume/plugins/pug.js";

const site = lume({
    dest: "_dist",
    prettyurls: true,
  });
site.use(pug());

export default site;
```

index.pug:
```
//- index.pug
doctype html
html
  include _includes/head.pug
  body
    h1 My Site!
    p Welcome to my super lame site!
```

pug.pug:
```
//- pug.pug
doctype html
html
  include _includes/head.pug
  body
    h1 Pug
```

_includes/head.pug
```
head
    title Hello Pug
```

P.S.
I noticed everything works just fine if the index is a markdown file, the problem gets out only on index.pug file
801358004.PlainText:
index.pug file changes not being written on index.html in --serve mode

I have this folder structure:
I noticed that changes made to the pug.pug file are detected and also written on _dest/pug/index.html, while changes made to index.pug file are detected, but not actually written on _dest/index.html file
_config.js:
index.pug:
pug.pug:
_includes/head.pug
P.S.
I noticed everything works just fine if the index is a markdown file, the problem gets out only on index.pug file

Comments.Body:
Hi.
It looks like the pug plugin is from a different version. Could you try to import the same version of the pug plugin?
In `_config.js`, instead:
```
import pug from "https://deno.land/x/lume/plugins/pug.js";
```
change to
```
import pug from "https://deno.land/x/lume@0.14.0/plugins/pug.js";
```

you can do it automatically running `lume update`.

Anyway, I need to make some changes to ensure that different plugins versions can work together. Thanks for notify this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just released v0.15 that should fix this bug. To update your project:
```
lume upgrade
lume update
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

809360646.Title: Changes in Pug layout files not updating (with `lume --serve`)
809360646.Author: jaymakes11
809360646.Body:
Example:

```pug
//- _layout.pug

html.no-js(lang="en")
   head
      meta(charset='utf-8')
   body
      div A message from `_layout.pug`
      block pageContent


//- home.pug

extends _layout.pug
block pageContent
   h1 Home
```

Making a change in `home.pug` triggers an update. Making a change in `_layout.pug` does _not_ trigger an update.

When making a change in `_layout.pug`, the cli does say "Changes detected. Building... Done", but the browser does not reload and Lume doesn't seem to actually rebuild (manually reloading the browser does not show the update).
809360646.PlainText:
Changes in Pug layout files not updating (with lume --serve)

Example:
Making a change in home.pug triggers an update. Making a change in _layout.pug does not trigger an update.
When making a change in _layout.pug, the cli does say "Changes detected. Building... Done", but the browser does not reload and Lume doesn't seem to actually rebuild (manually reloading the browser does not show the update).

Comments.Body:
Mmm, ok. Looks like pug caches the layouts loaded with `extends`. I'll try to fix it.
Meanwhile, you can use a frontmatter to configure your layout. Look this example: https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/pug/pug-example.pug
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ok, this bug is fixed in `0.15.1`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

823466269.Title: Updating post content with lume --serve generate incomplete index.html
823466269.Author: yadomi
823466269.Body:
Hello, I don't think this is the expected behavior, when updating a post while `lume --serve` is running, the index.html generated for the article is missing the whole template. I can reproduce this with the `base-blog`.

Steps to reproduce: 

1. run `lume --serve`
2. Go to http://localhost:3000
3. Click on a post
4. Edit the .md for that post
5. Reload the browser window
6. => The file contains only the html of the article without the template/layout html

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7341187/110180711-96d13d80-7e0a-11eb-816b-f6e243b214e6.mp4

Anyway, lume is still awesome 🚀🦕
823466269.PlainText:
Updating post content with lume --serve generate incomplete index.html

Hello, I don't think this is the expected behavior, when updating a post while lume --serve is running, the index.html generated for the article is missing the whole template. I can reproduce this with the base-blog.
Steps to reproduce:
run lume --serve
Go to http://localhost:3000
Click on a post
Edit the .md for that post
Reload the browser window
=> The file contains only the html of the article without the template/layout html
Anyway, lume is still awesome 🚀🦕

Comments.Body:
Hi, @yadomi 
Thanks for the detailed reporting. This is not the expected behavior, I'll fix it asap.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hi again, @yadomi 
I cannot reproduce this error. What's the version of lume you're using? (I have v0.15.2, run `lume upgrade` to upgrade to the latest version)

I see you're on Windows, maybe it's the reason, I have to get a windows machine to test it (I use macos)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hello, yes, indeed it look like it's windows specific as I've just tested on a Linux machine and I don't have the issue.
I'm using the latest version, `v0.15.2`
Comments.Author: yadomi

Comments.Body: @yadomi I just released a new version `v0.15.3` that fixes some issues related with Windows paths.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That was quick, you rock ! I've upgraded and it's working as expected. 

The issue can be closed then 🚀
Comments.Author: yadomi

823716231.Title: Image processing plugin
823716231.Author: kevinlang
823716231.Body:
It would be nice to have a plugin that can take a source image and output various optimized formats, leveraging `srcset` and the like to enable responsive images.

As far as I can tell, this is blocked by https://github.com/lovell/sharp/issues/2583 which is blocked by upstream Deno support.
823716231.PlainText:
Image processing plugin

It would be nice to have a plugin that can take a source image and output various optimized formats, leveraging srcset and the like to enable responsive images.
As far as I can tell, this is blocked by  which is blocked by upstream Deno support.

Comments.Body:
I've used sharp in other node projects and it's an incredible library, and a Deno version would allow us to create plugin similar to Eleventy (https://www.11ty.dev/docs/plugins/image/)

For now, the only way is a script on "afterBuild" event, for example using imagick:

```js
site.addEventListener("afterBuild", "convert -resize 800x800 _site/*.jpg");
```

But definitelly, if sharp is ported to Deno, we will have a image processing plugin.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
ImageScript has the `rewrite` branch that looks awesome: https://github.com/matmen/ImageScript/tree/rewrite
It includes a lot of improvements, like Typescript and more interpolation modes, so it's a good alternative to sharp.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I managed to hack something together with the afterBuild hook from @oscarotero and using the squoosh cli for now: https://github.com/enjikaka/barlingshult/blob/main/squoosh.js

As Squoosh is WASM; wouldn't that CLI, or Squoosh itself, be portable to Deno for this also? 🤔
Comments.Author: enjikaka

Comments.Body:
Squoosh is interesting. I never worked with WASM but looks like a Deno port should be feasible. If anyone want to work on this, that would be awesome.

BTW, @enjikaka I like you approach of using the HTML as the source to get and convert the images. It's similar to other plugins like [inline](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/inline.js). I'll change this plugin for something more similar to yours.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
If it's keeps raining outside I might give the Deno/WASM/Squoosh combo a try; otherwise garden word has higher priority here. 👨🏻‍🌾 

@oscarotero Glad you like it. I haven't thought it through completely either, so the HTML-approach might not be sustainable in the long run. But it worked for now at least. It would be a lot cleaner code with proper path handling and DOM parsing like in the inline plugin!
Comments.Author: enjikaka

Comments.Body: Garden has always priority over programing, agreed. And about the html-approach, yes, I'm refering to parse and use DOM API to search the images. I'm thinking on something like this: ```html ```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Hm, instead of `resize` maybe re-use srcset but without urls and add that in while transforming? In: ```html ``` Out: ```html ``` At first glance that feels like an easier API for an end-user, at least if familiar with srcet from before. That would need a check against exernal images though. The `resize` attribute option is better in that way; that it being defined or not would be checked for, instead of the src. `data-srcset` as input has the same pro, with the added benefit that the input doesn't have an invalid srcset or invalid attribute name.
Comments.Author: enjikaka

Comments.Body: More ideas: https://rbuchberger.github.io/jekyll_picture_tag/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I am experimenting with the new `imagick` plugin: https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/tree/main/imagick
It allows to process images using ImageMagick and generate different versions.

It uses `_data` files to assign different processing functions. Example:
- Modify the images in this directory, apply a resize and blur: https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/blob/main/imagick/demo/img/_data.ts
- Modify the images in this directory, generate three images with different sizes: https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/blob/main/imagick/demo/avatars/_data.ts
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero  Cool! How does it all hook up? I just see the plugin imported in _config.js but there's no reference to the method you export from _data.ts

On a side note I put my squoosh experiment in a repo of its own: https://github.com/enjikaka/lume_plugin_squoosh
Comments.Author: enjikaka
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Imagick plugin released: https://lume.land/plugins/imagick/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

849680624.Title: Bug: nunjucks not respecting filename case
849680624.Author: probins
849680624.Body: 0.16.0 introduced a file-naming bug. Up until 0.15.4, a file called `myFile.js.njk` would be correctly transformed into `myFile.js`, but since 0.16.0 is transformed into `myfile.js`. I assume this is linked to the 'slugifier' introduced in 0.16.0
849680624.PlainText:
Bug: nunjucks not respecting filename case

0.16.0 introduced a file-naming bug. Up until 0.15.4, a file called myFile.js.njk would be correctly transformed into myFile.js, but since 0.16.0 is transformed into myfile.js. I assume this is linked to the 'slugifier' introduced in 0.16.0

Comments.Body:
mmm, ok.
I don't recoment to use upper case filenames for the web, but I guess this should be optional, so I'm going to include a option to disable this behaviour, like we have for prettyUrls.
Thanks!

851000447.Title: Make `url` settable and replace `permalink` with it
851000447.Author: valtlai
851000447.Body:
Pages already have the `url` property, so if we could make it settable, the `permalink` property could be removed in favor of it. I think it would be nice if the getter (currently `url`) and setter (currently `permalink`) had a same name. Also that’s how `date` works.

This would be a breaking change, but we’re at `v0.x` so maybe it doesn’t matter. However, we can always make `permalink` an alias of `url` and possibly remove that later.
851000447.PlainText:
Make url settable and replace permalink with it

Pages already have the url property, so if we could make it settable, the permalink property could be removed in favor of it. I think it would be nice if the getter (currently url) and setter (currently permalink) had a same name. Also that’s how date works.
This would be a breaking change, but we’re at v0.x so maybe it doesn’t matter. However, we can always make permalink an alias of url and possibly remove that later.

Comments.Body:
I like it! Yes, initially there was only the permalink variable, and then I created the url for convenience. But you're right that they are almost the same and `url` is more clear about its purpose. 
I'm working on it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I close this because it's already implemented. It will be available in the next version
