852872876.Title: Using `site.copy()` with glob
852872876.Author: valtlai
852872876.Body:
I have a directory with both `.svg` and other files. I would like to copy the files with `site.copy("./assets")`, but then the `.svg` files inside are copied without going through the plugin. If `site.copy()` would support globs, I could do `site.copy("./assets/**/*.!(svg)")` to copy all but the `.svg` files in the directory.

If this gets implemented, maybe `site.ignore()` should support globs too.
852872876.PlainText:
Using site.copy() with glob

I have a directory with both .svg and other files. I would like to copy the files with site.copy("./assets"), but then the .svg files inside are copied without going through the plugin. If site.copy() would support globs, I could do site.copy("./assets/**/*.!(svg)") to copy all but the .svg files in the directory.
If this gets implemented, maybe site.ignore() should support globs too.

Comments.Body:
I didn't implement glob for performance reasons. Ignoring a directory is easier and faster because the [source scanner stops](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/source.js#L149) when it reaches to this directory so it doesn't continue scanning and evaluating the files inside this directory (think, for example in a node_modules folder).

Maybe, a way to do it without affecting to performance could be by separating the glob patterns and folders names in two different Map, but I haven't done much research.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
For `site.ignore()` you can just allow passing in a function like this: 

```
site.ignore((absFileName) => { // return true to keep, false to ignore })
```

This way you can support almost any rule.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: Implemented functions support to `site.ignore()`. I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

854116637.Title: Add ability to change sort direction for date
854116637.Author: kevinlang
854116637.Body:
I see this documentation here: https://lumeland.github.io/creating-pages/searching/

And I see that `date` is the default sort. As far as I can tell it dates in ascending order (oldest first). I would like to sort in descending order (newest first), but could not find how to.

Perhaps looking something like:
```
search.pages("type=post", "date=desc")
```

Cheers,
854116637.PlainText:
Add ability to change sort direction for date

I see this documentation here: 
And I see that date is the default sort. As far as I can tell it dates in ascending order (oldest first). I would like to sort in descending order (newest first), but could not find how to.
Perhaps looking something like:
Cheers,

Comments.Body:
I found how to do it, in my case just using nunjucks built-in `reverse` function.

```
search.pages("type=post", "date=desc") | reverse
```

May be worth adding docs, though.
Comments.Author: kevinlang

Comments.Body: ATM, the sort argument only accepts the field name (`date` by default), but it's a good idea to include support for asc/desc. I'll work on that. Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

854122652.Title: Date format does not behave as expected (off by one day)
854122652.Author: kevinlang
854122652.Body:
I have a template with the date specified as e.g., `2021-04-08`.

I would expect this formatting:
```
post.data.date | date('PPP')
```

To return:
```
April 8th, 2021
```

However, it returns:
```
April 7th, 2021
```

If I format with the `ATOM` format, I can see that the issue seems to be related to stripping off the timezone, or something.
```
post.data.date | date(`ATOM')
=> "2021-04-07T20:00:00-04:00"
```

(I'm in GMT-4)
854122652.PlainText:
Date format does not behave as expected (off by one day)

I have a template with the date specified as e.g., 2021-04-08.
I would expect this formatting:
To return:
However, it returns:
If I format with the ATOM format, I can see that the issue seems to be related to stripping off the timezone, or something.
(I'm in GMT-4)

Comments.Body: Mmm, interesting. Maybe we have to add an option to define a default timezone, or something like that.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
https://github.com/date-fns/date-fns/issues/489#issuecomment-570635228

Yeah, looks like this is a known issue. There are some workarounds in that thread, I linked to one above.
Comments.Author: kevinlang

Comments.Body:
Intead in the plugin, I've fixed when the date is detected: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/be274c7ecc7877a18dcc2a8158009168a9f99968

It's not published yet, but could you test it and confirm if it works fine to you?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Unfortunately it does not seem to have fixed the issue. It is still off by a day.

Here is how I ran the latest changes, in case that is somehow the issue (I'm not too familiar with deno):
1. Cloned this repo to an adjacent folder
2. Ensured that the commit above was present in the clone
3. From the adjacent folder that had my site, I ran `deno run --unstable -A ../lume/cli.js --serv`
Comments.Author: kevinlang
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Got it. I did that just now and the issue looks fixed. Thanks again for your assistance! :smile:
Comments.Author: kevinlang

Comments.Body: Great. You can undo this change in your _config.js and use the latest stable version after releasing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Mmm, interesting. Maybe we have to add an option to define a default timezone, or something like that.

The following seems to work:

```js
Deno.env.set("TZ", "Europe/Helsinki");
```

We could add an option to abstract that:

```js
const site = lume({
  timeZone: "Europe/Helsinki",
});
```

*****

There should also be a note in the docs to not use a time zone offset in date values. The following won’t work correctly after the commit be274c7ecc7877a18dcc2a8158009168a9f99968:

```njk
---
date: 2021-04-04 03:02:01 +03:00
templateEngine: njk,md
---

{{ date }}
```

On my machine (where the time zone is `Europe/Helsinki` and offset UTC+03:00 in DST), it outputs:

```
Sun Apr 04 2021 00:02:01 GMT+0300 (Eastern European Summer Time)
```

But without `+03:00` in the `date` field, it works correctly. Before the commit, it behaved on the contrary: correctly with a time zone offset and incorrectly without it.

That’s because the time zone offset defaults to UTC (`Z` = `+00:00`).
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
Thanks @valtlai You're absolutelly right, my commit was only a hack for this specific case, but it doesn't solve the problem.

I didn't know about `TZ` environment variable, and it looks a better solution because it will be applied to all `Date` instances created, not only to the `date` value.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Looks like `Deno.env.set("TZ", "Z");` does the job.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@kevinlang Finally I've reverted this change because it's not the best way to solve this problem.

For now, you can add `Deno.env.set("TZ", "Z")` to your `_config.js` file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
This is an old issue, but I'm basically having the same problem.  The date is behind by 1 day for me even after I set the TZ inside of the `_config.ts` file (America/Chicago).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44551614/204647662-5745e1a3-7fc9-4982-bff6-f5e110babb68.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44551614/204647734-9f3f799a-29ce-453b-8b02-569e892342ae.png)

In order to offset this somewhat, I change my TZ to Australia/Canberra and I then get the correct date.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44551614/204647859-7003facc-728f-4905-b582-160f3fcd2a7e.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44551614/204647939-cb7e0561-ec09-4800-8207-fc3672ff1353.png)

863889900.Title: How should `search.pages("foo,bar")` work?
863889900.Author: valtlai
863889900.Body: Should `search.pages("foo,bar")` find the pages having both a `foo` and `bar` tag (like `tags&=foo,bar`) or the pages having a `foo,bar` tag (like `tags*=foo,bar`)? The latter is the current behavior.
863889900.PlainText:
How should search.pages("foo,bar") work?

Should search.pages("foo,bar") find the pages having both a foo and bar tag (like tags&=foo,bar) or the pages having a foo,bar tag (like tags*=foo,bar)? The latter is the current behavior.

Comments.Body:
Spaces are the way to differentiate tags. To search pages with both tags, it should be `"foo bar"`. This was inspired by html `class` attribute in which `<div class="foo,bar">` is a `div` with one class named `foo,bar` not two classes.

The things got a bit complicated when I've introduced the ability to search by other properties with operators, so I had to add a way to search by several values in an array (to support the initial feature of searching by multiple tags), so introduced the comma. But this syntax is designed to be after an operator.

How should work `search.pages("one,two three")`? I think it should search pages with two tags: `one,two` and `three`, and it's equivalent to `search.pages("tags&='one,two',three")` (if quotes were supported). At this moment, there's no support to quotes in the comma-separated values, it could be implemented but I wanted to keep things simple for performance reasons.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I’m wondering could we simplify this:

https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/f50f509f20a1a7329264e653f2d3c009a4afe909/helpers/search.js#L82-L86

To this:

```js
arg = "tags*=" + arg;
```

Currently, the `*=` operator also works with arrays and I think that’s nice even if not originally intended.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
So you're proposing that searching for `foo bar` would be equivalent to `tags*=foo tags*=bar` instead `tags&=foo,bar` right? so we don't need the `&=` operator.

It makes sense.

We can even simplify more, removing the `|=` operator and use the pipe to separate values for the OR: `tags*=foo|bar`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've refactored the search engine to simplify things and give more flexibility:

- `foo bar` Search pages with these two tags
  - Equivalent to `tags*=foo tags*=bar`
- `foo|bar` Search pages with at least one of these two tags
  - Equivalent to `tags*=foo|bar`
- `title=foo` Search pages with the title `foo`
- `title=foo|bar` Search pages with the title `foo` or `bar`

Operators `&=` and `|=` are removed because they are not needed with this new syntax.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That looks very good. It feels simpler and is still more flexible because you can use alternatives (`|`) with all the operators.

Should the string matching be case-sensitive or would it be more useful to make it case-insensitive? In the latter case, `title*=foo` would match `Foo bar baz`, which makes sense to me.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
I'd leave them case-sensitive to simplify the code and avoid .toLowerCase() everywhere.

I'm also thinking in numbers. In yaml, any numeric value is converted to integer or float. This makes that searching by `order=12` won't return any value because the comparison is strict. For now, only "true" and "false" strings are converted to `true` and `false` booleans. Maybe it should do the same with numeric values. And it makes me think in `<` and `>` operator to search `order>12`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yeah, I agree that number comparisons should be possible. Maybe we also need the `<=` and `>=` operators in addition to `<` and `>` you proposed. I opened a PR for this: #78.

Maybe there also could be date comparisons, such as `date>=2021-03` or `date<2020-09-15`. At least the year and month would be required to syntactically differentiate the dates from numbers.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
Yes, dates would be great too.

I have been looking to how yaml parser convert the values to integer, boolean, date, etc. But it's too complicated (look for example integer conversion: https://deno.land/std@0.95.0/encoding/_yaml/type/int.ts)

Your solution of `isFinite()` is much more simple and enough for this case.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Updated search to add support for date and datetime. I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

871433496.Title: Outputting extensionless file (like `_headers`) from template
871433496.Author: valtlai
871433496.Body:
I would like to dynamically generate a `_headers` file to configure HTTP headers for Netlify. (And I’m going to do something similar with `_redirects` too.)

There’s no way to output a file named `_headers` from a template (i.e. without using `site.copy()`). Setting `slugifyUrls: false` in the config file and `url: /_headers` in a front matter results a file `_headers/index.html` (or `_headers.html` if `prettyUrls: false`).

The problem is that the `_headers` file has no file extension and Lume adds `.html` for missing one.

Should we stop adding the extension and only add `index.html` if `url` has a trailing slash? Then `url: /foo` would output `/foo` and `url: /foo/` would output `/foo/index.html`. Or should there be an option (such as `forceExtensions: false`) for this behavior?
871433496.PlainText:
Outputting extensionless file (like _headers) from template

I would like to dynamically generate a _headers file to configure HTTP headers for Netlify. (And I’m going to do something similar with _redirects too.)
There’s no way to output a file named _headers from a template (i.e. without using site.copy()). Setting slugifyUrls: false in the config file and url: /_headers in a front matter results a file _headers/index.html (or _headers.html if prettyUrls: false).
The problem is that the _headers file has no file extension and Lume adds .html for missing one.
Should we stop adding the extension and only add index.html if url has a trailing slash? Then url: /foo would output /foo and url: /foo/ would output /foo/index.html. Or should there be an option (such as forceExtensions: false) for this behavior?

Comments.Body:
I see some problems requiring trailing slash. For example if `prettyUrls: false`, you have to include the .html extension to all urls defined in the frontmatter.

One solution could be allow to set an object with properties for the url value. For example:

```yml
---
url:
  filename: _headers
  extension: false
---
```

The [`urlPage`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L447) function could use these properties to define the final name, instead the default behavior. I imagine available possible properties: `directory`, `filename` and `extension`. Not defined values (directory in the example above), will use the default value.

What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I like it. Should `filename` be `path`, because its value could include directories? What would `directories` do? Also, could there be `pretty` and `slugify` for overriding `prettyUrls` and `slugifyUrls` for a particular template?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
`directory` would be the directory where the file will be saved. For example, the output file `/posts/hellow-world/index.html` would be:

```yml
url:
  directory: /posts/hello-word/
  filename: index
  extension: .html
```

If the directory is not defined, the src directory of the file will be used.

But I like more your idea of using `path` and `extension`, because it's the same values that we have in `src` and `dest` of every page, so it's more consistent:

```yml
url:
  path: /post/hello-word/index
  extension: .html
```

The difference between using this object and a string like `url: /post/hello-word`, is that with the object we are defining the final output filename (the value of [`dest`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L464)) so I wouldn't apply the `pretty` and `slugify` transformations.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That makes sense and I think this looks pretty good now.

Should the value used to omit the extension be `null` or an empty string instead, because the variable isn’t a boolean?

So the solution for this specific use case would be the following:

```yaml
url:
  path: /_headers
  extension: null
```
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
> Should the value used to omit the extension be null or an empty string instead, because the variable isn’t a boolean?

Yes, a null or empty string is better. Or maybe nothing:

```yml
url:
  path: /_headers
```

In this case, the extension would be empty.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @valtlai I just implemented this new feature.  You can [see an example here](https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/headers.njk) and [the expected output here](https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/_headers).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That’s nice. However, relative paths aren’t supported with an object. Should them?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: Good point. I don't see why not. I'll add it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
One more question: How should a path with a trailing slash work?

```yml
url:
  path: /foo/
```

This currently throws an error. Is it ok or should it output `/foo/index.html` instead?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: I think it's ok the error. This is the explicit way to define the output file, so I wouldn't change the value introduced by the user.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That’s fine. I think this is ready, so closing.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
The commit 772f9e0fab314cd15cebb1482086f3b07e30e24a also changed how the following works:

```yml
---
url: /404.html
---
```

Before the commit, `/404.html` was outputted. After it, `/404/index.html` is outputted. Was that an intended change?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: No, it's not intended. Let me see.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: This didn’t work even before the commit, but I think an input file `404.html.md` should output `404.html` and not `404/index.html` as it currently does.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: I think it's fixed now. And subextensions like `404.html.md` are supported too (in fact this was implemented some time ago, but stopped working at some point).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thank you! It seems working now, so closing. :)
Comments.Author: valtlai

873972257.Title: Relative URLs to root of project
873972257.Author: fserb
873972257.PlainText:
Relative URLs to root of project

I have a site (same data) that will be served from multiple locations. Therefore, it's not great to use absolute URLs there.
The problem is: I'm trying to generate a page data that points to the root, so I can, for example have a link pointing to: {{ root }}/assets/x.png that correctly resolves for all internal pages, no matter how deep their URLs is.
I was able to do something like this with:
that mostly solves my problem but:
I didn't see any documentation on this.ctx and I'm worried this will break in the future. Should we make site.data() callbacks receives a context?
I know there's site.process plugins, but it would be great if there was a plugin mechanism to transform the data before rendering, so something like this could also be implemented.

Comments.Body: `this.ctx` does not exist in Lume. Where did you see it? Lume has a `url` filter to generate urls including the domain or only the path. For example, with nunjucks, you could do: ```njk ```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
`url` doesn't work for this case, because it assumes that it's being served on the root of the domain.

`this.ctx` comes from Nunjucks filters.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body: `url` use the `location` configuration as base. If the location is, for example, `https://mysite.com/subdirectory`, the prefix `/subdirectory` is prepended to the returned path. For example: `` returns ``. Does it work for you?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Not really, I'd still have to do one build for each location. Maybe a `relative_path` or `relative_url` filter?

But mostly, how do you feel about a `preprocess` plugin entry that happens before rendering?
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Ok, got it.
Processors are executed after rendering. I think it's good because doing so, they are independent of the template engine used.

There is a `inline` processor [here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/inline.js) to inline automatically all css, javascript, images, etc, by searching in the html code all elements with the `inline` attribute.

I think we can do something similar for your use case, a processor to search all elements using an absolute path in the href/src attributes and convert them to relative. In fact, I have done something like that in [another ssg I've created some years ago](https://github.com/oscarotero/zume/blob/master/src/plugins/urls.js)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ahnnn. That could totally work. Good point.

I made a [quick implementation of preprocess](https://github.com/fserb/lume/tree/preprocess). But I'd defer to you if you think it's a good idea for this to exist at all.
Comments.Author: fserb
fserb

Comments.Body:
I've created a plugin to convert all urls to relative: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/relative_urls.js

You only have to import to your `_config.js` file:

```js
import relative from "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/lumeland/lume/master/plugins/relative_urls.js";

site.use(relative())
```

I have tested it and seems to work fine. Could you check it?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: It works perfectly. Thanks! :)
Comments.Author: fserb

873972732.Title: site.options.dev not set on _config.js when using --dev
873972732.Author: fserb
873972732.Body:
Is supporting `--dev` specific config a thing?

It seems that `site.options.dev` is always false inside `_config.js` and it's only set afterwards.
873972732.PlainText:
site.options.dev not set on _config.js when using --dev

Is supporting --dev specific config a thing?
It seems that site.options.dev is always false inside _config.js and it's only set afterwards.

Comments.Body: `dev` mode is used to render pages in `draft`. And you can set it in `_config.js` by `const site = lume({ dev: true })` or override with the `--dev` option in CLI.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yeah, I get that. But wouldn't it make some sense for it to be set on the `lume()` call, so it can be used inside _config?
For the a case like this:
```
const site = lume();

site.options.location = new URL(site.options.dev ? "http://localhost" : "https://mysite.com");
```

that can be controlled by the command line flag.

I'm not 100% sure if that's a reasonable use case.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
For this specific use case, you can set a different location from CLI (`--location=url`). But I'm agree that dev configuration could be useful in `_config.js`.

Maybe a environment variable, like `DENO_ENV` (like `NODE_ENV`) or `LUME_DEV` (specific for lume) could help.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @fserb FYI, this is fixed in [0.22.2](../blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#0222---2021-06-07) (released 2021-06-07).
Comments.Author: valtlai

874000711.Title: update matches different ignore file than build
874000711.Author: fserb
874000711.Body:
if you have a `example.njk` file and a `site.ignore('example')`.

`lume build` will build `example.njk` correctly.

But if you run `lume build --serve`, it will detect the change, but not rebuild the file, because it will match the ignored list.
874000711.PlainText:
update matches different ignore file than build

if you have a example.njk file and a site.ignore('example').
lume build will build example.njk correctly.
But if you run lume build --serve, it will detect the change, but not rebuild the file, because it will match the ignored list.

Comments.Body:
I guess you want to ignore a folder named `example`, right?

Ok, that's a bug because it only check if the path starts with this pattern [here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/source.js#L91)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Exactly. 

Yeah, I saw `isIgnored()`, but I couldn't find where in the code is the ignore filter for `build`, so they could be the same.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
This function is only needed for update. In `build` the files/folders are filtered [here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/source.js#L149)

I just made a change and now it should work fine. Can you confirm it, please?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Yep. It does work now. :) Thanks.
Comments.Author: fserb

874002494.Title: Tags don't propagate on layouts
874002494.Author: fserb
874002494.Body:
I'm not sure if this is a feature request or a bug.

if you have:

_include/mylayout.njk
```
---
tags: mytag
---
```

page.njk
```
---
layout: mylayout.njk
---
```

Then I'd expect that `page.njk` would be part of `mytag`. Other data seems to propagate properly, but tags seem to be ignored. When used like this:

```
---
renderOrder: 1
---
{%- for post in search.pages('mytag') -%}
```
874002494.PlainText:
Tags don't propagate on layouts

I'm not sure if this is a feature request or a bug.
if you have:
_include/mylayout.njk
page.njk
Then I'd expect that page.njk would be part of mytag. Other data seems to propagate properly, but tags seem to be ignored. When used like this:

Comments.Body: we seem to only save `content` from `#renderPage`. I think it would make sense to update `data` too?
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Data in layouts doesn't affect to the pages, it's used only for rendering. You can use `_data.*` files to apply the same tag to all files in a directory.
Why do you want to do this?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
It's a bit quality of life. It feels it should just work.

My use case is: I have a special type of post, that has a different layout and I want to search all of those. Mostly to avoid having to do:
```
layout: x
tags: x
```
everywhere. In my case, `_data` will probably solve it.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Note that you can search a page not only by tags but any other value. For example:

```njk
{%- for post in search.pages('layout=mylayout.njk') -%}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That totally solves it. :)

"The purpose of layouts is rendering pages, not assign data. You have _data.* files and folders for that purpose."

That's fair, I guess.
Comments.Author: fserb

874038961.Title: data.content not available if document contains an async filter
874038961.Author: fserb
874038961.Body:
As the title says.

If you have an async filter `site.filter('x', async () => {}, true)` and try, for example, to build an RSS feed with the content of the document, the whole `page.data.content` is empty, even if `renderOrder` is used.

data.content not available if document contains an async filter

As the title says.
If you have an async filter site.filter('x', async () => {}, true) and try, for example, to build an RSS feed with the content of the document, the whole page.data.content is empty, even if renderOrder is used.

Not sure to understand.
The rendered content of the page is saved in `page.content`. `page.data.content` stores the initial content (before render).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: ahn, wrong issue. Let me open a proper one.

874084060.Title: njk filter returns empty if there's another async filter
874084060.Author: fserb
874084060.Body:
here's the case.
If you have a config: `site.filter('x', async () => "hello", true);`
and a page A that uses `{{ x }}`.

If you need to render content from page A into page B: `{{ A.page.content }}`  (pre rendered content) will work. But `{{ A.page.content | njk }}` will return empty.

This is useful to render content of RSS feeds, where you don't want `A.content` (the full rendered page) but still want to resolve the templates.

I think the problem is that the njk plugin/filter should either assume it's async by default or have an option to become so.
874084060.PlainText:
njk filter returns empty if there's another async filter

here's the case.
If you have a config: site.filter('x', async () => "hello", true);
and a page A that uses {{ x }}.
If you need to render content from page A into page B: {{ A.page.content }}  (pre rendered content) will work. But {{ A.page.content | njk }} will return empty.
This is useful to render content of RSS feeds, where you don't want A.content (the full rendered page) but still want to resolve the templates.
I think the problem is that the njk plugin/filter should either assume it's async by default or have an option to become so.

Comments.Body: I've made some changes and now `njk` filter is async. Can you test it now?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Yep! It works now! :)
Comments.Author: fserb

925459315.Title: Performance observability
925459315.Author: shah
925459315.Body:
As you start your TypeScript journey (or even before!) I suggest adding some _observability_ across all the major pipeline steps. For example, if I have 100 pages being generated but 5 of them take 95% of time to generate, we should be able to see that when the pages are being tracked. For large sites some parts of site take much longer to generate so the developer should be responsible for caching those pages but she'd need to be told which ones they are.

Something like this?

```bash
🔥 Deno prepare (900ms)
🔥 /images /images (copy: 25ms)
🔥 /index.html /index.tmpl.ts (load: 1025ms, render: 255ms, process: 190ms, etc: ??)
🔥 /styles.css /styles.css (??)

🍾 Site built into ./_site (1255ms)
```

It would be good to build [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) and [OpenMetrics](https://openmetrics.io/) (Prometheus) into Lume so that large sites can fine tune performance through observability.
925459315.PlainText:
Performance observability

As you start your TypeScript journey (or even before!) I suggest adding some observability across all the major pipeline steps. For example, if I have 100 pages being generated but 5 of them take 95% of time to generate, we should be able to see that when the pages are being tracked. For large sites some parts of site take much longer to generate so the developer should be responsible for caching those pages but she'd need to be told which ones they are.
Something like this?
It would be good to build OpenTelemetry and OpenMetrics (Prometheus) into Lume so that large sites can fine tune performance through observability.

Comments.Body: That's good idea. Deno has support for the performance web API, so it should be something easy to do.

And about the `--verbose` option, it's a good idea. I'll add it. 
Thanks!

}
```

But this doesn't work:

import lume from "./my-lume-fork/mod.js";

By emitting in both JSON and OpenMetrics/PEF we'll be able to use proper metrics ingestion systems for alerting. When you're ready to implement, [this is a good Deno library](https://github.com/base698/promts) as is [this one](https://github.com/marcopacini/ts_prometheus) to use. If we need ability to track Deno metrics we can also check out [this library](https://deno.land/x/observability).
Comments.Author: shah
oscarotero

I love the idea of _exporting_ the observability to different formats/file with events in `config.ts` or plugins.

926569342.Title: lume server doesn't reset computed extraData
926569342.Author: fserb
926569342.Body:
I have a plugin that does a Table of Contents based on Headings (h1, h2, h3... instead of tags). For this, I process() each page and extract the headings. All is good and it works fine.

The problem comes when I run lume in server/watch mode.
In that case, there doesn't seem to be way to "reset" the TOC and not double it at every reload, since I need to keep it global to all pages.

If I try as a global list, it obviously doesn't get cleared.
But if I try as part of site.extraData, it also doesn't get cleared.

I think it might be overkill to have a `site.rebuild(callback)`, but it feels like this should have a proper solution? I tried using events, but `beforeBuild` is not called all the time and `beforeUpdate` only reload some files. My current solution is to find a way to update the TOC instead of adding to it.


(related, but not really, it would be really nice to have a `--watch` option that does only watch/rebuild and no HTTP server)

And about the `--watch` option, why do you need this? Do you want to use a different HTTP server or you don't need a server at all?

927742251.Title: JavaScript source maps contain incorrect URL
927742251.Author: valtlai
927742251.Body:
Source maps generated by the Terser plugin have an incorrect URL `0` in the `sources` array.

A test case:

* `_config.js`:

  ```js
  import lume from "lume/mod.js";
  import terser from "lume/plugins/terser.js";
  
  const site = lume()
    .use(terser({ sourceMap: true }));
  
  export default site;
  ```

* `index.njk`:

  ```html
  <script src="/foo.js"></script>
  ```

* `foo.js`:

  ```js
  console.log("Test!");
  ```

That results the following source map:

* `foo.js.map`:

  ```json
  {"version":3,"sources":["0"],"names":["console","log"],"mappings":"AAAAA,QAAQC,IAAI","file":"/foo.js"}
  ```

  Should have `foo.js` instead of `0`.

----

**Edit**: The issue originally covered the PostCSS plugin too, but I think its source maps are correct after all.

JavaScript source maps contain incorrect URL

Source maps generated by the Terser plugin have an incorrect URL 0 in the sources array.
A test case:
_config.js:
index.njk:
foo.js:
That results the following source map:
foo.js.map:
Should have foo.js instead of 0.
Edit: The issue originally covered the PostCSS plugin too, but I think its source maps are correct after all.

928801231.Title: Understanding most errors is hard because of printError()
928801231.Author: fserb
928801231.Body:
I think Lume is used by people who are somewhat familiar with Deno (at least you need to be familiar enough to create a config file and most documentation assumes one can code a bit of Javascript).

That said, I think hiding the exception stack by default is a bad idea.

When I have a problem in my config, most of the time there's no good info on `printDataError`, and I end up getting something like:

```
Error: Cannot read property 'toLowerCase' of undefined

Task completed in 0m1.283s
```

This is unexpected and not really useful since most of the time the error message (even if it had the accompanying src page file that caused it) will still not uniquely identify where the error is coming from. 

I think it's a fair effort to try to associate a particular error to a source page file (that `printDataError` does) and I think Lume should definitely keep doing this and I'll try to track why I'm not getting it on those cases: I'm not sure if it's data files or preprocessors, or a Markdown/Nunjuck plugin. This reinforces my point! I got an error and I have no clue where the error is even coming from. Did I mess up my config? Is the plugin I'm using broken? Did I forgot a field somewhere?

I know I'm able to set `{verbose: 2}`, but my point is a bit deeper than that. Deno errors are not made to be presented without the stack. They make almost as much sense as just writing "Error" and nothing else.

I think we should remove the `debug` option on `printError()` and always output the full exception. It's usually not that verbose (2 to 10 lines); even non "core" developers can understand what it is (and most people using Lume will be familiar with them); it makes the error messages meaningful; and it makes it easier for users to create issues when it comes to that.

The way it currently is, we are almost saying _"there's an error, I'll sometimes tell you what is the offending file, but in any case change something until it works"_.

This reinforces my point! I got an error and I have no clue where the error is even coming from. Did I mess up my config? Is the plugin I'm using broken? Did I forgot a field somewhere?
I know I'm able to set {verbose: 2}, but my point is a bit deeper than that. Deno errors are not made to be presented without the stack. They make almost as much sense as just writing "Error" and nothing else.
I think we should remove the debug option on printError() and always output the full exception. It's usually not that verbose (2 to 10 lines); even non "core" developers can understand what it is (and most people using Lume will be familiar with them); it makes the error messages meaningful; and it makes it easier for users to create issues when it comes to that.
The way it currently is, we are almost saying "there's an error, I'll sometimes tell you what is the offending file, but in any case change something until it works".

930715227.Title: Is _data.* read multiple times or is it a singleton?
930715227.Author: shah
930715227.Body:
I am using `_data.ts` to setup my common data across pages. One of my variables is _sourced_ from a PostgreSQL database so in `_data.ts` I connect to the database, read the content, and store in a variable that I then use across multiple pages. Everything is working well with respect to generating HTML.

What I noticed was that `_data.ts` seems to be sourced for each page (and is running my database connection for each page). Since the data is common to all pages, I assumed `_data.ts` would only be loaded once and then the variables are made available to all page templates but that does not seem to be the case.

So I'm wondering is `_data.*` _meant_ to sourced once for _each page_ or once _per build_? For now I've solved my problem by creating a singleton in `_data.ts` but wanted to report this issue to see what you thought.
930715227.PlainText:
Is _data.* read multiple times or is it a singleton?

I am using _data.ts to setup my common data across pages. One of my variables is sourced from a PostgreSQL database so in _data.ts I connect to the database, read the content, and store in a variable that I then use across multiple pages. Everything is working well with respect to generating HTML.
What I noticed was that _data.ts seems to be sourced for each page (and is running my database connection for each page). Since the data is common to all pages, I assumed _data.ts would only be loaded once and then the variables are made available to all page templates but that does not seem to be the case.
So I'm wondering is _data.* meant to sourced once for each page or once per build? For now I've solved my problem by creating a singleton in _data.ts but wanted to report this issue to see what you thought.

Comments.Body:
`_data` files/folders are loaded only once.

You can see here a real example: https://github.com/tarugoconf/tarugo21/blob/master/_data/eventbrite.js#L12
This file get data from the eventbrite API and export it with the name `metrics`.

Maybe you are exporting a function that is executed for each page instead the result?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ok great to know that, _by design_, `_data.ts` should only be loaded once. I'll try to figure out what I might have done to load it multiple times because for me (to get typesafety) I have to manually import `_data.ts` in my pages so it's possible I caused my own grief :-).
Comments.Author: shah

939287230.Title: Using `site.dest()` to set metrics destination throws exception
939287230.Author: shah
939287230.Body:
@oscarotero per our conversation on Discord, I'm trying to set the destination of the metrics file to the site's output directory in my `_config.ts` using your suggestion:

`site.options.metrics = site.dest(".observability-metrics.json");` 

Doing a build with that line in `_config.ts` results in:

```javascript
in _config.ts shows this error:
error: Uncaught (in promise) NotFound: No such file or directory (os error 2)
  async save(file: string) {
                         ^
    at deno:core/core.js:86:46
    at unwrapOpResult (deno:core/core.js:106:13)
    at async open (deno:runtime/js/40_files.js:46:17)
    at async writeFile (deno:runtime/js/40_write_file.js:58:18)
    at async Metrics.save (https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.25.1/metrics.ts:80:26)
    at async printMetrics (https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.25.1/cli/build.ts:50:33)
```

When I used the following, it works fine:

`site = lume({metrics: "_site/.observability-metrics.json"})`

It looks like a timing issue when using `site.dest(...)` (perhaps because dest isn't created?). I like my metrics to go to the destination folder because it's useful for CI/CD and my site has a `/health` directory.
939287230.PlainText:
Using site.dest() to set metrics destination throws exception

@oscarotero per our conversation on Discord, I'm trying to set the destination of the metrics file to the site's output directory in my _config.ts using your suggestion:
site.options.metrics = site.dest(".observability-metrics.json");
Doing a build with that line in _config.ts results in:
When I used the following, it works fine:
site = lume({metrics: "_site/.observability-metrics.json"})
It looks like a timing issue when using site.dest(...) (perhaps because dest isn't created?). I like my metrics to go to the destination folder because it's useful for CI/CD and my site has a /health directory.

Comments.Body:
The problem is `site.dest()` returns the full path to this file, but `site.options.metrics` expects a path relative to cwd.
The solution is use the `site.options.dest` value:

```js
site.options.metrics = join(site.options.dest, ".observability-metrics.json");
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That worked, thanks!

Another quick request: if `--quiet` is requested, `⏲ Metrics data saved in ...` message should probably be suppressed.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: Ok, done in `master`. I'll release `v0.25.2` in a couple with this changes and other possible bugfixes.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

941041082.Title: Improve metrics to be more analytics friendly
941041082.Author: shah
941041082.Body:
@oscarotero and team, I have these suggestions for improving metrics:

1. Increase granularity
2. Allow "baggage" (in open telemetry terms) to be contributed by pages

**Increasing granularity**. Right now we have the following in `metrics.json` where the "name" has event details that need to parsed in order to be consumable for analytics:

```json
{
    "name": "Load: /support/generate-static-build.sh",
    "entryType": "measure",
    "startTime": 1009.5581999999999,
    "duration": 0.1815000000000282,
    "detail": null
  }
```

Instead, I suggest something like this:

```
{
    "name": "Load: /home", // human-friendly, not useful for analytics
    "nature": { category: "load" }, // analytics-friendly, nature can be like a URL with depth /load/X, /preprocess, /render/Z, etc. and can be merged with metrics contributed by pages being rendered
    "target": { url: "/page/url", ...anything else we know about target... }, // analytics-friendly
    "entryType": "measure", // "measure" is the only entry I see here -- is there anything else?
    "startTime": 1007.8624, // analytics-friendly, 
    "duration": 2187.5929, // suggest removing "duration" and only including analytics-friendly "endTime"
    "detail": null // suggest renaming to "baggage" using OpenTelemetry terminology; and allow this to be contributed/merged from generated pages (through filter.metrics({ nature: {  }, baggage: {... anything ...}})
}
```

**Allow metrics "contribution" by pages**. Many of my pages run database calls or other expensive calls and I'd like to add my own metrics for those expensive items.

```ts
export default async function ( // for Lume
  page: lumeAide.RenderablePage,
  filters: lumeAide.Filters,
) {
  // do something expensive
  filter.metrics({ nature: {  }, baggage: {... anything ...}}); // I can call my own metrics?
}
```
941041082.PlainText:
Improve metrics to be more analytics friendly

@oscarotero and team, I have these suggestions for improving metrics:
Increase granularity
Allow "baggage" (in open telemetry terms) to be contributed by pages
Increasing granularity. Right now we have the following in metrics.json where the "name" has event details that need to parsed in order to be consumable for analytics:
Instead, I suggest something like this:
Allow metrics "contribution" by pages. Many of my pages run database calls or other expensive calls and I'd like to add my own metrics for those expensive items.

Comments.Body:
These metrics are generated with the standard [`Performance` web interface](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance), that allows to [create marks](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance/mark) and measure the time between these marks (or one mark to now) with [performance.measure](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance/measure). I think it's better to use an already implemented and available interface instead of reiventing the wheel and create new system.

The performance object is already available in Deno as a global object, so you can use it at any point of your javascript/typescript code. `site.metrics` is just a wrapper of the `performance` object to be used internally by lume: See for example: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/metrics.ts#L33

So, if you want to add additional metrics from your typescript pages, just need to do something like this:

```ts
export default async function (data, filters) {
    performance.mark("SQL query"); //Create a timing mark named "SQL query"
    const result = await makeExpensiveQuery();
    performance.measure("SQL query"); //Measure the timing from the mark "SQL query" to now.
}
```

Now, the measure of "SQL query" will be included in the list of your metrics. Note that lume only export the entries of type "measure" (not "mark") because marks are used only as the starting point of a measure. [See this](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/metrics.ts#L57)

Maybe we could include additional info in the `details` property but, due marks cannot have duplicated names, we have to contain all info in the entry name to avoid duplications.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Perfect @oscarotero - I _completely_ agree we should stay with Deno's strategy of being as close to web interface as possible. With this new information you've armed me with about metrics I'll do some studying and implement what I need on top. I'm trying to get the metrics to be useful on the server side through Prometheus and OpenTelemetry taxonomy and I'll let you know if I get stuck anywhere. Thanks!
Comments.Author: shah
shah

Comments.Body: @oscarotero if you'd rather put the extra information in `detail` that's fine too -- my only goal is to have is parseable without doing any string manipulation. Thanks!
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: I don't think the name is a good place to set info. This is the job of `detail` property.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
shah
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Sure, if this is the best I can get I'll take it :-).

But, it's not as scalable for complex needs because the Lume core is providing the messages and subjects instead of a type-safe instrumentation supplier doing all messages and categorization. With the design I suggested, tossing in OpenTelemetry as the supplier would be trivial. With your (even much improved) design I am still forced to create an adapter in the middle instead of using a proper type-safe interface. In general I agree with you about reducing verbosity but complex sites like mine that have hundreds of thousands of pages with over a dozen developers need different flexibility in different page generators.

I appreciate you taking the input and improving it though, thanks! I'll use what you've created when the next release comes out and give you more feedback.
Comments.Author: shah
shah

Comments.Body:
I think the solution is very similar to your proposal. The only difference is that instead having one method for every thing to measure, we have just one method. I know that you're concerned about typings (so you can accept only some options for every measure, for example require a `Page` for every `renderPage` measurement), but in practice, is almost the same. In fact, there are other metrics that you have not taken into account, like processors and preprocessors. And if you want to measure database queries, for example, you should have to create new methods.

This is why I believe this way is more scalable. It works like events: there isn't an event class for every event type, there's just one event with the property `type`. This allows to have only two methods: `addEventListener` and `dispatchEvent`, instead of `addBeforeBuildEvent`, `dispatchBeforeBuildEvent`, `addAfterBuildEvent`, `dispatchBeforeBuildEvent`, and so on.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero I know it _looks_ similar to my proposal but it's missing the main purpose: flexibility for analytics through multiple implementations. In your version, you're making the decision _a priori_ about what to name a subject (which makes it type-unsafe and defeats typing) because you have _concrete classes_ instead of an _interface_. 

By putting in a minor abstraction (`Instrumentable`) you get unlimited flexibility with your primary goal of allowing everything complex to be done outside of the simplicity-focused core. Lume's initial TypeScript implementation is fantastic but there are many places there are concrete classes where interfaces should be (like Metrics). This may be the first place where you might take the TypeScript-centric approach to creating small interfaces and default _simple_ implementations in Lume core with non-core implementations can do all the complex work on their own. Similar to your plugins concept.

This is mainly an architecture choice difference -- your style is good, solid, JavaScript concrete classes approach ... my suggestion is to use TypeScript-focused style with interfaces and multiple implementations. If it would be helpful to have a quick voice call to discuss this topic I'm happy to jump on a call. The reason I'm spending time on this discussion is that the architecture of Lume is fantastic but not type-safe yet and it would be great to increase type-safety as you progress to 1.0.

Thanks for hearing me out!
Comments.Author: shah
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Affirmative @oscarotero -- returning a function is not as extensible as returning an interface (like you said, I'm planning on expanding the `observe` object) :-). 

Also, you're right that I would like to create an interface without extending the base class. Requiring _inheritance_ (`class X extends Y`) is generally more brittle than using _composition_ (implementing an interface with property functions). With interface implementations the core never worries about any _base classes_ - only _default implementations_. 

As Lume grows and more developers join the core (like I would love to!) the more base classes you have, the more brittle the system will be because base functionality can break in inherited classes. Adding more interfaces with default implementations in favor of base classes with inheritance is ideal - it would allow us to use Inversion of Control (IoC) in `_config.ts` for all complexity and leave the core super-simple.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: I think this is already implemented, so I'm closing it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

981636268.Title: Issue with dot in file name that is not an extension
981636268.Author: kevinlang
981636268.Body:
If I have a markdown post named `blah.6.md`, it will generate an HTML file as usual, but the out path will be `_site/blah.6`. 

If I change it to `blah-6.md`, it will correctly generate the HTML file at `_site/blah-6/index.html`
981636268.PlainText:
Issue with dot in file name that is not an extension

If I have a markdown post named blah.6.md, it will generate an HTML file as usual, but the out path will be _site/blah.6.
If I change it to blah-6.md, it will correctly generate the HTML file at _site/blah-6/index.html

Comments.Body:
This is the expected behavior. If the filename has a sub-extension, it's used to generate the output filename, instead of html. For example, `styles.css.njk` is a nunjucks file that generates a css file (`styles.css`).

If you want to avoid this, use the `url` variable in the front matter:

```md
---
url: /blah.6/
---
```

If you want to apply this automatically to all files in this directory, create a `_data.js` file with the following code:

```js
export function url(page) {
    return `${page.src.path}/`;
}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1005926553.Title: Provide system to force reload of files
1005926553.Author: amatiasq
1005926553.Body:
Following https://github.com/lumeland/lume/pull/134, we need a way to force lume to reload a file.

Example use case is if we know `a.ts` depends on `b.ts` whenever we receive a `beforeUpdate` event because `b.ts` has changed we want Lume to also re-evaluate `a.ts`.

We can add `a.ts` to `event.files` but that won't trigger a re-evaluation because of `Source` internal cache.
1005926553.PlainText:
Provide system to force reload of files

Following , we need a way to force lume to reload a file.
Example use case is if we know a.ts depends on b.ts whenever we receive a beforeUpdate event because b.ts has changed we want Lume to also re-evaluate a.ts.
We can add a.ts to event.files but that won't trigger a re-evaluation because of Source internal cache.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Oh i see, you're proposing to split the Readding From Disk from execution
of the files.



We can use url parsing to evaluate the module's code but without the
sourcemaps we won't have the list of dependencies of the files. We could
use that rust library you mentioned before.



I was also considering we could check if the loaded file is outside 'src'
(but inside 'cwd()'?) and watch them too.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
> Oh i see, you're proposing to split the Readding From Disk from execution
of the files.

Yes, exactly. The same dependency can be used several times, but we only read it once.

> We can use url parsing to evaluate the module's code but without the
sourcemaps we won't have the list of dependencies of the files. We could
use that rust library you mentioned before.

I think it's better because the rust library only return the graph of dependencies (is the equivalent to run the comand `deno info a.ts`), but don't execute anything, so it's faster that bundle, create a source map, parse it and inspect.

> I was also considering we could check if the loaded file is outside 'src'
(but inside 'cwd()'?) and watch them too.

This can't be done. The watcher only see changes in the src directory. The idea is that all src files needed to build the site are in this directory. The cwd can change (you can build the same site from different cwd). Any external file (outside of this directory or a remote dependency) won't be watched.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I am just jumping into the middle of this and don't know anything, but I'm curious if doing the source maps helps with debugging? Ideally, it would be nice when having trouble to be able to step through code even in templates. I know that nunjucks is supposed to be adding source maps support at some point.
Comments.Author: ejsmith

Comments.Body:
SourceMaps were not used as such, we were using them just as a medium to
get the module's dependencies.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
@amatiasq I just [created a branch](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/tree/improved-watcher) implementing a new way to watch files, compatible with javascript modules:

- The build and watch are executed in a Worker [code here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/improved-watcher/cli/build.ts#L65).
- If at least one of the changed file is javascript or typescript, the worker is terminated, and a new one is created, so the entire site is built again with fresh content. 

This system has two drawbacks:
- On change a js/ts file, the entire site is rebuilt, so it may be slower than just update the changed file.
- There's no support for import maps in Workers [see issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/6675) so the `_config.ts` file cannot use bare imports.

I've added this mode under the `--rebuild` flag, so both modes are available. `lume --serve` will work as now, and `lume --serve --rebuild` enables this watch mode.

what do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero running the code in a Worker is a great improvement regarding modules isolation!

I'll need time to carefully read and understand the new code so I can understand why those limitations apply, sadly I doubt I can get half as much time as last week.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
Don't worry, no rush :)
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: The new version 1.1.1 was released. It includes the new experimental watcher (enabled with `lume --serve --experimental` or `lume --watch --experimental`. Once Deno fixes some issues, it could replace in the future the default watcher.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1015863698.Title: cli.utils.runWatch timeout is too long?
1015863698.Author: fserb
1015863698.Body:
Right now, there's a 500ms debounce timer on `runWatch()` that makes the rebuild happen 500ms after the last event has been seen.

I understand that the intention is to try to wait a bit before doing an unnecessary rebuild if multiple files are being saved. But there are two issues:

1. 500ms is a small eternity for fast save-rebuild-reload feedback (and there's also the actual build time).
2. `FSWatcher ASyncIterator` will do the right thing if multiple files are being detected at the same time. I.e., if we do *any* `setTimeout` it will happen **after** all the FSWatchers are done. The FSWatcher async iterator will always happen before (if it actually detects a file change).

I've tested this on my machine by editing multiple files and saving them all (with "save all" on my editor).
With a `setTimeout(callback, 0)` I still get a single reload that detects all the changes before (the 2 events for each file, etc).

No matter what the debounce time is, it probably won't be fast enough for multiple manual saves, so we are only really ready for automatic saves (i.e., save all). I understand that theoretically there may be machines that take XXms to save files, but that's probably not common (or at least not very relevant for save-rebuild?).

That said, I'd suggest moving this to 0, but I'd be happy with either a much much smaller number (less than 10ms?) or with a way to set this with a config. WDYT?
1015863698.PlainText:
cli.utils.runWatch timeout is too long?

Right now, there's a 500ms debounce timer on runWatch() that makes the rebuild happen 500ms after the last event has been seen.
I understand that the intention is to try to wait a bit before doing an unnecessary rebuild if multiple files are being saved. But there are two issues:
500ms is a small eternity for fast save-rebuild-reload feedback (and there's also the actual build time).
FSWatcher ASyncIterator will do the right thing if multiple files are being detected at the same time. I.e., if we do any setTimeout it will happen after all the FSWatchers are done. The FSWatcher async iterator will always happen before (if it actually detects a file change).
I've tested this on my machine by editing multiple files and saving them all (with "save all" on my editor).
With a setTimeout(callback, 0) I still get a single reload that detects all the changes before (the 2 events for each file, etc).
No matter what the debounce time is, it probably won't be fast enough for multiple manual saves, so we are only really ready for automatic saves (i.e., save all). I understand that theoretically there may be machines that take XXms to save files, but that's probably not common (or at least not very relevant for save-rebuild?).
That said, I'd suggest moving this to 0, but I'd be happy with either a much much smaller number (less than 10ms?) or with a way to set this with a config. WDYT?

Comments.Body:
Hi, @fserb 
Yes, perhaps 500ms is too much. Maybe the native `Deno.watchFS` works better now, but previously in my computer, it returned multiple iterations with different files or the same file multiple times. So after make a change (or even without making a change but just opening a file), Lume built the site twice or more times. Perhaps it depends on the OS (in my case I have a iMac with SSD).

Anyway, the debounce can be much more smaller (100 or 200ms is enough, I guess) because we are using two watchers: one for the src folder to build the site and other for the dest folder to reload the browser so, in the worst case, the time to wait to see a change in the browser after saving is the double (for 500ms of debounce, you should wait 1000ms).

I'll change that (or if you want to work on a PR, feel free). But I won't release a new version this week because I'll be out the next 4 days.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: New version released, including this change. The debounce waits only 100ms instead 500ms.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Linux uplink 5.10.72-1-lts #1 SMP Sat, 09 Oct 2021 17:34:39 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ deno --version 
deno 1.15.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.5.172.19
typescript 4.4.2
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ lume --version 
v1.1.1
```

Is this something that could be solve changing something in Lume, or is it like that because of Deno? I would really like that warning to disappear, even though it doesn't do any harm 😆
1028140307.PlainText:
Lume (local) server always warns "Unable to detect your local IP address"

Every time I start the (local) server I'm getting this warning: Unable to detect your local IP address.
I'm running Arch Linux, Deno 1.15.1, and Lume v1.1.1:
Is this something that could be solve changing something in Lume, or is it like that because of Deno? I would really like that warning to disappear, even though it doesn't do any harm 😆

Comments.Body: This is because Lume uses [deno-local-ip](https://github.com/joakimunge/deno-local-ip) to detect the local-ip of the local server and this package depends on `net-tools` installed in your computer. I'm not familiarized with Arch Linux, so don't know if you can install it. Perhaps `pacman -S net-tools`??
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Indeed, it's looking for `ifconfig` on non-Windows systems...and yes, I don't have it 😏

Feel free to close this one, since there is nothing you can do, if the IP is certainly needed.
Comments.Author: x80486

1028141093.Title: Make quiet even more quiet
1028141093.Author: x80486
1028141093.Body:
I'm running Arch Linux, Deno `1.15.1`, and Lume `v1.1.1`:

```shell
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ uname -a 
Linux uplink 5.10.72-1-lts #1 SMP Sat, 09 Oct 2021 17:34:39 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ deno --version 
deno 1.15.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.5.172.19
typescript 4.4.2
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ lume --version 
v1.1.1
```

I'm building a sample project with `lume --quiet`, but still, there is a lot of output in the console:

```shell
[x80486@uplink:~/lume-template]$ lume --quiet 
Download https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.1.1/cli.ts
Download https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.1.1/cli/build.ts
Download https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.1.1/cli/init.ts

1028141093.PlainText:
Make quiet even more quiet

I'm running Arch Linux, Deno 1.15.1, and Lume v1.1.1:
I'm building a sample project with lume --quiet, but still, there is a lot of output in the console:
I'm assuming that with --quiet Lume will only show warnings and/or errors, which is what I'm expecting with this. So is it possible to make --quiet to remove those Download ... statements as well?

Comments.Body: This is from Deno and is shown only the first time Lume is executed. Then, these modules are cached by Deno so these messages won't be shown anymore.

Comments.Body: This one is more towards CI/CD workflows, where all that output is not really needed...but if they come Deno, it's going to be tricky to "mute" them. Not sure if Deno has anything for that 🤔
