Comments.Author: oscarotero

1034288334.Title: Generate robots.txt automatically
1034288334.Author: x80486
1034288334.Body: I think it would be handy to have an option to generate a customized `robots.txt` in the same way as any other template, [in the same way Hugo does](https://gohugo.io/templates/robots/).
1034288334.PlainText:
Generate robots.txt automatically

I think it would be handy to have an option to generate a customized robots.txt in the same way as any other template, in the same way Hugo does.

Comments.Body:
You can do that in two ways:

- Create a page file adding a subextension. For example `robots.txt.njk` will output the file `robots.txt`.
- Or create a page file and set the `url` variable to `/robots.txt`.

You have more info here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/discussions/113
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1034590955.Title: Built-in assets compression
1034590955.Author: x80486
1034590955.Body: It would be great if `Lume` supports this "natively". I've read that we should be able to accomplish something similar by using _processors_, but I think HTML and CSS compression/minification could be offered out-of-the box, since it's far too common, and (I would assume) a high percentage of developers would take advantage of this pretty much by default.
1034590955.PlainText:
Built-in assets compression

It would be great if Lume supports this "natively". I've read that we should be able to accomplish something similar by using processors, but I think HTML and CSS compression/minification could be offered out-of-the box, since it's far too common, and (I would assume) a high percentage of developers would take advantage of this pretty much by default.

Comments.Body:
For CSS, it could be done with a postCSS plugin, maybe [cssnano](https://cssnano.co/). It could be enabled by default, like nesting and autoprefixer plugins.
For HTML, I have to investigate if there's any library for Deno to minify HTML. Any suggestion?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I think `cssnano` is [one of the best options](https://goalsmashers.github.io/css-minification-benchmark/) for this matter out there. On the other hand, trying to search for the HTML counterpart didn't yield anything super-useful, except for [this one](https://github.com/sno2/minifier/), but it looks very much abandoned at this time, even though the latest published version could be used to start with something, if desired.
Comments.Author: x80486
oscarotero

Comments.Body: That actually did a pretty decent job on the HTML side...but I don't see it's doing anything (in my setup) to the CSS files 😐
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body: To compress css files, you need to load them first (with `site.loadAssets([".css"])`) and then, configure the `minify` plugin to accept `.css` files (see my previous example).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: The new incoming Lume version (1.5.0) will include the `parcel_css` plugin, to use [Parcel CSS](https://parceljs.org/blog/parcel-css/) minifier.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1047840309.Title: Triggering update from beforeUpdate
1047840309.Author: fserb
1047840309.PlainText:
Triggering update from beforeUpdate

I have some js that I bundle with esbuild. Writing a plugin for it, I ended up with something like:
I make sure that all the dependencies are in an ignored dir, and everything works fine.
Now I want to update the main bundle when one of the deps change. I did something like:
This "works", except that this triggers the whole update cycle twice (including all afterUpdate events). If instead of doing this, I simply add the files to ev.files, it doesn't work because the beforeUpdate of core/source doesn't get called on the new files.
So now I'm stuck: there's no way to restart or cancel the current update, and no way to ask source to clean the cache just for this file. What would be a good idea here?

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: BTW, is there any specific reason to use a custom bundler? Have you try the bundler plugin?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yeah. The Deno blunder is not even close to good enough for this (to be fair, it's not designed for this). I need inline source map, minification (and keeping top names, because of WebComponents), tree-shaking, backward compatibility js version support, etc. 

I'll try Asset/process version, I think I tried it before and failed, but I'll report back.
Comments.Author: fserb
fserb

Comments.Body: Well, technically now the bundles are re-running every time anything changes, it seems.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Yes, this is the expected behavior. 
The only difference between `build` and `update` is that update only reload the changed files and save the output files if they changed the content (using a hash [as you can see here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/emitter.ts#L31)) but the process in the middle (preprocessor, render and processor) are executed to all pages.
This is because you never know how a change in a file can affect to other files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hi again, @fserb 
I just found your repo (https://github.com/fserb/bkc) and created a `esbuild` plugin for Lume using your code. You can take a look here. https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Oh cool. :)

Yeah, there are a few issues with the current plugin that we may want to solve:

1. as mentioned before, it would probably be better if we rebuild only if some set of extensions changed, as opposed to any file.
2. it may be useful to consider two use cases:
   a. when we want to esbuild every js/ts files in the tree and we have a clear way to define dependencies (like `node_modules` or something).
   b. when we have a well defined set of js/ts files as entry points and everything else is a dependency. 

What I currently do for b is: I have a `jslib` directory that I `site.ignore()` and leave all dependencies there.
It's kinda okey, but I wonder if there could be a way inside the plugin to handle those cases (like passing lists of matching globs either for entry points or dependencies).

I'm not sure if the current way Lume handles assets processing supports that. Do you think this is a reasonable request? If so, how could we do it?

I could try to do it over the weekend, but I'm not sure even if there's a way to solve either of those things at this point.

nit: we also probably want to remove `incremental: true` from the user options. But I think the overall way to deal with the args (and overwritting the build specific ones) was a great way to deal with it.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Ok, I understand.

The reason to rebuild the entire site after any change, instead of only some pages, is because it's very complex to know in advance the pages that will be affected by any file change. For example:

- A change in a css file can affects to all files that imports this file.
- The affected files can be `.css` files, but also other extensions that generate css files (for example, a njk file that outputs a css file).
- And if you use the `inline` plugin, that inlines assets like css, js, images, etc in the html documents, the change in the css file can affect to other html pages.

The only way I can think to have this is configuring directly by the user. For example, with an option like `site.updateContext([".js", ".ts"])` (or a better name), indicating that the ".js" and ".ts" extensions are independent of the other pages, meaning that:
- If a ".js" and/or ".ts" file changes, all ".js" and ".ts" files are regenerated but the other pages aren't.
- If any other extensions changes (like "njk" files), all pages of the site will be regenerated except ".js" and ".ts" files.

But this option should be configured manually by the user because, as said, it depends on many things like relations between pages, plugins installed, etc.

---

And about the second issue, there's a similar request here https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/53. But I'd like to avoid globs because it can affects to performance.
Perhaps, this comment https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/53#issuecomment-864572282 is a good solution: allow not only strings, but also functions that returns true/false, so you can do something like this:

```js
site.ignore((filename) => isIgnored(filename));
```

This could be a good solution for complex ignored conditions. Anyway, in your case I think the best solution is just ignore the folder (or rename the folder prefixing it with `_`).

Btw, can I add your site to the pages of examples? (https://lumeland.github.io/getting-started/examples/)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@fserb I just pushed some changes in this direction. The idea is the following:

You can configure scoped extensions that will be treated independently of the rest of the pages. For example:

```ts
site.scopedExtensions([".css"], [".js", ".ts"]);
```

Here we are defining two scopes (one for `.css` files, and other for `.js` and `.ts` files).
This means that if a `.css` file changes, only the `.css` files will be rebuild, but not the other pages. And if a `.js` or `.ts` file changes, only `.js` and `.ts` will be updated.
If any other page changes (ex: a `.njk` file), all the "unscoped" pages will be rebuild, but scoped extensions (`.css`, `.js` and `.ts`) won't.

To manage this, there's a new `Changes` class that you can see here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/changes.ts. This class generates a function that will be used to filter the pages that will be updated.

I not sure if this explanation is clear enough, so let me know your thoughts. I'm also open to change the function name if there's a better name.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
This makes sense to me. And it does address the problem.

I wonder if it would make more sense to make those scopes more generic.  The reason I say this is that the two obvious dependency connection for any file is "my directory or any subdirectory" and "same extension". 

I know you've been trying to avoid globs for perf reasons on `copy`. But maybe in the case of changes is a more contained problem, as we only have to iterate over the changes, not all files?

Overall, I keep wondering if extensions is the right abstraction across the board on Lume, or if things would be cleaner and more powerful if everything was a regexp (and extensions and globs are just subset of Regexps).

Re: adding the file as an example, sure. I haven't made the site officially public yet, but go ahead. :)
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Ok, fair enough.
I've renamed the function to `scopedUpdates` and now it accepts functions instead of extensions. This allows to use glob or any other method to validate a file path:

```js
site.scopedUpdates(
    (path) => path.endsWith(".css"), // A scope with all css files
    (path) => path.startsWith("/js/"), // A scope with all files inside "js" folder
)
```

And about the use of extensions instead of regexp, I have thought several times in a way to allow to define functions, but it's more complicated that it seems. The truth is that extensions are a very flexible and understandable format because it define which files must be loaded and how they will be processed:

- Lume needs to know the extension to build the url of a page. For example:
  - `/about-me.md` is converted to `/about-me/` url, removing the `.md` suffix
  - `/styles.css.njk` is converted to `/styles.css` url, removing the `.njk` suffix
  - `/styles.css.tmpl.js` is converted to `/styles.css` url, removing the `.tmpl.js` suffix
- It also needs to know the extension to know how to handle every file (md, njk, jsx, etc)

A regexp only returns `true` or `false` (well, it can return captured values too), but it's more tricky to work with it to define loaders because we need to know the suffix of the path that we have to remove and sometimes it's not too easy. For example `.tmpl.js` suffix is used to generate pages using javascript templates and uses the module loader, which is different to `.js` that is the format of client side javascript files that are loaded as text files.

In short, we need the extensions, even if we use regexp, glob or any other advanced way to load pages. And having the two things (extensions and glob/functions/etc) would make Lume more complicated and confusing (in addition to possible performance issues). I'm not entirely closed to implement that, but I have to think in the best way to do it so they can coexist harmoniously.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
fserb

Comments.Body:
I love to hear good ideas for Lume, so don't worry :)

Your idea of the `Format` type sounds great, but I'm not sure if it covers all use cases. For example:

- A format has a `loader` assigned, a function responsive to read the file content. You can see [the different loaders here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/tree/master/core/loaders).
- There are two types of files: pages and data. The same format can have different loaders depending on the type. For example:
  - `.js` files can be loaded as text files, because they aren't executed, but bundled, minified, etc.
  - But javascript data files (like `_data.js` or `_data/site.js`) are loaded as javascript modules, because they need to be executed at the build time.
- Formats are used also to detect the template engine used to render the pages. For example:
  - The page `about-me.njk` uses the Nunjucks template engine.
  - A page with the variable `layout: main.pug` uses the Pug template engine to render the layout file in `_includes`.
  - A page with the variable `templateEngine: njk,md` will be rendered twice, first using Nunjucks and then markdown [see this](https://lumeland.github.io/core/multiple-template-engines/)
- Processors and preprocessors use also the extensions to determine if a file must be processed before/after rendering. Both extensions (src and dest) are used. Let's say we register a processor to minify `.css` files:
  - A sass file (`.scss`) that ouputs `.css` is processed, because the extension is present in the dest file.
  - A `.css` file is processed too because the extension is present in the src file.

File extensions can cover all these casuistry with a single api (just prefix the format at the end of the file name) and it works in both directions (you can register anything (loader, processor, etc) for all files of the same extension; and you can assign a extension to a individual file). In addition to that, you don't need to see the code in the `_config.js` file to find out how a file will be loaded and processed, because it's clear in the file extension that, for example, a `.md` file is a markdown file that will be loaded and render as markdown.

Your proposal could work as a low level API to handle this but I see some limitations, for example how to use `my format` (in your example) as a format for layouts stored in `_includes`? or for processors/preprocessors? The format priority to resolve conflicts sounds a bit hacky...

Anyway, I'll keep thinking about this and open for suggestions.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1088450679.Title: Support React?
1088450679.Author: audrow

1088450679.PlainText:
Support React?

Hey, Lume looks great. Is there a good way to use React with Lume? If not, can a plugin be added to use React? Not being able to use React is the main reason that I'm looking at other options for making a static site. I understand if that's not in the design goals of Lume.

Comments.Body:
Hi, @audrow 
React is available but not enabled by default. You have to use the JSX plugin, that uses React under the hood. More info: https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/jsx/

1090841598.Title: Nunjuck's options stopped working
1090841598.Author: fserb
1090841598.PlainText:
Nunjuck's options stopped working

I have a:
With the HEAD version of Lume, the autoescape (which, btw, I think should be the default for Lume, but not the point) doesn't get used anymore.
I'm not sure what the issue here is:
Is this the proper way to pass options to a default plugin? I see that nunjuck ends up being initialized twice (one for the default and one from my site.use().
On the 2nd call, I see that options.options has the proper autoescape value, but that is not doing what it used to do (i.e., disable autoescape). If I manually switch my templates to include {{ content | safe }}, then it works fine... I thought it could be a nunjuck's problem, but I don't see a version bump anywhere.
help?
[]s
F.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ahn, I used to have that.

The reason I changed (and it used to work fine, but I get it it's a bit weird). Is that I have plugins on both Nunjucks and Markdown that I want to pass `site.options.dev`. Is there any way we can support accessing it before site is initialized?
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Nevermind, I just added a:

```js
const dev = Deno.args.indexOf('--dev') != -1;
```

to the beginning of the file. Much simpler. :)
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Yes, I was about to suggest something like that.
Perhaps, there could be something more solid (an environment variable like `LUME_DEV` for example) but this is the only way at this moment.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1091221046.Title: How to replace a default engine?
1091221046.Author: fserb
1091221046.Body:
I have a plugin with:

```js
site.loadPages([".md"], textLoader, new MyEngine());
```

With the new code this breaks, because `Engines.engine` ends up like:

```js
Extensions {
  default: undefined,
  entries: [
    [ ".tmpl.ts", ModuleEngine { helpers: [Object] } ],
    [ ".tmpl.js", ModuleEngine { helpers: [Object] } ],
    [ ".md", MarkdownEngine { engine: [Object] } ],
    [
      ".njk",
      NunjucksEngine { env: [Object], cache: [Map], ... }
    ],
    [ ".md", MyEngine {} ]
  ]
```

and `Engines.engine.search()` returns the first entry. `Extensions` doesn't seem to worry about duplicates. Ideas?
1091221046.PlainText:
How to replace a default engine?

I have a plugin with:
With the new code this breaks, because Engines.engine ends up like:
and Engines.engine.search() returns the first entry. Extensions doesn't seem to worry about duplicates. Ideas?

Comments.Body:
Mmm. The `Extensions` class should replace existing values instead of duplicate them. 
Here https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/extensions.ts#L14 the function should remove the existing value before adding the new one.
Feel free to createa PR or I will fix it (I cannot right now).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I just released a new version with the duplicated extensions bugfix.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1097294791.Title: HTML <template> contents are cleared when using "some" plugins
1097294791.Author: johanbrook
1097294791.Body: ## The bug When using a HTML `
1097294791.PlainText:
HTML  contents are cleared when using "some" plugins

The bug
When using a HTML <template> element in lume, its contents are cleared when building with a special configuration of plugins.
Demo
Project setup
src/index.njk
_config.ts
Test run
Expected
Actual
Culprits
In _config.ts above, comment out the line .use(basePath()) and the build will work as expected. Replacing basePath with the inline plugin will also cause the bug. Not the date plugin, however.
Versions
I thought I was going mad while developing my code, since my <template> elements kept going empty on build, whereas they worked all over the rest of the internet 😄😄😄

Comments.Body: Yes, this is an known bug in deno-dom (https://github.com/b-fuze/deno-dom/issues/18) that wasn't fixed yet and I'm not sure when is going to be fixed, it's going slow :(
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Aha! Alpha status things ✨ Then I know, at least. Thanks!
Comments.Author: johanbrook

Comments.Body: I think this was fixed in the latest `deno-dom` version, which is included in the latest Lume version (1.7.3).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1097682474.Title: `page.data.url` disappears when `page.addData` is called
1097682474.Author: aiotter
1097682474.PlainText:
page.data.url disappears when page.addData is called

Description of the bug
When you set processor or preprocessor to call page.addData, page.data.url will be lost.
Reproduction procedure
Create index.md at the root directory of the project.
Create _config.ts as following:
You'll get the following result:
Commenting out the processor registration, or passing a function with no side effect as a processor will create the desired output:

Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
Hi.
`page.addData()` is a function to use internally by Lume. To add data to a page use `page.data` property as you did in the workaround.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I see!
Do you think it is possible to hide the `Page#addData` method so that it is clear for users that it is not intended to be called?
Like making it private or prepend `_`?
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body: It's public because it's used by an external class [the `Source` class](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/source.ts#L202) but prepending with `_` is a good idea.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Nice work! Thanks 👍
Comments.Author: aiotter

1107950396.Title: Referring to `page.document` breaks non-HTML file
1107950396.Author: aiotter
1107950396.PlainText:
Referring to page.document breaks non-HTML file

This is a quick bug report.
Reproduction procedure
Create _config.ts as:
Create test.css as:
Execute lume to get _site/test.css as:

Comments.Body:
Mmm, good point.
Maybe the `document` property should return something only if the page has `.html` extension.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
aiotter

Comments.Body:
I just committed a change to fix this issue (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/c1666a761c0b27bea480ea44cac8320b8e59e028#diff-1efcf4bbdf6611ee325c25c2f2581b150825745bc0734d67dbe7d03adb003eb4)

Regarding to document fragments, yes it's not already implemented. But what's your specific use case? Why do you want pages without html/body tags?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Nope, I don't have particular case for document without html/body tags. Someone want to provide a fragment of document for embedding purpose? IDK, it's just imagination.
I just worried about modifying something just by referencing. Maybe I'm a big worrier.
If you feel it okay, then go ahead!
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
I understand. A page represents a complete html page of the site, so I don't think this is a common casuistry.
Anyway, with the latest change, only pages with `.html` or `.htm` extensions are parsed, so if anyone want to provide a fragment of a document, a different extension can be used, so the document won't be modified.

I'm closing this. Thanks!!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1108143579.Title: Default map should be always preferred over import map given by user
1108143579.Author: aiotter
1108143579.Body:
https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/89a7954854367531c3b7fbcafff3d60ff695cfd6/ci.ts#L88

Shouldn't this be opposite?
`{ ...resolvedMap.imports, ...map.imports }` seems better.

I spent 2-3 hours resolving the following error, and that was because of mismatch between the import map I specified (lume==1.4.2) and the real Lume version(lume==1.4.3).
```
TypeError: Cannot redefine property: Symbol(Symbol.hasInstance)
    at (https://deno.land/x/deno_dom@v0.1.20-alpha/src/api.ts:58:8)
```

I set Lume url on my import map just for LSP.
On the real situation of building the site, the lume version specified through import map is better to be same as the running lume.
1108143579.PlainText:
Default map should be always preferred over import map given by user

Shouldn't this be opposite?
{ ...resolvedMap.imports, ...map.imports } seems better.
I spent 2-3 hours resolving the following error, and that was because of mismatch between the import map I specified (lume1.4.2) and the real Lume version(lume1.4.3).
I set Lume url on my import map just for LSP.
On the real situation of building the site, the lume version specified through import map is better to be same as the running lume.

Comments.Body: I assume a user may want to override the default import map of Lume, so the user provided configuration should replace the default one. Why do you want to define a different import map for Lume, but at the same time, Lume override it?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
It's for LSP. I have to tell my code editor what that `import "lume"` means in my code.
And I often switch the Lume path when I am modifying Lume itself, so it's often the case that Lume path specified on `deno run` command is different from the one on import map.

Do you think user want to override the lume path? Isn't it the partial monkey patch, which would be done by scope section of the import map?
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
Mmm, I see.
Lume has the `lume init --only=vscode` to configure vscode for that. Maybe we can improve this command and allow to assign a different import map file?
Comments.Author: oscarotero
aiotter

Comments.Body:
Mmm, I understand.

Maybe we can have a `lume init --import-map` (or something like that) that only create/update an import map, so you can use it to customize any LSP provider.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
But in that case I have to maintain two different import map. One for LSP, the other for building site. It's against DRY.

If deno LSP can read two different import map, your suggestion seems the best. But it's not the case, so...
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
How about creating import map inside the _config.ts?
You can import it in ci.ts like `import {importMap} from "./_config.ts"`. In this case we can build import map dynamically at runtime before building a site. You may export the default import map from ci.ts, which enables users to import it in _config.ts to merge with their own import map for LSP.

Or more simply, import it from `./_importMap.ts`.
Comments.Author: aiotter
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ah, I got what you mean. That `import-map` sub-command is an updater of import map. It will be called after `lume upgrade` so that import map would match the latest lume version. Nice.

Additionally, how about warning if the version of Lume is different from that of specified at import map?
Just like notification of available update:
https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/a6a8a91e8d7838096bfd31aa48646788b1a0644c/cli.ts#L152-L160
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body: Yes, good point.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: The latest development version of lume (`lume upgrade --dev`) includes the `lume import-map` command to generate/update import maps files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1124819330.Title: `Engine.render()` and `Engine.renderSync()` take generator as its first argument
1124819330.Author: aiotter
1124819330.Body:
When `Page` is a generator, `Engine.render()` and `Engine.renderSync()` take generator as its first argument, and returns generator which yields `Data`.
I don't feel this intuitive. They would better to take an iterated content and data as its argument, and returns `string`.

Also, `Engine.render()` can return generator, on the other hand, `Engine.renderSync()` cannot. I don't think the typing here is correct.
1124819330.PlainText:
Engine.render() and Engine.renderSync() take generator as its first argument

When Page is a generator, Engine.render() and Engine.renderSync() take generator as its first argument, and returns generator which yields Data.
I don't feel this intuitive. They would better to take an iterated content and data as its argument, and returns string.
Also, Engine.render() can return generator, on the other hand, Engine.renderSync() cannot. I don't think the typing here is correct.

Comments.Body:
When the page content is a function, it returns the value returned by the function. The special use case of generators is to generate one page for every yield (code here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L51-L82)

This is behavior is intentional as you can see in the docs: https://lumeland.github.io/core/pagination/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Sorry, I don't fully understand what you mean.
You said when the raw page content from Loader is a function, then the page content will be the returned value of the function after rendering. Is my understanding correct?

I still don't know why you want the Engine to handle generators. Engine is, in my understanding, responsible for modifying the raw content from the Loader to create a string (or something convertible to string by `.toString()`), which is generally a content of HTML files or assets.
Do you do this because you think generators from Loader itself should be better to be passed to the Engine? I cannot imagine the case this behaviour is needed.

For me, this sounds simpler (here, Engine is a function of `page content and data -> page content`):
```
input file -- [Loader] --> Generator<Data> -- [iterate] --> multiple Data -- [Engine] --> multiple Pages
```

Comparative to the current flow (Engine is a function of `Generator<Data> -> Generator<Data>` ):
```
input file -- [Loader] --> Generator<Data> -- [Engine] --> Generator<Data> -- [iterate] --> multiple Pages
```
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body: And if this behaviour is intended, you would better change the signature of `Engine.renderSync()`. Currently it can only return string, but it's better to return `GeneratorFunction` which returns `Generator<string>` as well.
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
The automatic page creation using generators is designed to cover any use case.
The most obvious one is pagination. In this example of paginated result: https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/blob/master/archive.tmpl.js we need to get all pages `type=post` and create new pages with the result. So we are creating new pages based on the existence of other pages.

Other example is creating a page for every tag: https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/blob/master/tag.tmpl.js Depending on the tags assigned to the existing pages, new pages will be automatically created.

We also can use the `renderOrder` option depending on if we need to create new pages after or before render other pages.

The generator function to create new pages can access to the contextual `_data` and other global values like `search` or `paginate`, filters, etc. In other words: it has the same arguments of regular pages. The only difference is it returns a generator instead of a string. This is why it uses an Engine for that, because the context is the same. And this is why the pages creation is managed by the renderer, because it's part of the rendering process. If you're curious about how Lume works internally, take a look to this explanation: https://lumeland.github.io/advanced/order-of-operations/

I don't understand what's the problem with this. Do you have a specific use case blocked by this behavior?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> And if this behaviour is intended, you would better change the signature of Engine.renderSync(). Currently it can only return string, but it's better to return GeneratorFunction which returns Generator<string> as well.

`renderSync` is used only for components, not to render/generate pages. This is why it should return `string` or objects with `toString()`
Comments.Author: oscarotero
aiotter
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I understand the reason why the returning type have to be unknown.
Then what needed may be a fine document for creating Engine interface. I had to learn it by reading the standard plugins in this repository, and it seems I had much more to know about it.

As for my JSX engine, Nano JSX already uses `Component#toString` for rendering it, so I wasn't able to override it for Lume.
And you cannot export the result of function components, or even call them by yourself. Nano JSX require `document` variable in global scope to render them. This variable is defined by default in browsers as you know, and while rendering on deno, Nano JSX will define it inside `Nano.renderSSR()`.
I don't want to render the components twice while rendering layouts, but I have no idea better than that.
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body: Anyway, now I know the current interface of `Engine.render()` and `Engine.renderSync()` is designed as intended. You can close the issue. Thanks for clarifying!
Comments.Author: aiotter

Build only one or some page

Is possible to build only one or some pages ?
something like
lume build post1.njk

Comments.Body:
No, Lume works with directories.
You could do it programaticaly, but only render the page, not save it:

```ts
const page = site.renderPage("post1.njk");
```

1184052036.Title: Distribute Lume through asdf plugins
1184052036.Author: x80486
1184052036.Body:
`asdf` is a CLI tool that can manage multiple language runtime versions on a per-project basis.

While Lume is quite easy to install, sometimes it's desirable to handle multiple versions. I think it might look very similar to the [asdf-deno](https://github.com/asdf-community/asdf-deno) repository.
1184052036.PlainText:
Distribute Lume through asdf plugins

asdf is a CLI tool that can manage multiple language runtime versions on a per-project basis.
While Lume is quite easy to install, sometimes it's desirable to handle multiple versions. I think it might look very similar to the asdf-deno repository.

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure if this is useful. `lume import-map` generates the `import_map.json` and `deno.json` files, so you have the lume version number there. It also creates two tasks in the `deno.json` file, so you can run `deno task build` and `deno task serve` directly, without using the installed lume cli (example: https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/blob/main/deno.json)

So, in theory you can manage different lume versions just editing these files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I saw that, and it looks very much like some other platforms are doing, but I think this time `https://deno.land/x/lume@vX.Y.Z/ci.ts` is downloaded all the time for every command?

At any rate, I think this could/should be a community effort instead of being maintained by `Lume` owners — until proven sufficiently useful.

As a side note, in some places people aren't able to be connected all the time.
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body:
> but I think this time https://deno.land/x/lume@vX.Y.Z/ci.ts is downloaded all the time for every command?

It's downloaded only the first time, the next time Deno use the cached version, so you don't need to be connected all the time.

If you want to work on a `asdf` plugin, I'm ok with that, but I don't know what's the advantage over the import map file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
OK, I think from the moment users can get into any recent version of `Lume` that wouldn't be needed. It's more convenient to have that in the `deno task`s.

Wouldn't that approach retire as well the Docker images? Basically with the proper `Deno` version, `Lume` will be able to run without problems.
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body: Good point. Yes I think `deno task` combined with the `import_map.json` file makes more convenient to run any lume version without install it. Users can install Deno from the official docker image and run lume directly. The only problem is if an outdated deno version is used, but in this case lume shows an error message.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Great! Then at some point we can set the path to start retiring the `Docker` images. I think whenever `Deno` tasks become stable could be the starting point :thinking:
Comments.Author: x80486

1193652712.Title: JSX does not reload if included as import
1193652712.Author: RitzyMage
1193652712.Body:
(this may be related to [issue #135](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/135)) If JSX contains a component using the comp object (e.g. ``` export default ({comp}) =>
{comp.componentName({ prop: "value", })}
``` ), then the hot reloading works just fine. Specifically, Lume outputs ``` Changes detected: - /_components/componentName.jsx 🔥 / /index.jsx Changes sent to the browser ``` However, if included via an import (e.g. ``` import ComponentName from "./_components/componentName.jsx"; export default () =>
``` ), then the reload does not work for changes in the included component (`componentName` in this example). In fact, the server must be completely restarted to get these changes. Specifically, Lume outputs ``` Changes detected: - /_components/componentName.jsx ``` but does not output the hot reloading text.
1193652712.PlainText:
JSX does not reload if included as import

(this may be related to issue #135)
If JSX contains a component using the comp object (e.g.
),
then the hot reloading works just fine. Specifically, Lume outputs
However, if included via an import (e.g.
), then the reload does not work for changes in the included component (componentName in this example). In fact, the server must be completely restarted to get these changes. Specifically, Lume outputs
but does not output the hot reloading text.

Comments.Body:
This is a know limitation of Deno, see https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8327 ATM, there isn't a good way to implement a hot module reload system for imported modules. But you can use jsx with Lume modules. In your example, you could do this: ```jsx export default ({comp}) =>
``` This is the only way to use jsx modules supporting hot reloading in Lume. And it's even more convenient because you don't need to import the modules, they are automatically available.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Just tested on `v1.7.2` and the `<comp.componentName />` syntax does hot reload. Thanks!
Comments.Author: RitzyMage

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Hmm. I've ran `deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume/install.ts` today saying it installed v1.7.3 and using `deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume/ci.ts --serve --dev` to run the dev server but running `deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume/ci.ts --version` gives me `v1.4.3`. How do I get ci.ts to use latest? I thought it did since there wasn't any version lock in the URL.

Comments.Author: enjikaka

Comments.Body: Yes, by using a module without version, deno download the latest available version at this moment and cache it. The lume cli fixes this, but if you don't want to depend on having lume installed in your computer, you can run `lume import-map`, that creates two files: A `import_map.json` file with your lume version and  a `deno.json` file containing two tasks, so you can run `deno task build` or `deno task serve` to run lume. (Tasks is a new thing in Deno, similar to run scripts with npm: https://deno.land/manual/tools/task_runner)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Okey, thanks for the info. I'll try the tasks!
Comments.Author: enjikaka

1205420213.Title: Notify if a page is empty and wont be exported
1205420213.Author: oscarotero
1205420213.Body: Empty pages aren't saved in the `dest` folder (for example, after creating a `css` file without content). There should be a notification in the build process about these pages. Something like `Skipped empty page ${page.data.url}`
1205420213.PlainText:
Notify if a page is empty and wont be exported

Empty pages aren't saved in the dest folder (for example, after creating a css file without content). There should be a notification in the build process about these pages. Something like Skipped empty page ${page.data.url}

1205422524.Title: Convert string dates to `Date` automatically
1205422524.Author: oscarotero
1205422524.Body:
Lume expects the `date` property to be a `Date` instance. In `yaml` this is converted automatically but in JavaScript this code fails:

```js
export const date = "2022-04-15";
```

because it needs to be:

```js
export const date = new Date("2022-04-15");
```

Lume should convert valid strings to `Date` here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/page_loader.ts#L86
1205422524.PlainText:
Convert string dates to Date automatically

Lume expects the date property to be a Date instance. In yaml this is converted automatically but in JavaScript this code fails:
because it needs to be:
Lume should convert valid strings to Date here:

To add custom headers to some urls.

Comments.Body:
I'm guessing this is related, but it would be nice if we could cache HTTP requests. It seems there is middleware for 'no-cache' but when I push a copy of (https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/)[https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/] to Vercel it does not cache any HTTP requests. I'm guessing the example at (https://lumeland.github.io/base-blog/)[https://lumeland.github.io/base-blog/] has HTTP headers automatically set within the GitHub service since there does not appear to be anything in the code setting the caching header.

(I just began playing with Lume today. I love it.)
Comments.Author: kuhlaid

Comments.Body: @kuhlaid These middlewares are used only for Deno runtime environment. Vercel doesn't run Deno (just serve statically the pages build by Lume). For cache, Lume has the expires middleware, that set the `Expires` header to the http responses. I think you can achieve something similar in Vercel by configuring your headers (more info: https://vercel.com/docs/project-configuration#project-configuration/headers)
Comments.Author: oscarotero
kuhlaid
oscarotero

1206192087.Title: How to slot content inside template : Nunjucks
1206192087.Author: chadams
1206192087.Body:
Given the template ```
{{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}}
``` What I really want is to slot content into a reusable component, like React ``` {{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}} ``` My initial thought is to pass in like this... ``` {{comp.Container({content:[ comp.Button({text:"example1"}), comp.Button({text:"example1"}) ]})}} ``` but is starting to look messy, is there a way to put JSX components inside NJK? or somehow pass NJK blocks to a NJK component?
1206192087.PlainText:
How to slot content inside template : Nunjucks

Given the template
What I really want is to slot content into a reusable component, like React
My initial thought is to pass in like this...
but is starting to look messy, is there a way to put JSX components inside NJK? or somehow pass NJK blocks to a NJK component?

Comments.Body:
You can use lume components as react components. For example: ```jsx export default function Container({ comp }) { return <> } ``` And then this component can be used in other jsx component or nunjucks: ```njk
{{ comp.Container() }}
``` The `comp` argument is always available not only in the layouts but also the components. FYI, I'm planning to implement the following syntax for components in nunjucks: ```njk {% comp Container %} {{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}} {% endcomp %} ``` So this ease the use of nested components. But it's not implemented yet.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1225116154.Title: Extract the Date from the directory path, not only filename
1225116154.Author: oscarotero
1225116154.Body:
Related: #197 

Currently, Lume only extracts the data from the filename (e.x: `/posts/2022-01-01_first-post.md`).
For better compatibility with Hugo, it should extract the date if it's in the directory (`/posts/2022-01-01_first-post/index.md`).
1225116154.PlainText:
Extract the Date from the directory path, not only filename

Related: #197
Currently, Lume only extracts the data from the filename (e.x: /posts/2022-01-01_first-post.md).
For better compatibility with Hugo, it should extract the date if it's in the directory (/posts/2022-01-01_first-post/index.md).

1251609916.Title: Support global templateEngine settings with images in content folders
1251609916.Author: Brixy
1251609916.Body:
I am just porting another site to Lume from Eleventy. And it works just great!

The old sites uses Eleventy’s Nunjucks shortcodes within markdown files. Lume supports this with `templateEngine: njk,md` in front matter.

Yet, I noticed an issue: Apparently you cannot use this setting globally for all blog posts in combination with images in folders, probably because images are pages in Lume:

```
# posts/_data.js
export const templateEngine = 'njk,md';
```

```
posts/
└── 2022-05-28_a-post/
    ├── index.md
    ├── file.pdf
    └── image.jpg
```

As soon as you remove the image file from the folder everything works as expected.

(Honestly speaking I have not tried Lume’s modules plugin with this combination.)

Thank you.
1251609916.PlainText:
Support global templateEngine settings with images in content folders

I am just porting another site to Lume from Eleventy. And it works just great!
The old sites uses Eleventy’s Nunjucks shortcodes within markdown files. Lume supports this with templateEngine: njk,md in front matter.
Yet, I noticed an issue: Apparently you cannot use this setting globally for all blog posts in combination with images in folders, probably because images are pages in Lume:
As soon as you remove the image file from the folder everything works as expected.
(Honestly speaking I have not tried Lume’s modules plugin with this combination.)
Thank you.

Comments.Body:
Mmm, I understand.

In Lume all files are "pages" but there are two types of pages: regular pages and assets pages (it's [explained here](https://lume.land/docs/core/concepts/#page-files)). Assets pages don't apply the `layout` variable (to avoid an image/css/js be wrapped in a layout), but it do apply the render. Maybe assets pages shouldn't be rendered, and only apply (pre)processors.

As a workaround, you can use a preprocessor to apply the templateEngine variable only to md files:

```js
site.preprocess([".md"], (page) => page.data.templateEngine = "njk,md");
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
This works perfectly! Thank you.

A global setting of `templateEngine` does not seem to have any disadvantages.
Comments.Author: Brixy

1292404827.Title: Parcel plugin should process existing sourcemaps
1292404827.Author: binyamin
1292404827.Body: The parcel module Lume uses doesn't support passing a source-map. However, it _is_ possible to merge them, and it doesn't look too difficult. See https://github.com/parcel-bundler/parcel-css/issues/219#issuecomment-1173149370.
1292404827.PlainText:
Parcel plugin should process existing sourcemaps

The parcel module Lume uses doesn't support passing a source-map. However, it is possible to merge them, and it doesn't look too difficult. See .

Comments.Body:
Ok, I'll take a look.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero Update: at the moment, the `@parcel/source-map` module doesn't work with Deno (See https://github.com/parcel-bundler/source-map/issues/110).
- Unpkg serves the code as CommonJS, which Deno doesn't like
  - May be fixed in https://github.com/parcel-bundler/source-map/pull/111
- Esm.sh _does_ serve the code as ESM. But, the way it servers WASM files means that the JS can't import it.
  - Open issue https://github.com/esm-dev/esm.sh/issues/358
- I managed to get it working, by rewriting some of the module locally, but that's not an ideal situation.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
Very interesting info, thanks!

I guess we have to wait until this PR is merged: https://github.com/parcel-bundler/source-map/pull/111
Another option is use temporary your fork

1299607630.Title: Can't copy files with prefix `_`
1299607630.Author: fabon-f
1299607630.Body:
# Problem

I want to copy `_headers` and `_redirects` to dest directory and tried like this in `_config.js`.

```js
site.copy("_headers")
site.copy("_redirects")
```

But these files are just silently ignored.

To my understanding, files with prefix `_` are ignored by default. But in my opinion it is better that this default behavior can be overridden by explicit config.

# Workaround for now

Renaming files and use second argument of `site.copy` works properly.

```
site.copy("headers", "_headers")
```

But these files are just silently ignored.
To my understanding, files with prefix _ are ignored by default. But in my opinion it is better that this default behavior can be overridden by explicit config.
Workaround for now
Renaming files and use second argument of site.copy works properly.

Comments.Body: Good point. I'll fix it in the next version. Thanks!

1299980654.Title: Support `deno.jsonc` for Deno config file
1299980654.Author: binyamin
1299980654.Body:
### Summary
Currently, Lume only supports `deno.json` files. Deno itself supports `deno.jsonc` files as well. Ideally, Lume would match Deno's behavior.

### Brainstorming
- Deno's standard library includes a JSONC parser in [`std/encoding`](https://deno.land/std@0.147.0/encoding/jsonc.ts). It does _not_ include a method for _serializing_ JSONC (see https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/discussions/1803).
- We only write to the deno config after the _vendor_ and _init_ commands. That's not too often. It might be worth parsing the JSONC using `std/encoding`, and doing some RegExp magic to overwrite a portion of the file. Just brainstorming here.

### Notes
- This feature is not in any way critical. I simply wanted to track JSONC support, and to explain why Lume does not in fact support JSONC.
1299980654.PlainText:
Support deno.jsonc for Deno config file

Summary
Currently, Lume only supports deno.json files. Deno itself supports deno.jsonc files as well. Ideally, Lume would match Deno's behavior.
Brainstorming
Deno's standard library includes a JSONC parser in std/encoding. It does not include a method for serializing JSONC (see ).
We only write to the deno config after the vendor and init commands. That's not too often. It might be worth parsing the JSONC using std/encoding, and doing some RegExp magic to overwrite a portion of the file. Just brainstorming here.
Notes
This feature is not in any way critical. I simply wanted to track JSONC support, and to explain why Lume does not in fact support JSONC.

Comments.Body:
As you said, Lume needs to read and write `deno.json` files to configure tasks and the import map file. In addition to `vendor` and `init`, this file is updated after `upgrade` and `import-map` commands.

Personally, I think it was a bad idea from Deno developers to allow to use jsonc here, it's not standard, introduce a unneeded complexity and inconsistency (what happens if `deno.json` and `deno.jsonc` exists in the same folder?, why it can be used for deno config but not for import_map.jsonc?), it's hard to parse and, specially to write while keeping the existing comments.

When Deno standard library include a option to parse and stringify JSONC, I'll consider support it, althought I think everybody should use json format.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> why it can be used for deno config but not for import_map.jsonc?

Because Deno follows the spec, which only uses JSON: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12993
Comments.Author: kidonng

Comments.Body:
Upgrading Lume still yields this error:
```
deno task lume upgrade
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A --v8-flags=--max-old-space-size=8192 - "upgrade"
Deno configuration file not found: deno.json
```
Btw, there is a std lib for jsonc now: [@std/jsonc - JSR](https://jsr.io/@std/jsonc "@std/jsonc - JSR")
Comments.Author: ooker777

Comments.Body: @ooker777 You're right. In recent Lume versions the upgrade script has changed and jsonc files are not loaded. I'll fix it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1300002345.Title: Passing options to plugins
1300002345.Author: bryophyta
1300002345.PlainText:
Passing options to plugins

Hi, I was trying to add options to a markdown-it plugin like markdown-it-toc-done-right, where the plugin docs suggest passing options as a parameter to the markdown.use() call, e.g.
The built-in way to add plugins to markdown-it in lume doesn't (as far as I can see) give you access to the markdown object itself, because the markdown.use() calls are done behind the scenes by lume. So I was looking through the source code to see if there was a way to patch lume to allow me to pass options to markdown-it-toc-done-right. When I looked, I found that if a markdown plugin is an array, then the contents of the array will be spread into the markdown.use() call:
This makes it possible to pass options to a specific markdown-it plugin from lume, by passing an array of the form [plugin, { options }] as a value in the plugins array in the markdown object that's passed to site.use(), e.g.:
This does exactly what I was hoping! But I haven't been able to find any mention of this method in the lume docs. So I wanted to ask: Is this the intended behaviour of the lume markdown plugins API? If so, is it documented anywhere?
If it is the intended behaviour, but isn't documented, then I would be happy to open a PR to add it to the docs. If it isn't intended behaviour, would you be open to the idea of making it a stable part of the API?
Thanks in advance!

Comments.Body:
Hi.
Yes, it's the intended behavior. In fact, it's used in the lume.land website: https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/blob/main/_config.ts#L13-L17

And you're right, it's not documented, so a PR is appreciated. Thanks!!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
great, thanks very much! I should have thought to check the lume.land code 🤦‍♂️

I'll try to open a docs PR in the next few days
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body: I've opened a PR for this in the docs repo: https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/pull/12/files
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body: Merged! Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1301295229.Title: SASS plugin includes
1301295229.Author: jrson83
1301295229.Body:
# Version Info
Windows 11 (64 bit)
deno 1.23.2 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.4.132.8
typescript 4.7.2
lume v1.10.0

# Description

The SASS docs [Configuration](https://lume.land/plugins/sass/#configuration) chapter states:

> includes: An array of directories to search for the `@imported` files. By default is _includes.

When I move `.scss` files outside the `_includes` directory, they get transformed to `_site/whatever_dir/[file].css`, even if I do not specify any `sass.includes` path.

I created the following project structure:

```
lume-root
 ┣ _includes
 ┃ ┗ sass
 ┃ ┃ ┣ index.scss
 ┃ ┃ ┗ _test.scss
 ┣ deno.json
 ┣ import_map.json
 ┗ _config.ts
```

```scss
// index.scss
@import "./test";

body {
  background-color: #000;
}
```

```js
// _config.ts
import lume from "lume/mod.ts";
import sass from "lume/plugins/sass.ts";

const site = lume();

site.use(sass());

export default site;
```

When I run `deno task serve`, the `.scss` files are not getting transformed to `.css`.

In serve mode, if I make changes to the files they getting registered:
```shell
Changes detected:
- /_includes/sass/index.scss
```

I tried setting `sass.includes` with `./` and without `./` combinations as `string` and also as `Array<string>`, but nothing worked.

- ["./_includes/sass/"]
- ["./_includes/sass"]
- ["./_includes/"]
- ["./_includes"]

I created a different directory with an underscore `_styles` and moved the files in the directory, they not getting transformed. I also tried to specify the `sass.includes`.

So it seems, the plugins default is to search in every subdir of the rootdir, but not if the dir contains an underscore.
If you need I'll create an example for reproduction. Thanks for the great work!
1301295229.PlainText:
SASS plugin includes

Version Info
Windows 11 (64 bit)
deno 1.23.2 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.4.132.8
typescript 4.7.2
lume v1.10.0
Description
The SASS docs Configuration chapter states:
includes: An array of directories to search for the @imported files. By default is _includes.
When I move .scss files outside the _includes directory, they get transformed to _site/whatever_dir/[file].css, even if I do not specify any sass.includes path.
I created the following project structure:
When I run deno task serve, the .scss files are not getting transformed to .css.
In serve mode, if I make changes to the files they getting registered:
I tried setting sass.includes with ./ and without ./ combinations as string and also as Array<string>, but nothing worked.
["./_includes/sass/"]
["./_includes/sass"]
["./_includes/"]
["./_includes"]
I created a different directory with an underscore _styles and moved the files in the directory, they not getting transformed. I also tried to specify the sass.includes.
So it seems, the plugins default is to search in every subdir of the rootdir, but not if the dir contains an underscore.
If you need I'll create an example for reproduction. Thanks for the great work!

Comments.Body:
The `_includes` folder contains files that can be included by other files but they aren't loaded and rendered if no other file include them. In your example, you have all scss files in the `_includes` folder but there isn't any other scss file outside this folder that import these files. Let's say you have this structure:

```
lume-root
 ┣ _includes
 ┃ ┗ sass
 ┃   ┗ component1.scss
 ┃   ┗ component2.scss
 ┣ deno.json
 ┣ import_map.json
 ┣ styles.scss
 ┗ _config.ts
```
And the `styles.scss` file (that it's ouside `_includes` so Lume will load it) import the other two files:

```scss
// styles.scss

@import "sass/component1.scss";
@import "sass/component2.scss";
```

This will output the `_site/styles.css` file with the content of the two imported files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> In your example, you have all scss files in the _includes folder but there isn't any other scss file outside this folder that import these files. Let's say you have this structure:

Okay, now I got it, If I set it up like this, it is working. Thanks for the explanation.

Closing.
Comments.Author: jrson83

1303186163.Title: Generator plugin
1303186163.Author: oscarotero
1303186163.Body:
A plugin that inject the `lume` global variable with the lume version and other environment variables that can be used in the page, for example to create a `<meta name="generator">` tag.

"Inspired" by https://www.11ty.dev/docs/data-eleventy-supplied/#eleventy-variable
1303186163.PlainText:
Generator plugin

A plugin that inject the lume global variable with the lume version and other environment variables that can be used in the page, for example to create a <meta name="generator"> tag.
"Inspired" by

Comments.Body: I don't think this needs a separate plugin. Just expose a global `lume.version` data property. This also makes room for other build metadata, such as `site.options.dev` (as `lume.dev`), or `lume.mode` to represent whether lume is in build, serve, or watch mode.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body: It could be added to the "metas" plugin, for easy usage.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
> It could be added to the "metas" plugin, for easy usage.

Good idea!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just added the `generator` property to the `metas` plugin. It can be `true` (so it's generated automatically) or a string (to customize the value).

I'm not going to add other values for now.
BTW, the `dev` mode will be deprecated soon: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/201.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Gotcha. I do think it would be useful to expose the Lume version as a global variable.

oscarotero

1306357981.Title: Detect and load `.env` files automatically?
1306357981.Author: oscarotero
1306357981.Body:
Should Lume load automatically .env files in the root directory?

[bun.sh](https://bun.sh/) does it automatically. Deno's std library has dotenv implemented: https://deno.land/std@0.148.0/dotenv so it's easy to use it in Lume.
1306357981.PlainText:
Detect and load .env files automatically?

Should Lume load automatically .env files in the root directory?
bun.sh does it automatically. Deno's std library has dotenv implemented:  so it's easy to use it in Lume.

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure if you're seeking feedback on this, so please feel free to ignore this if not!

For what it's worth, I would be inclined against adding this behaviour, unless there's a significant reason for adding it. While it would definitely be convenient for most cases, I think there is an advantage to keeping `env` management more explicit. Especially given that, as you noted, it's not too inconvenient to add it to a project with Deno's standard lib.

Having said that, I could imagine that some people using Lume might not know Deno in great depth yet, so it would be nice to make it easy for them to use `.env` config. What would you think of the idea of adding a section to the docs which gives an example of how to add `env` management using Deno's standard libs?

Again, this is just my opinion. And I'd definitely be interested to hear the argument for the alternative view!
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body: I don't have opinions about the feature itself, but I'm very curious about use cases in a static site generator. Environmental variables are mostly for server side programs and for a SSG it would require at most `NODE_ENV`.
Comments.Author: kidonng

Comments.Body:
Interesting question! A couple of use-cases for environmental variables which I can think of, for a library like Lume:

- Set a target directory for the build process to build to (e.g. if you have an experimental build and a stable build).
- You might also use an abstraction like `dev`/`prod` to control other similar things about the build process (e.g. in dev you might want to skip lengthy processes like image processing).
- If you're running the build process in a continuous integration setup like Github Actions, you may want to be able to pass sensitive values like an API key or salt to the build process, without exposing them in your logs etc.

You could probably find good alternatives to using env variables for all of these use-cases, but it does seem to me like they'd be quite helpful. I'd be interested to hear your thoughts though -- do you think that using env variables would be unsuitable in these cases?
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body:
> I'm not sure if you're seeking feedback on this

@bryophyta Feedback is always welcome! It's interesting your thoughts about making the `env` variables more explicit and reduce the  "magic" from Lume.

> I'm very curious about use cases in a static site generator.

A real use case is a static site using the data from the Eventbrite API to show the number of tickets sold. The site is regenerated every hour to get up to date data. It requires a token and an event Id to connect to the API, that are stored in env variables.


> - Set a target directory for the build process to build to (e.g. if you have an experimental build and a stable build).
> - You might also use an abstraction like dev/prod to control other similar things about the build process (e.g. in dev you might want to skip lengthy processes like image processing).

I think these use cases can be addressed easily passing the env variable directly in the build script. For example: `ENV=prod deno task build`. Or creating two deno tasks like `deno task build` and `deno task build-prod`.
To me, the most interesing use case of env files is to store secret values that you don't want to include in the git repo. But I'm agree that maybe it's not a so common use case.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> * Set a target directory for the build process to build to (e.g. if you have an experimental build and a stable build).

This should be using different config, not environment variables.

> * You might also use an abstraction like `dev`/`prod` to control other similar things about the build process (e.g. in dev you might want to skip lengthy processes like image processing).

This is what `NODE_ENV` does (pardon me for mentioning again, I just need a metaphor).

> * If you're running the build process in a continuous integration setup like Github Actions, you may want to be able to pass sensitive values like an API key or salt to the build process, without exposing them in your logs etc.

I think @oscarotero's example covers this well, and also quoting them:

> But I'm agree that maybe it's not a so common use case.

If lume starts to load `.env` automatically, then it probably needs to provide a config to opt out. Or lume can provide a option to load `.env` that is disabled by default.

But it's just too much hassle. Anyone who really wants `.env` need to do nothing more than putting the following line in `_config.ts`:

```js
import "https://deno.land/std/dotenv/load.ts"
```

This is not much harder in Node either, just an extra step to install `dotenv`. The whole point of `.env` is easy to configure.

To sum it all, I love tools picking up related files, but only if those are indeed related.

I would also like to quote one of the goals (or selling points) of Deno:

> Secure by default. No file, network, or environment access, unless explicitly enabled.

---

> [bun.sh](https://bun.sh/) does it automatically

It's a marketing strategy expected from an all-in-one Node alternative that aims to eliminate as much dependencies as possible. But it also contradicts to Deno's philosophy.
Comments.Author: kidonng

1310185532.Title: Recommended way to add post-`use()` configuration to markdown-it plugins?
1310185532.Author: bryophyta
1310185532.PlainText:
Recommended way to add post-use() configuration to markdown-it plugins?

Hi, I'm not sure if this issue belongs here or in the main lume repo, but I'm happy to re-open it there if that's better.
Issue
For at least some markdown-it plugins, the recommended way of customising the output requires setting properties on the markdown-it instance's .renderer.rules object. An example is customising the markup for footnotes in markdown-it-footnotes.
As far as I can tell, the built-in implementation of markdown-it in lume doesn't expose the markdown-it instance after initialisation. If so, it won't be possible to make these kinds of customisations. If you create a custom instance of markdown-it then the built-in implementation can be overridden using site.loadPages() in the lume _config.ts. (Minimal reproducible example included below.)
This works, but I was wondering if this is the recommended way to make these sorts of customisations to renderers in lume or not? If so, do you think it would be worthwhile to document it? (I'm happy to open a PR.) If there is an alternative recommended method, would you be able to say anything about how you would suggest doing this?
Thanks!
Minimal working example
Running the lume ci.ts from the command line with these from the directory containing these files renders a page with the custom footnote heading, as intended:
(The backref character link character at the end is rendered wrongly in the screenshot, but I'm pretty sure that this is an unrelated bug with my system.)

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I just added the `rules` option to markdown. It will be available in the next version.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> I just added the rules option to markdown. It will be available in the next version.

wow, thanks for such a quick response!

just to clarify one point from your original response -- is the `site.formats.get()` method that you mentioned a part of lume's external API? Or is it only designed for use within the internals of lume? (I'm partly wondering whether as users we can consider it to be a stable API or not.)
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body:
I only consider stable API all `site` functions that allows to configure the site building (`site.copy()`, `site.use()`, `site.ignore()`, etc (all functions documented in the lume.land site).

All internal objects, like `site.formats`, could change in the future, for performance improvements, implementing new features, etc. I think they won't change much in the short term, but I cannot confirm 100%.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1310425925.Author: jrson83
1310425925.Body:
I'm using the [remark plugin](https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/tree/main/remark), which now adds `vFile.data` to the markdown `pages.data`. It is working great, so I wrote another rehype plugin, to exctract an `excerpt` (the first paragraph, with max length of 140 characters), from every blog post. The `vfile.data.excerpt` gets appended to the `page.data.excerpt`, like using the `rehype-extract-toc` plugin. Now using the `.md` files layout file, I have access to the `page.data` which includes: ```js url: "/posts/blog-post-02/", excerpt: "Lorem markdownum exclamant. Saevit iussa, in parentem praenovimus gerit sceleratior ipse ima quam\nal...", toc: [ { depth: 2, value: "Praevisos ductae et poenae esse pereunt enituntur" }, { depth: 2, value: "Illis Hyadasque dederat" }, { depth: 2, value: "Adversi firmat nigra et traiectus quos" }, { depth: 2, value: "Dignior lucis multa comitavit tenet" } ], ``` In my `index.tsx` page file, for which the `excerpt` data was supposed to be, I loop through the posts: ```js export default ({ search }: SiteData) => { const posts = search.pages("type=post", "date=desc", 3); return (

Recent blog posts

{posts.map(({ data }) => (

{data.title as string}

))}
); }; ``` Here I don't have access to the data, since like I read in the docs [render order](https://lume.land/docs/core/render-order/), the pages didn't exist before the `index` page was rendered. I tried changing the `renderOrder` from all posts inside `./posts/_data.ts`: `export const renderOrder = -1;` But after build I can see no navbar items at all, (they are defined through `menu` inside the pages), the blog posts getting displayed, but the `page.data` still not contains `excerpt` & `toc`. When I use `export const renderOrder = 1;` inside the index page, the navbar does not contain the index page, the blog posts getting displayed, but the `page.data` still not contains `excerpt` & `toc`. Here is my `_config.ts` order: ```js site .copy("assets", ".") .use(readingTime()) .use(date()) .use(slugifyUrls()) .use(remark({ remarkPlugins, rehypePlugins, })) .use(preactjsx()) .use(postcss()) ``` If you can give any advice, I would be pleased.

Here I don't have access to the data, since like I read in the docs render order, the pages didn't exist before the index page was rendered. I tried changing the renderOrder from all posts inside ./posts/_data.ts: export const renderOrder = -1;
But after build I can see no navbar items at all, (they are defined through menu inside the pages), the blog posts getting displayed, but the page.data still not contains excerpt & toc.
When I use export const renderOrder = 1; inside the index page, the navbar does not contain the index page, the blog posts getting displayed, but the page.data still not contains excerpt & toc.
Here is my _config.ts order:
If you can give any advice, I would be pleased.

Comments.Body:
This is not related with `renderOrder`. The reason is the data inserted by remark plugin is not persistent. It's only available while the page is being rendered (the page file and the layouts used by the page), allowing to insert the `toc` inside the page layout.

I have to think in a way to make this data persistent, in order to be used by other pages. Meanwhile, a simple way to extract an excerpt is this https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/main/mod.ts#L24-L26, using a `<!-- more -->` comment in the post content.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> I have to think in a way to make this data persistent

That would be cool. For my readingTime plugin I figured out I had to use `site.preprocess` to make the data persistent.

> Meanwhile, a simple way to extract an excerpt is this https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/main/mod.ts#L24-L26, using a <!-- more --> comment in the post content.

Thanks I will try it out.
Comments.Author: jrson83
jrson83

Comments.Body:
What @oscarotero has suggested is a workaround and not a solution. We should have a discussion around how Lume can have an API for any data that is accessible to `search` and `paginate` that can be safely enriched by plugins.

I was thinking about introducing some sort of global store that can be referred by `search` but this is something that needs deliberation and community feedback.
Comments.Author: naiyerasif

Comments.Body:
The data object passed to the renderers is a copy of the original page data object (here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L240) to prevent issues caused by modify this object later (like here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L271-L272), specially after rendering the page again after an update.

This is the reason why data stored in this object is accessible only when the page is being rendered, but is not really stored in the `page.data` object, hence, is not present when retrieving this page with `search`.

One simple solution could be include the page in the data object, like:

```ts
let data = { ...page.data, page };
```

So plugins can store data in the `data` object, like they do now or in the `data.page.data` object (so it's persistent).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1315929359.Title: Invalid `url` values are not caught
1315929359.Author: bryophyta
1315929359.Body:
When specifying a `url` for a page in its yaml header, some values will fail silently. For example:

```markdown
---
# index.md

url: 0
---

# Hello world
```

This page will be built as `_site/index.md`. I believe that this is because the top-level checks that happen on the `url` value are to see if it is `false` or has `typeof string` or `typeof function`. https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/8e6bb7b112861b2699a37d89501aeb000768298f/core/renderer.ts#L175-L220

Because values like `0` are parsed as numbers, they fail these conditions (thereby bypassing the validation that happens for strings) and end up with the default `url`.[^1] My assumption is that this is not the desired behaviour?

Do you have a sense of what the desired behaviour would be, here? I'm not sure whether it would be better for it to crash, or to warn the user, or to coerce the value to a string. But if you have a view on what the desired behaviour should be, I'd be happy to try and write up a PR to implement it.

[^1]: Similarly, if an array is provided as the `url` value, this will also fail silently and the page will be built with its default url.
1315929359.PlainText:
Invalid url values are not caught When specifying a url for a page in its yaml header, some values will fail silently. For example: This page will be built as _site/index.md. I believe that this is because the top-level checks that happen on the url value are to see if it is false or has typeof string or typeof function. Because values like 0 are parsed as numbers, they fail these conditions (thereby bypassing the validation that happens for strings) and end up with the default url.1 My assumption is that this is not the desired behaviour? Do you have a sense of what the desired behaviour would be, here? I'm not sure whether it would be better for it to crash, or to warn the user, or to coerce the value to a string. But if you have a view on what the desired behaviour should be, I'd be happy to try and write up a PR to implement it.
  1. Similarly, if an array is provided as the url value, this will also fail silently and the page will be built with its default url.↩

Comments.Body:
A quick workaround for your use case is force a string using quotes:

```md
---
# index.md

url: "0"
---

# Hello world
```

I think the behavior should be to throw an error with a message like `Invalid url value. It must be a string or a function returning a string`.

Your PR is very appreciated. Thank you!
Comments.Author: oscarotero
