1327630117.Title: Extending interface Data for better user TypeScript experience
1327630117.Author: jrson83
1327630117.PlainText:
Extending interface Data for better user TypeScript experience

Description
Since I'm currently using the JSX plugin in combination with TypeScript for creating my blog, after talking to @oscarotero we came up with the idea of extending the Data interface with a PageData interface. To make a better user experience it makes sense to include already known default properties of Lume types in that interface.
Example of TS errors, when no type assertions/definitions exist for [index: string]: unknown.
This would reduce the needing of creating type assertions or type guards for those properties by the user.
Experience
While developing I was in need of the following missing but existing propperties in Data:
children: JSX.IntrinsicElements
comp: any since ProxyComponents did not work that great
paginate: Paginator
pagination: ?
results: unknown[] (pain)
search: Search
While developing as a workaround, I created a custom interface to extend Data and also override Pages and added the missing typings:
While I could easily use Search and Paginator, the following changes had to made on existing interfaces:
PaginateResults["pagination"] had to be moved out of Paginator as it's own interface
ProxyComponents some how needed a modification. I could not get into and used any to overcome the ts error:
Suggestions for restructuring existing types
Move Pagination object  out of PaginateResult
I have no idea how to modify ProxyComponents or ComponentFunction to get rid of the error described above.
Suggestions for interface PageData
Implementation of existing Lume types and default propperties
Conclusion
It would be awesome if we could restructure the interfaces like this. I'm not sure how many users are using JSX with TypeScript in Lume, but I think this will be a great improvement, since I struggled with custom type guards and also type assertions and didn't like them.
In addition it would be cool to have two more docs on the Lume page:
Core > Navigation (which desrcribes the usage of menu, since it's too deep nested/hidden at the moment)
Configuration > Typescript (a small guide on how to use with TypeScript, extend existing or implement custom typings)
@oscarotero, I appreciate that you take the time. Looking forward on your thoughts. If you need an example repo, let me know.

Comments.Body: Thanks. Just to clarify: `menu` is not a thing in Lume. It's just a property I've created in the base blog repository in order to create the navigation bar (select all pages with menu.visible as `true` and sort them by `menu.order`: https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/blob/master/_includes/layouts/base.njk#L21).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Thanks. Just to clarify: `menu` is not a thing in Lume. It's just a property I've created in the base blog repository in order to create the navigation bar (select all pages with menu.visible as `true` and sort them by `menu.order`: https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/blob/master/_includes/layouts/base.njk#L21).

I saw this first on the `base-demo`, when I was looking on how to create a nav and found the docs refering to it later. I think this concept could be implemented in general in Lume and then also referenced in the docs, to help the users.

This would be a great, since also [Jekyll](https://jekyllrb.com/tutorials/navigation/#scenario-8-retrieving-items-based-on-front-matter-properties) and [Hugo](https://gohugo.io/templates/menu-templates/#menu-entries-from-the-pages-front-matter) introduces this feature on there website.

1333052958.Title: Remove Deno arguments passed by Lume
1333052958.Author: oscarotero
1333052958.Body:
Lume allows to pass arguments to Deno using the `--` separator. For example to use the `--compact` flag for Node compatibilty:

```
lume -s -- --compact
```

This feature was introduced before Deno tasks and Deno config file. Now this can be done editing the deno.json config file. So this feature (that is not documented) must be removed.

1333473953.Title: Remove CLI interface
1333473953.Author: oscarotero
1333473953.Body:
For v2.0.0 I'm thinking of removing the possibility of installing Lume with `deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume/install.ts`.

There are some reasons for this:
- Deno install doesn't work in all cases and requires to update the `PATH`.
- It's more clear and portable to update Lume by editing the `import_map.json` file. It allows to have different projects using different versions of Lume.
- Deno tasks is a better way to run Lume.
- This removes completely the bug of having two different versions of Lume (one installed globally and other different in the import_map.json).

The new workflow could be the following:

- Init a new project with `deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume/init.ts`. This script is like `lume init`, creating the _config.ts, import_map.json and deno.json files.
- The task `deno task lume` is the main entry for all Lume comands. For example:
  - `deno task lume upgrade` To upgrade Lume (the import_map.json file)
  - `deno task lume vendor` To vendor the project.
  -  `deno task lume` To build the project
  -  `deno task lume -s` To build the project and start a server
  - etc
- The tasks `deno task build` and `deno task serve` can be keept, as an alias of `deno task lume` and `deno task lume -s` (as they are already).

The only drawback is that all projects must be initiated. It's not allowed to run Lume without the `_config.ts` file as already. But I don't think there are many cases in which the default config is enough and having a explicit `_config` file is always better.
1333473953.PlainText:
Remove CLI interface

For v2.0.0 I'm thinking of removing the possibility of installing Lume with deno run -A 
There are some reasons for this:
Deno install doesn't work in all cases and requires to update the PATH.
It's more clear and portable to update Lume by editing the import_map.json file. It allows to have different projects using different versions of Lume.
Deno tasks is a better way to run Lume.
This removes completely the bug of having two different versions of Lume (one installed globally and other different in the import_map.json).
The new workflow could be the following:
Init a new project with deno run -A  This script is like lume init, creating the _config.ts, import_map.json and deno.json files.
The task deno task lume is the main entry for all Lume comands. For example:
deno task lume upgrade To upgrade Lume (the import_map.json file)
deno task lume vendor To vendor the project.
deno task lume To build the project
deno task lume -s To build the project and start a server
etc
The tasks deno task build and deno task serve can be keept, as an alias of deno task lume and deno task lume -s (as they are already).
The only drawback is that all projects must be initiated. It's not allowed to run Lume without the _config.ts file as already. But I don't think there are many cases in which the default config is enough and having a explicit _config file is always better.

Comments.Body: To me, this creates another step for getting set-up. I vote that the CLI stays. It could be less dominant, less powerful even, but it can provide a better experience for developers.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
@binyamin Ok, I think we can combine the best of the two worlds. I've created Lume CLI as a separated module.
Here's more info: https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-cli/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1337091376.Title: Allow to assign multiple template engines to a format.
1337091376.Author: oscarotero
1337091376.Body:
It's possible to assign multiple template engines to a page, using the `templateEngine` value. For example:

```md
---
title: Hello
templateEngine: njk,md
---

# {{ title }}
```

But it's not possible to register a format to use multiple template engines. For example:

```ts
site.engine([".njk.md"], nunjucksEngine, markdownEngine);
```

This would allows to bring a initial mdx support with:

```ts
site.engine([".mdx"], jsxEngine, mdEngine);
```

1353447163.Title: Inline plugin does not respect additional style tag attributes
1353447163.Author: jrson83
1353447163.Body:
When using the [inline plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/inline/) with `style` tag to inline CSS, the plugin removes the actuall `style` tag from DOM and [creates a new one](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/inline.ts#L108).  This has the consequence that additional attributes are not considered or removed. For `script` tags this is not the case, since the actuall script tag gets just modified.

The plugin should respect additional attributes like `nonce="whatever"` and keep those. An example of this is the use of the `nonce` attribute for the [CSP](https://content-security-policy.com/examples/allow-inline-style/).
1353447163.PlainText:
Inline plugin does not respect additional style tag attributes

When using the inline plugin with style tag to inline CSS, the plugin removes the actuall style tag from DOM and creates a new one.  This has the consequence that additional attributes are not considered or removed. For script tags this is not the case, since the actuall script tag gets just modified.
The plugin should respect additional attributes like nonce="whatever" and keep those. An example of this is the use of the nonce attribute for the CSP.

Comments.Body:
`link` and `style` elements are of different types, so they cannot share all attributes. But I'm agree that some attributes like `nonce`, `id` or `class` must be keept.

By the way, I just realized that the attribute `media` is not taken into account. The expected behavior is the css code must be wrapped by a `@media` rule. For example:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" media="print" href="print.css" inline />
```
should become to:
```html
<style>
@media print {
...
}
</style>
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1359020574.Title: `search.pages()` and `search.page()` should return the `data` object instead of the full `Page` instance.
1359020574.Author: oscarotero
1359020574.Body:
This makes working with pages easier. For example, instead of this: ```njk {% for article in search.pages("type=article", "date=desc") %}

{{ article.data.title }}

 {% endfor %} ``` It would become to this: ```njk {% for article in search.pages("type=article", "date=desc") %}

{{ article.title }}

 {% endfor %} ``` If it's necesary to access to the `Page` instance (to get the `src` data, for example) it's available at `data.page`. Example: ```njk {% for article in search.pages("type=article", "date=desc") %}

{{ article.title }}

Loaded from {{ article.page.src.path }} {% endfor %} ``` Due this is a breaking change, it will be in Lume 2.0.

Due this is a breaking change, it will be in Lume 2.0.

Comments.Body:
I like this but aren't the functions `search.pages()` and `search.page()` signalling that the return type would be a `Page` (at least from naming perspective)? 

For data, I'd say, there should be `search.data()` which can return `Array<Data> | Data` depending on filter. If we choose to add `search.data()`, we can bring it in 1.x by deprecating `search.pages()` and `search.page()`.
Comments.Author: naiyerasif

Comments.Body:
`search.data()` already exits and returns the data associated to a file or directory. For example `search.data("/")` returns the data associated to the root directory (the _data files/folders), and `search.data("/post/first-post.md")` the data associated to this specific page. In Lume `1.11`, I've added the `data` filter as a temporary solution, so it's possible to do the following: ```njk {% for article in search.pages("type=article", "date=desc") | data %}

{{ article.title }}

Loaded from {{ article.page.src.path }} {% endfor %} ``` But I think the `Page` instance is something internal, a implementation detail that users working with templates shouldn't need to know.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Might it be possible to enable this behaviour in v1.0 via an option in the plugin’s config?
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body: Yes, it's a good idea.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Implemented in v2 branch https://github.com/lumeland/lume/pull/446
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Events.Author: oscarotero
Events.RenamedFrom: `search.pages()` and `search.page()` should return the `data` object instead of the full page object.
Events.RenamedTo: `search.pages()` and `search.page()` should return the `data` object instead of the full `Page` instance.

1372081303.Title: filterPages plugin
1372081303.Author: oscarotero
1372081303.Body:
A simple plugin to filter pages. It allows to ignore some pages, for example ignore `draft` pages in "prod" environment:

```ts
site.use(filterPages(
  (page) => !page.data.draft || Deno.env.get("ENV") !== "prod"
));
```

This is useful once the `dev` mode is deprecated and removed (#244).

Other option is include this functionality built-in in the Site instance, without need to import and use a plugin, example:

```ts
site.filterPages(
  (page) => !page.data.draft || Deno.env.get("ENV") !== "prod"
);
```
But not sure if this can be confused with the `site.ignore` function, very similar but it works at path level (before the page is loaded, so it can't filter by page data).

1379650502.Title: Use minimum system permissions necessary
1379650502.Author: cmoog
1379650502.Body:
I noticed that the default dev script invokes itself with full permissions over the entire system. Ideally, Lume should function fine if invoked with the following minimum permissions.

```console
$ deno run --allow-net=localhost:3000 --allow-read=./ --allow-write=./_site lume/task.ts
```
1379650502.PlainText:
Use minimum system permissions necessary

I noticed that the default dev script invokes itself with full permissions over the entire system. Ideally, Lume should function fine if invoked with the following minimum permissions.

Comments.Body:
It's not so easy, for several reasons:

- This depends on the site configuration. For example, if the site is configured to use a different `dest` folder or if the server port is different (like `8080` instead of `3000`), the command should change also the permissions configuration.
- `deno run lume/task.ts` doesn't work, because the `lume/task.ts` file is not resolved with the import map, so you have to include the full file url: `deno run https://deno.land/x/lume@1.11.4/task.ts`. But this introduce another url to maintain and keep up to date on update Lume, in addition to the Lume url in the import_map.json file. This is why the lume task is `deno eval "import 'lume/task.ts'"` because `deno eval` takes the import_map.json file into account to resolve the imports, making this file a single source of truth. More info about `deno run` and `deno eval` here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14760
- `deno eval` has implicit all permissions (it's like `--allow-all`).
- `lume/task.ts` file just run https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/ci.ts and this file run the real deno command to run Lume with all required parameters. More info: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/ci.ts#L107-L115

I'd like to simplify this in the near future, when this proposal is implemented: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12763 so you can configure the permissions in the Deno config file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> This depends on the site configuration.

Point taken. Then the minimum permissions for most projects would be `--allow-net=localhost --allow-read=./ --allow-write=./`. If some plugin needs to make other network requests, the user should need to explicitly allow that.

> deno eval has implicit all permissions (it's like --allow-all).

After reading [this issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14760), I can't help but feel the current behavior is a big mistake. If my understanding is correct, the lack of support for import maps in `deno run` means the only ergonomic way to invoke "framework" CLIs is without any sandboxing. This erases a major benefit of Deno's security model.

And to preempt, I don't think this is bikeshedding. Given that Lume is plugin-based, it's plausible that there will be a long tail of unaudited, untrusted plugins. Why should plugins have exec permissions?

### Potential (temporary) solution

I haven't thought about this too much... but this came across my mind as a potential solution. This way, only one line of code runs with global permissions, then the `task.ts` process itself is sandboxed.

```json
{
  "importMap": "import_map.json",
  "tasks": {
    "lume": "deno run --allow-read=./ --allow-write=./ --allow-net=localhost $(deno eval \"console.log(JSON.parse(Deno.readTextFileSync('import_map.json')).imports['lume/'])\")task.ts",
    "build": "deno task lume",
    "serve": "deno task lume -s"
  }
}
```

Of course this would also require changes to `ci.ts` and `task.ts` to remove the unnecessary self-invocation.
Comments.Author: cmoog

Comments.Body:
`task.ts` and `ci.ts` works as wrappers to execute the real CLI script (`cli.ts`). This wrapper [fixes different issues](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/ci.ts#L12-L19). So, restricting permissions to these files has no effect to `cli.ts`(AFAIK, permissions are not propagated between childprocesses: a file with only `--allow-run` could run a script passing the `--allow-all` argument).

The use cases are so variable that this is the reason I've decided to run `cli.ts` with `--allow-all`, for example:
- Sites that fetch data from an external API, needs `allow-net` or `allow-net=api-url`.
- Sites that have different configurations depending on the environment variables (like `ENV=dev`) needs `--allow-env`.
- Sites that need to run some scripts during the build needs `--allow-run`
- Sites that use some binary libraries connected with ffi needs `--allow-ffi`.
- Sites that use WebAssembly libraries needs `--allow-read` to read the WebAssembly file.

Restricting the permissions right now can break many sites. I think it's better to let the users configure the permisisions once Deno team implements permission configuration in the deno.json file. In Lume 2.0 I'm planning to remove these wrapper files and depend only on deno.json that will be mandatory (unlike now, that both deno.json and import_map.json files are optional).

If you're concerned about that, the correct way to apply permissions is by running the `cli.ts` file directly. You can create a task like this:

```json
{
  "importMap": "import_map.json",
  "tasks": {
    "lume": "deno run --unstable --allow-read=./ --allow-write=./ --allow-net=localhost https://lume.land/x/lume@1.11.4/cli.ts",
    "build": "deno task lume",
    "serve": "deno task lume -s"
  }
}
```

This conversation makes me think that a Permissions section in the documentation website would be really useful for users that want to restrict these permissions.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
>This conversation makes me think that a Permissions section in the documentation website would be really useful for users that want to restrict these permissions.

This sounds like a great idea! It will also be useful for raising awareness of permissions considerations among users who wouldn't otherwise be thinking about it.

I don't think I know enough about Deno's permissions API or Lume's requirements to write this myself, but I'd be very happy to help with writing this documentation if anyone feels like collaborating on it.
Comments.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body:
Permissions are explained here: https://lume.land/docs/advanced/permissions/
Closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1388021659.Title: Imagick plugin: Allow `format` property to accept an array
1388021659.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1388021659.Body:
Currently, `format` only allows a single value. If you want to output multiple formats, you need to declare separate transformations

```yml
imagick:
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format: jpg
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format: webp
```

To simplify the configuration of different image transformations, it would be helpful if the `format` property could accept an array. This would allow the above to be rewritten as follows:

```yml
imagick:
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format:
      - jpg
      - webp
```

To simplify the configuration of different image transformations, it would be helpful if the format property could accept an array. This would allow the above to be rewritten as follows:

1389774758.Title: Allow other ISO 8601 date representations in `date` field
1389774758.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1389774758.Body:
Pages can only include dates in the following formats:

* `2021-01-01`
* `2021-01-01 03:10:10`
* `2021-01-01 03:10:10 -0700`

This appears to follow RFC 3339. However, IS0 8601 dates typically don’t include spaces, but instead use `T` to separate the date and time. For example, the above can also be represented as:

* `2021-01-01`
* `2021-01-01T03:10:10Z`
* `2021-01-01Y03:10:10-0700`

Support for this representation in Lume would be useful as it would make migrating pages from an Eleventy-powered blog easier. For reference, Eleventy supports thew following [date formats](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/dates/):

* any valid [YAML date value](https://yaml.org/type/timestamp.html)
* any valid ISO 8601 string that [Luxon’s DateTime.fromISO](https://moment.github.io/luxon/#/parsing?id=iso-8601) can parse

It should be noted that Luke’s date format filters support both of the above representations (`ATOM` and `DATETIME`).

ISO 8601 dates also don’t need to include `-` or `:` separators, so the following dates are also valid:

* `20210101`
* `20210101T031010Z`
* `20210101Y031010-0700`

There may be less need to support this, although I’be come across it on my own site where I’m parsing iCalendar data which formats dates using this representation. As I can’t use them directly when generating pages, I need to manipulate them to the format Lume allows.
1389774758.PlainText:
Allow other ISO 8601 date representations in date field

Pages can only include dates in the following formats:
2021-01-01
2021-01-01 03:10:10
2021-01-01 03:10:10 -0700
This appears to follow RFC 3339. However, IS0 8601 dates typically don’t include spaces, but instead use T to separate the date and time. For example, the above can also be represented as:
2021-01-01
2021-01-01T03:10:10Z
2021-01-01Y03:10:10-0700
Support for this representation in Lume would be useful as it would make migrating pages from an Eleventy-powered blog easier. For reference, Eleventy supports thew following date formats:
any valid YAML date value
any valid ISO 8601 string that Luxon’s DateTime.fromISO can parse
It should be noted that Luke’s date format filters support both of the above representations (ATOM and DATETIME).
ISO 8601 dates also don’t need to include - or : separators, so the following dates are also valid:
20210101
20210101T031010Z
20210101Y031010-0700
There may be less need to support this, although I’be come across it on my own site where I’m parsing iCalendar data which formats dates using this representation. As I can’t use them directly when generating pages, I need to manipulate them to the format Lume allows.

Comments.Body:
Deno' std modules include some datetime utils, like this parser; https://deno.land/std@0.157.0/datetime/mod.ts?s=parse but it doesn't auto-detect the format. Maybe the [date-fns](https://deno.land/x/date_fns@v2.15.0) library (a already used dependency) can handle these formats.

I like also the git related dates of Eleventy. It's something that @dz4k implemented in his site: https://github.com/dz4k/denizaksimsek.com/blob/master/_build/get-dates-from-git.ts
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1389800113.Title: Connect source-maps with source-files
1389800113.Author: binyamin
1389800113.Body:
### Summary
When **plugin a** creates a source-map `Page` for a given file, **plugin b** should have access to the same source-map `Page` via the given file.

### Scenario
1. User writes a file `input.scss`
2. The `sass` plugin transforms it to CSS, and adds a source-map
3. The `lightningcss` plugin minifies the CSS, and updates the source-map
4. Source-map is accurate!

### Solution
- Store the source-map on `Page` as either JSON or a string.
    - This would mean checking for a source-map when writing any file, and dealing with it accordingly.
    - This comes with the complexity of whether to write it inline or externally, and whether to add a "source-mapping URL" comment to the source's content.
- The source file's `Page` instance has a property which references a source-map `Page` instance.
    - It only needs to be one-way.
    - It should probably be a getter/setter, so we can validate it, and so it won't get in the way when debugging.
1389800113.PlainText:
Connect source-maps with source-files

Summary
When plugin a creates a source-map Page for a given file, plugin b should have access to the same source-map Page via the given file.
Scenario
User writes a file input.scss
The sass plugin transforms it to CSS, and adds a source-map
The lightningcss plugin minifies the CSS, and updates the source-map
Source-map is accurate!
Solution
Store the source-map on Page as either JSON or a string.
This would mean checking for a source-map when writing any file, and dealing with it accordingly.
This comes with the complexity of whether to write it inline or externally, and whether to add a "source-mapping URL" comment to the source's content.
The source file's Page instance has a property which references a source-map Page instance.
It only needs to be one-way.
It should probably be a getter/setter, so we can validate it, and so it won't get in the way when debugging.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Agreed, the 2nd solution sounds better that the 1st.

I'm not sure whether the 3rd solution is useful. If the source-map is inline, then it is included with the content. Any further plugins that need the source-map will also be accessing the content, so they'll have what they need. For example, LightningCSS just reads any inline source-map, and parses it automatically.

Also, I just realized that the 2nd and 3rd solutions would break once a plugin changes the name of the source file.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
> Also, I just realized that the 2nd and 3rd solutions would break once a plugin changes the name of the source file.

What source file do you mean? For example, if the `styles.scss` is converted to `styles.css`, with a source map, but then minified by other plugin and renamed to `styles.min.css` but the source map is not updated accordingly?

Another solution, more inline with your 1st solution is creating a plugin to generate source maps. Something like this:

```ts
site.use(sass())
  .use(postcss())
  .use(lightningcss())
  .use(sourceMaps());
```

The three first plugins would consume and save the source map in the `page.data.sourceMap` variable, and the last plugin would generate the source map page (or inline the code, depending on the configuration).

This would remove the `sourceMap` option that is already available for the first plugins. The source map will be generated always and stored in this variable. And from this unique plugin you can configure what do you want to do with it (css and javascript).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@binyamin I have created a branch ([feat/source-map](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/tree/feat/source-map)) to test the idea the sourceMaps plugin. You can see it in this commit: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b

It's implemented in sass, esbuild, terser, postcss and lightningcss. The idea:
- All plugins can read the previous source map from `page.data.sourceMap` and replace this value with the new source map.
- I've created a helper to normalize the sourceMap urls and ensure all plugins generate the source maps in the same way: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b/core/utils.ts#L273-L302
- The new `source_maps` plugin outputs the source map of all pages containing the `sourceMap` data: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b/plugins/source_maps.ts
- This plugin can be configured to inline the code, generate an external file, include the source content, etc.

I've tested and seems to work fine. What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
After some iterations, I'm confortable with the new `source_maps` plugin and merged it to master. 
It will be included in Lume 1.12.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1391157799.Title: Unable to paginate generated pages
1391157799.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1391157799.PlainText:
Unable to paginate generated pages

I have a section of my site where I want to generate pages from a data source. I also want to generate an index page (that may then be paginated) which includes links to those generated pages. As far as I can tell, it is not possible to paginate over generated pages.
Here is a reduced test case. The page generator:
And the paginated pages generator:
In this scenario, the individual pages are generated, but not the paginated pages. Of note:
If I log search.pages("page"), an empty array is returned.
If I update search.pages() to search for a tag (or other query) for a set of non-generated pages, the paginated pages are generated.

Comments.Body:
Yes, this is because all pages are generated in parallel, so the paginator may be rendered before the other pages even exists. You can configure the render order to ensure that the paginator is executed after the other pages are generated.
See this: https://lume.land/docs/core/render-order/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah, I missed this. Works like a charm – thanks!
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

1393570858.Title: Include file slug value in `Page.src` object
1393570858.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1393570858.Body:
Lume does some calculations behind the scenes to determine information about the source of a generated page. For example, given a file saved at `/articles/2022-08-29_porto.md`, Lume provides the following data in `Page.src`:

```js
{
  path: "/articles/2022-08-29_porto",
  lastModified: 2022-10-01T17:46:49.762Z,
  created: 2022-10-01T17:16:39.480Z,
  remote: undefined,
  ext: ".md"
}
```

Lume will also add a `page.data.data` value if the base file name contains `YYYY-MM-DD_` (so for the above this would be calculated to be `2022-10-01T00:00:00.000Z`).

However, there is one other piece of information that may be useful to site authors: the file slug. In the case of the above, this value would be `porto`.

This value could be used in `url()` functions:

```js
export function url(page) {
  return `./example/${page.src.fileSlug}/`;
}
```

It could also be used by page (pre)processors. This is my use case, where I am currently having to calculate this value myself to generate a new URL.

(Eleventy provides a similar [`fileSlug`](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/data-eleventy-supplied/#fileslug) value).
1393570858.PlainText:
Include file slug value in Page.src object

Lume does some calculations behind the scenes to determine information about the source of a generated page. For example, given a file saved at /articles/2022-08-29_porto.md, Lume provides the following data in Page.src:
Lume will also add a page.data.data value if the base file name contains YYYY-MM-DD_ (so for the above this would be calculated to be 2022-10-01T00:00:00.000Z).
However, there is one other piece of information that may be useful to site authors: the file slug. In the case of the above, this value would be porto.
This value could be used in url() functions:
It could also be used by page (pre)processors. This is my use case, where I am currently having to calculate this value myself to generate a new URL.
(Eleventy provides a similar fileSlug value).

Comments.Author: oscarotero
paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> The `url()` functions are executed before the preprocessors.

Ah, of course. So to be clear, the only way to build a URL that includes a post count is using a preprocessor and using `page.updateDest()`, as I described above?

> A solution could be saving the `slug` property the first time it's calculated

Where would you do this? Any attempt to derive a slug from the original source file falls foil of the issue described above; I can’t seem to get a value for the original file slug if I’m using `page.updateDest()`.
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I’m still not sure this helps, and I think I know why. Because the URL is calculated before any preprocessor runs, and because, **with pretty URLs enabled, base names are considered to be `index`**, the destination value is no longer useful in this regard. For example:

```
page.src.path  = /articles/2022-03-10_my_example_post.md
basename(page.src.path) = 2022-03-10_my_example_post

page.dest.path = /articles/my_example_post/index.html
basename(page.dest.path) = index
```

So there’s currently no easy way to get the original file slug, without applying a regex to `page.src.path` to remove any dates. The same is true also true for page generators, whose base name includes the page index (i.e. `articles[2]`).
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks for this, that works for now (bit better than my regex!)

I do think this value should be exposed somewhere, after all it’s a user defined piece of data, much like the date in the file name.

I wouldn’t mind this value being provided as a default value for `page.data.slug`, or even `page.src.path`, but you have a better idea of the overall architecture and project direction.
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body: This will be available in Lume 1.13.0. Closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1393603235.Title: Imagick plugin: Option to filter transformations based on source file information
1393603235.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1393603235.PlainText:
Imagick plugin: Option to filter transformations based on source file information I have a number of images, some of which I want to perform additional or different transformations on. Right now, this isn’t possible without saving files to a different directories with different directory data. For example, for a given blog post, say I have a folder of associated images. To enable responsive images, I may want to generate different formats and/or sizes for each of these images. However, one of these images is also the ‘key’ image. For this image, I also want to: Generate a cropped 1200×630 JPEG, with suffix -summary, to use for the post’s Open Graph image Generate cropped 600×400 JPEG and WEBP images to use on the articles index page To achieve this, I either need to move/copy this ‘key’ image to a different folder (possible, but inflexible) or perform transformations on all the images in the same directory – an expensive file operation that also increases the number and size of files needing to be managed during deployment. One possible way to achieve the above would be to provide a matching mechanism in the imagick data, under a new key called matches1: I’m not sure what the right syntax for determine matches would be, and it’s hard to envisage it would be able to cover all possible use cases. Perhaps a better idea would be to allow matches to accept a function (meaning you’d need to use a JavaScript data file): Alternatively, maybe filters can query file information about the image:
  1. matches is the first name that came to mind. I thought about filter, but this might get confused with graphic image filters.↩

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've implemented `matches` which accepts a `RegEx` or a `string` (that is used to create a RegEx instance).
Now, you can do:

```yml
imagick:
  - suffix: -summary
    matches: -main.jpg
    resizeCrop: [1200, 630]
    format: jpg
```

It's included in the coming Lume 1.12.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Amazing!! Thank-you so much for the quick updates. Excited to try out the next version 😄
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

1396922305.Title: Option to quieten console output
1396922305.Author: paulrobertlloyd
1396922305.Body:
Sometimes it’s necessary to log output to the console. Sometimes however messages can get missed if lots of pages/images are generated, and there are multiple rows of 🔥 and 🎨!

It would be helpful to have an option to quieten the CLI output, and not display which items have been generated. Perhaps there’s an option where you could set the level (info, warning, error, etc.), which would give site authors more control of what’s shown here.

Sometimes it’s necessary to log output to the console. Sometimes however messages can get missed if lots of pages/images are generated, and there are multiple rows of 🔥 and 🎨!
It would be helpful to have an option to quieten the CLI output, and not display which items have been generated. Perhaps there’s an option where you could set the level (info, warning, error, etc.), which would give site authors more control of what’s shown here.

1397621789.Title: Processed image files and filename parsing for dates
1397621789.Author: kari
1397621789.PlainText:
Processed image files and filename parsing for dates

I noticed that when using imagick plugin, because it "processes" the image files, these files are apparently treated as pages? Anyway they seem to be subject to Lume's "page date" rules. I had images named like 14455_full.jpg that were outputted as full.jpg. This was a bit unexpected behavior.
My current workaround is to have _data.ts file in my images folder
Obviously another workaround is to rename input image files so that they don't match with Lume's date parsing algorithm...
Is there are better way to override Lume's page date parsing? Is this date parsing intended behavior for all kinds of processed files (especially non-text ones)?

Comments.Body:
Yes, they are loaded as pages, but not regular pages, but asset pages (See the difference here: https://lume.land/docs/core/concepts/#page-files).

But I agree that the date extracting from the file name makes no sense in the asset pages, I'll fix this in the next version. Meanwhile, your workaround is fine.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
After some tests, I think that the best solution is remove the interpretation of files starting with a number + `_` as a date. It doesn't make sense and it's a too generical pattern that can conflict with many files.
Due it's a kind of breaking change I re-schedule this change to 1.13.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed. It will be available in the next version 1.13.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1402432784.Title: When serving, asset paths are not reset after modifying with updateDest
1402432784.Author: valpackett
1402432784.Body:
When running a dev server, something like this:

```js
let cnt = 1

site
  .loadAssets([".css"])
  .process([".css"], (page) => {
    cnt += 1
    page.updateDest({ path: page.dest.path + `.${cnt}` })
  })
```

keeps modifying *the same* path string, building up new additions all the time, i.e. like `s.2.3.4.5.css`:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/208340/194784399-94f75dbe-bb3a-4d55-b6bd-68edab9e3b5a.png)

This is not the expected behavior :)

(version 1.12.0)

Comments.Body:

I have to think in a way to reset the page data to prevent this incremental changes, without affecting to the performance.

But this may take a while. For now you have to replace the old value with the new.

1403687725.Title: Remove `page.dest`.
1403687725.Author: oscarotero
1403687725.Body:
Currently, the `Page` class has the `dest` property with info about the destination.

The `dest` value must be in sync with the `url` value. For example, a page with the url `/foo/` will have the following dest:

```js
{
  path: "/foo/index",
  ext: ".html"
}
```

This is why we have the `page.updateDest()` function, that change not only the `dest` value of the page but also regenerate the url  accordingly.

The problem is this only work by changing the `dest` property, but not the `url`. If the `url` of a page is changed directly, the `dest` keeps the old value.

I think `dest` is a redundant value. It should be calculated only at saving time, according to the page's `url`. So we have a single source of truth that processors and preprocessors can change at any time.
1403687725.PlainText:
Remove page.dest.

Currently, the Page class has the dest property with info about the destination.
The dest value must be in sync with the url value. For example, a page with the url /foo/ will have the following dest:
This is why we have the page.updateDest() function, that change not only the dest value of the page but also regenerate the url  accordingly.
The problem is this only work by changing the dest property, but not the url. If the url of a page is changed directly, the dest keeps the old value.
I think dest is a redundant value. It should be calculated only at saving time, according to the page's url. So we have a single source of truth that processors and preprocessors can change at any time.

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero How about the other way around? Meaning, if `page.data.url` is never stored, only calculated. Maybe with a "getter" which references `data.dest`.

My reasoning is twofold:
1. Lume's primary purpose is to generate a website, and write it to disk. It _can_ be used as a server, but it's really a static-site _generator_.
2. It sounds easier to tell plugins to edit `page.dest`, which is always either a `string` or `false`. If plugins try editing `page.data.url`, they might get a function by accident, since the user can specify a function for `page.data.url`.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
It's more difficult and probably confusing for the users.

- People are used to define the `url` variable and use this variable in the templates. `page.dest` is an internal value.
- Getters for dynamic values are problematic in some template engines (like nunjucks). 
- The functions of the `url` values are invoked very early, plugins never get the function.

I'm aware of the multitype problem of `url`. Ideally it should allow only `string` and maybe allowing `false` was a bad idea. I'm thinking of removing this in Lume 2.0 and use a plugin or something to filter the pages that you don't want to export.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Done in v2 branch
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1409979836.Title: Use `deno run` instead of `deno eval` in deno.json
1409979836.Author: DrSensor
1409979836.Body:
I just notice `deno run` support evaluating from stdin. So I think it's better to use it because you can write the lock file and reload the dependencies. For example:
```sh
echo "import 'lume/task.ts'" | deno run --reload --lock=lock.json --lock-write -
```
> **Note** there is a `-` in the end of line

This is better since `deno eval` doesn't support `--lock`/`--lock-write` and `--reload` flag. Some people (including me) might want to keep the lock file to comply with [reproducible build](https://reproducible-builds.org/) philosophy without vendoring the whole dependencies.

Here is my deno.json
```json
{
  "importMap": "build.importmap",
  "tasks": {
    "lume": "echo \"import 'lume/task.ts'\" | deno run --unstable --allow-read --allow-run --lock=lock.json -",
    "update": "deno cache --unstable --lock=lock.json --lock-write --reload _config.ts && echo \"import 'lume/task.ts'\" | deno run --unstable --allow-read --allow-run --lock=lock.json --lock-write --reload - --version"
  }
}
```
1409979836.PlainText:
Use deno run instead of deno eval in deno.json

I just notice deno run support evaluating from stdin. So I think it's better to use it because you can write the lock file and reload the dependencies. For example:
Note there is a - in the end of line
This is better since deno eval doesn't support --lock/--lock-write and --reload flag. Some people (including me) might want to keep the lock file to comply with reproducible build philosophy without vendoring the whole dependencies.
Here is my deno.json

Comments.Body:
Oh, that's fantastic! I didn't know this was possible. It also allows to configure the permissions (`deno eval` has all permissions implicitly).
I'll change it in the next version. Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1420743598.Title: Error when using React hooks with JSX plugin
1420743598.Author: JanKaul
1420743598.PlainText:
Error when using React hooks with JSX plugin

Expected behavior
Being able to use React hooks in React components for creating a static site with Lume.
Component:
Config:
Actual behavior

Steps to reproduce

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
alternatively, you can create [Lume components](https://lume.land/docs/core/components/):

- Store your component in `_components/index.tsx` and export it as default.
- In your pages, you have the `comp` variable with all components found:
```tsx
export default function ({ comp }) {
  return <comp.Index />
}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> It looks like some React internals doesn't work in this way. This works fine:
> 
> change `index.tsx` to this:
> 
> ```tsx
> import { Index } from "./src/components/index.tsx";
> 
> export default function () {
>   return <Index />
> }
> ```
> 
> The `index` component doesn't return anything. You need to add a `return`.

Ah great. That fixed the issue. Thank you.
The return somehow got lost when I tried to make a small example.

1429644034.Title: Modification of the translation system
1429644034.Author: AugustinMauroy
1429644034.Body:
# how it is now

https://lume.land/plugins/multilanguage/

# how it could be

> This change would make the work easier for the translators.

What I propose is simply that we have a `json` that contains the list of languages ex:`En, Fr, Es, De` and the date format. This file would be the config of the translation. Then to translate a content file you have to create a file adding the language ex:`about.en.md` => `about.fr.md`.

# Crowdin

with this relatively simple system we could add a crowdin integration.

with this relatively simple system we could add a crowdin integration.

1437167797.Title: Unable to set absolute path for includes
1437167797.Author: ggodlewski
1437167797.PlainText:
Unable to set absolute path for includes

Version
1.12.1
Platform
Linux
What steps will reproduce the bug?
I want to keep includes outside of content directory using absolute path.
My config is following:
I'm getting error:
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
Always
What is the expected behavior?
Do not concatenate includes path with content path if it starts with /
What do you see instead?
I want to be able to set any path in my filesystem for the includes/theme
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body:
This is not a bug but the expected behavior. Everything in Lume must reside in the `src` folder.
If you want to load files from different places, you can configure remote files. For example:

```ts
site.remoteFile("_includes/layout.njk", import.meta.resolve("./theme/layout.njk"));
```

More info here: https://lume.land/docs/core/remote-files/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero 
I really hope lume can have more freedom in path configuration.
I can't use a structure like this in lume:
```
├── _config.ts
├── deno.json
└── src
    ├── layouts
    │   ├── base.njk
    │   └── index.njk
    └── pages
        └── index.md
```
Comments.Author: shuaixr

Comments.Body: @shuaixr What's the problem with this structure? You can configure Lume to use the `src` folder.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1449203963.Title: nunjucks template-inheritance/block not working as intended
1449203963.Author: angelside
1449203963.Body:
### Version

1.12.1

### Platform

Arch Linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

```shell
~ git clone https://github.com/angelside/lume-block-test.git
~ deno task serve
```

### What do you see instead?

```
Caused by Template render error: (/home/angelside/code/static/lume-block-test/index.njk)
  Error: no super block available for "breadcrumb"
```

### Additional information

Block and super structure not working with default frontmatter layout definition. 

But, it's working if replacing frontmatter with classic nunjucks extends _(Which is not the desired method, I like to use single data.yml file for layout)_.

```
---
layout: layout.njk
---
```

```
{% extends "_includes/layout.njk" %}
```

Docs: https://mozilla.github.io/nunjucks/templating.html#template-inheritance
Minimal example: https://github.com/angelside/lume-block-test

-------

layout.njk

```njk
<div class="breadcrumb">
    {% block breadcrumb %}
        block in base
    {% endblock %}
<div>
```

index.njk

```njk
{% block breadcrumb %}
    {{ super() }}
    block in index
{% endblock %}
```

1452554473.Title: Replace Page.isHtml with Page.contentType
1452554473.Author: Protonull
1452554473.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Just saw the [changelog](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/v1.13.0/CHANGELOG.md#1130---2022-11-16) which included the addition of `Page.isHtml`, which is great, but I wonder if a broader is-function might be more useful. Lume's docs mention the possibility of dynamically generated assets [here](https://lume.land/docs/core/processors/#create-or-remove-pages-dynamically), and those assets could be blobs like PDFs of other files, or text files, indeed like the example'd Javascript-map file. But what if it's an XML file like an RSS feed? Would that appear to Lume as a HTML file? Would that therefore impact how that file is rendered? I'm not sure tbh, but I do think either way there's enough of a reason to allow for more exhaustive check. You could even keep the `Page.isHtml` by having its value set via `Page.isHtml = Page.contentType === "text/html";` You could check the prefix of the content-type to know how to save the file: if the type is prefixed with `text/` then you know that you can safely use `Deno.writeTextFile()` instead of another method.
1452554473.PlainText:
Replace Page.isHtml with Page.contentType

Enter your suggestions in details:
Just saw the changelog which included the addition of Page.isHtml, which is great, but I wonder if a broader is-function might be more useful. Lume's docs mention the possibility of dynamically generated assets here, and those assets could be blobs like PDFs of other files, or text files, indeed like the example'd Javascript-map file. But what if it's an XML file like an RSS feed? Would that appear to Lume as a HTML file? Would that therefore impact how that file is rendered? I'm not sure tbh, but I do think either way there's enough of a reason to allow for more exhaustive check. You could even keep the Page.isHtml by having its value set via Page.isHtml = Page.contentType === "text/html"; You could check the prefix of the content-type to know how to save the file: if the type is prefixed with text/ then you know that you can safely use Deno.writeTextFile() instead of another method.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero What about markdown and nunjucks files, which _become_ HTML? I found this property very useful.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
@binyamin Pages have two properties:
- `page.src`: Info about the source file (`page.src.ext` returns the extension, like `.md`, `.njk`, etc).
- `page.outputPath`: The output path of the page. For example `/about-me/index.html` (with prettyUrls enabled) or `/about-me.html` with prettyUrls disabled.

So, if you want to detect if a page will become to HTML, just do `page.outputPath.endsWith(".html")`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Deno deploy doesn't support `npm` imports.
Why do you want to run Lume in Deno Deploy? The recomended way to deploy static sites to Deno Deploy is by using the GitHub Actions. More info here: https://lume.land/docs/advanced/deployment/#deno-deploy
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That is exacly how i started, and the github action throws the exact same error.. That was my reason to roll with deno deploy, so i could capture the complete log output. The strange thing is, is that my initial deployment worked fine.... My reason for deploying is to better understand the on_demand plugin... My working deployment: https://willem-haring-lume.deno.dev/
Comments.Author: willem-haring

Comments.Body:
Ok, understood. The `on_demand` plugin doesn't work in Deno Deploy because Lume now uses `npm:` imports which aren't supported yet in Deno Deploy. I guess it's matter of time they implement this (issue: https://github.com/denoland/deploy_feedback/issues/314).

Meanwhile, you can use the version `1.11.x` (the last version without npm imports) that should work fine). You have a test here: https://github.com/lumeland/test-lume-ondemand
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks for the feedback, will close this issue then, but might be use-full for others. 

Keep up the good work!
Comments.Author: willem-haring
oscarotero

1459272498.Title: Allowing filters to use dynamic variables in Pug
1459272498.Author: pluiedev
1459272498.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Pug has [a filter system](https://pugjs.org/language/filters.html) that is currently hooked up to Lume's filters, that other template engines like Nunjucks also use. However, Pug limits filter inputs to plain text only, which is incredibly inconvenient for Lume users.

For example, the `date` filter added by [the Date plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/date/) can be used in Pug like this:
```pug
:date(pattern="HUMAN_DATETIME") 2022-11-22 7:00:30Z
```
However, this does not work with Pug variables (so something like `:date()= variable` does not work), and that is by design: 
> **Warning**
> **Filters are rendered at compile time.** This makes them fast, but it also means that they cannot support dynamic content or options.
>
> By default, compilation in the browser does not have access to JSTransformer-based filters, unless the JSTransformer modules are explicitly packed and made available through a CommonJS platform (such as Browserify or Webpack). In fact, the page you are reading right now uses Browserify to make the filters available in the browser.
>
> Templates pre-compiled on the server do not have this limitation.

As Lume precompiles templates as an SSG, I think we should lift this limitation and bring Pug in line with other template engines like Nunjucks.
1459272498.PlainText:
Allowing filters to use dynamic variables in Pug

Enter your suggestions in details:
Pug has a filter system that is currently hooked up to Lume's filters, that other template engines like Nunjucks also use. However, Pug limits filter inputs to plain text only, which is incredibly inconvenient for Lume users.
For example, the date filter added by the Date plugin can be used in Pug like this:
However, this does not work with Pug variables (so something like :date()= variable does not work), and that is by design:
Warning
Filters are rendered at compile time. This makes them fast, but it also means that they cannot support dynamic content or options.
By default, compilation in the browser does not have access to JSTransformer-based filters, unless the JSTransformer modules are explicitly packed and made available through a CommonJS platform (such as Browserify or Webpack). In fact, the page you are reading right now uses Browserify to make the filters available in the browser.
Templates pre-compiled on the server do not have this limitation.
As Lume precompiles templates as an SSG, I think we should lift this limitation and bring Pug in line with other template engines like Nunjucks.

Comments.Body:
Okay, I have to investigate the Pug API. I'm already precompiling the templates [see the code here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/bb2232e9b645a2342d1dd8ea4f7a1e566e9f1864/plugins/pug.ts#L67-L69), so it should work for dynamic variables.

Any help here is very appreciated :)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@pluiedev I've tried to implement this but without success.
Do you know how to run Pug in server side to allow to use dynamic variables?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> @pluiedev I've tried to implement this but without success. Do you know how to run Pug in server side to allow to use dynamic variables?

I don't either, sadly — it looks like Pug is kinda poorly documented in that regard. We would have to ask the Pug maintainers I guess? No clue.
Comments.Author: pluiedev

Comments.Body: If you want to try, that's fine. But the last commit to the repo was almost 2 years ago and the maintainers don't reply the issues, so it's seems the project is abandoned.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> If you want to try, that's fine. But the last commit to the repo was almost 2 years ago and the maintainers don't reply the issues, so it's seems the project is abandoned.

Mhm yeah, which is a shame — I really like the terseness that Pug provides and I don't want to give it up just yet. I'll try to look into it myself then :eyes:
Comments.Author: pluiedev
pluiedev

Comments.Body: Sound good!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Okay so I did some more digging, and I think this is impossible without substantially modifying Pug, or using filters in another manner.

Basically, Pug evaluates filters before linking and code gen — there's no way to externally bypass filter evaluation, and there's no code in code gen (as far as I can tell) that supports filters, since `pug-code-gen` actually throws an error if it manages to find an unprocessed filter in the AST. Tl;dr if we were to support dynamic filters, we have to substantially alter the Pug compiler, which is admittedly too complicated for me.

However, I think we can just avoid using the builtin filter feature, and directly use interpolated, (un-)buffered code.
Instead of:
```pug
:date(pattern="HUMAN_DATETIME")= date
```
We can write
```pug
= filters.date(date, "HUMAN_DATETIME")
```

Which IMO actually works better, but we'd have to think about what to do with filters like `md`, which works great with just plain text.
Comments.Author: pluiedev

Comments.Body:
Nice!
Is it possible to have both? For example  `filters.md(value)` and `:md`?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Nice! Is it possible to have both? For example `filters.md(value)` and `:md`?

It's possible yeah, you just have to inject filters into the page data before rendering, which makes them available to the template as a global variable. Gonna open a PR that does exactly that very soon 👍
Comments.Author: pluiedev

1460263171.Title: Use property alias for `metas` plugin
1460263171.Author: oscarotero
1460263171.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

The `metas` plugin has the `defaultPageData` option to configure default values for the `metas` properties. I think this is not convenient due two reasons:

- It cannot be configured per page because it's defined in the `_config.ts` file
- If some defaults values are defined in a `_data` file (for example, `metas.title`), then you need to do `metas.title = null` to use the default value configured.

I propose to change the plugin to implement a syntax to define aliases to existing variables. For example:

```yml
title: Hello world
description: This is my site

metas:
  title: $title
  description: $description
```

Any value starting with `$` sign would be interpreted as an alias to an existing value (in the example above, `title` and `description`. It could allow also values like `$header.title`.

This allows to have different defaults for different types of pages, using `_data` files in subfolders.

**Edit:**

Maybe the sign `~` is better, because it's what's used by the `url` plugin to refer to source files (like `~/about-me.md`):

```yml
title: Hello world
description: This is my site

metas:
  title: ~title
  description: ~description
```

Other alternative syntaxes:

```yml
title: Hello world
description: This is my site

metas:
  title: &title
  description: &description
```
(Cons: The `&` sign is a thing in Yaml).


```yml
title: Hello world
description: This is my site

metas:
  title: {{ title }}
  description: {{ description }}
```
(Const: It looks like nunjucks, people may think it's possible to use filters and other nunjucks features).

Any feedback is welcome!
1460263171.PlainText:
Use property alias for metas plugin

Enter your suggestions in details:
The metas plugin has the defaultPageData option to configure default values for the metas properties. I think this is not convenient due two reasons:
It cannot be configured per page because it's defined in the _config.ts file
If some defaults values are defined in a _data file (for example, metas.title), then you need to do metas.title = null to use the default value configured.
I propose to change the plugin to implement a syntax to define aliases to existing variables. For example:
Any value starting with $ sign would be interpreted as an alias to an existing value (in the example above, title and description. It could allow also values like $header.title.
This allows to have different defaults for different types of pages, using _data files in subfolders.
Edit:
Maybe the sign ~ is better, because it's what's used by the url plugin to refer to source files (like ~/about-me.md):

Correct me if I'm wrong, but the plugin doesn't seem to support CPTs, Custom Taxonomies (CT) and Custom (role) Users right?

In my current WP project, I created a CPT with 2 CT. Not to mention I also have plenty Custom Fields. If you plan to release the plugin now, the user scope will be limited. It not that bad if it can support wordpress.com smoothly.

Comments.Body:
The plugin is very limited as is now.
I think maybe is not a good idea because for big sites it's very slow because it depends on the WP API, HTTP connection, etc.

I think a better aproach would be to simply create a script to download the content once, save it as markdown files and then you can build the site using the downloaded content. If you need to upgrade the content, just download it again.

Probably [an archetype](https://lume.land/docs/core/archetypes/) would fit better for this use case.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Well, indeed that it is the most flexible way to do so. But I wonder if the `type: "post",` could be set manually with fallback value is "post". If possible, it would make the plugin be much more flexible. 

I'm not worry much about big sites, however, with one more field can be add manually, ten more unaware problems may arrive =))

But still, I'm kinda curious of how far I can push this one :?

1462542302.Title: Can't enable code splitting in esbuild
1462542302.Author: DrSensor
1462542302.Body:
### Version

1.31.1

### Platform

Linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Here is my `_config.ts`
```ts
import esbuild from "lume/plugins/esbuild.ts";
import minify_html from "lume/plugins/minify_html.ts";

import lume from "lume/mod.ts";

export default lume()
  .ignore("lib")
  .use(esbuild({
    options: {
      splitting: true,
      mangleProps: /^_/,
    },
  }))
  .use(minify_html());
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

When `splitting` option enabled in esbuild

### What is the expected behavior?

For example, I have this project structure:
```
lib
└── utils.ts

elements
├── render-scope.ts
└── page-link.ts

index.html
```
where both `render-scope.ts` and `page-link.ts` depend on `lib/utils.ts`.

If I run it with esbuild CLI, the result would look like this:
```console
$ esbuild elements/*.ts --bundle --splitting --outdir=_site/elements --format=esm

Comments.Body:
> I had similar issues updating NPM packages. I think it's because Deno caches the npm package internally. You can remove the Deno cache directory (`deno info`) or run the Lume task with the `--reload` flag:
> 
> ```
> {
>   "importMap": "./import_map.json",
>   "tasks": {
>     "lume": "echo \"import 'lume/cli.ts'\" | deno run --reload --unstable -A -",
>     "build": "deno task lume",
>     "serve": "deno task lume -s"
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> Once you run it with `--reload`, you can remove the flag because the new package is cached.

Fixed...
Comments.Author: mskian

yes because of `deno.lock` file
deno `1.28` Generate a Lock File Now

1504869208.Title: Allow to define data with `site.data()` to a folder/file
1504869208.Author: oscarotero
1504869208.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

`site.data()` is a function to assign a shared data to the site. For example:

```js
site.data("site_name", "My Blog");
```

This assign the variable `site_name` to all pages with the value "My Blog". It is similar to include this variable in a `_data.yml` file in the project root.

The idea is to be able to assign data only to a directory or even a page. For example:

```js
site.data("site_name", "My Blog"); // Assign data to the whole site
site.data("site_name", "My Gallery", "/photos"); // Assign data to the "/photos" folder
site.data("site_name", "My Photo", "/photos/photo1.md"); // Assign data to the "/photos/photo1.md" file
```

Allow to define data with site.data() to a folder/file

1505689262.Title: Fresh lume site fails: "could not find npm package markdown-it-attrs"
1505689262.Author: Sam-Spencer
1505689262.Body:
### Version

1.14.2

### Platform

macOS

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

 1. Install the latest stable version of node (18.12.1)
 2. Create an empty directory
 3. Follow the instructions to get started with Luke **exactly** as they are provided in the docs
 4. Use a typescript config
 5. Do not add any plugins
 6. Create your first page: `index.md` (again, as stated in the docs)
 7. Run `deno task serve` (as per the docs)
 8. The following error prints in the terminal:

> error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Every time

### What is the expected behavior?

Should be able to initialize a new lime site by following the provided instructions

### What do you see instead?

There is an ambiguous npm package error:

> error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload

Interestingly, that specific version of that specific npm package *does* exist and was published 10 days ago (at time of writing).

### Additional information

_No response_
1505689262.PlainText:
Fresh lume site fails: "could not find npm package markdown-it-attrs"

Version
1.14.2
Platform
macOS
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Install the latest stable version of node (18.12.1)
Create an empty directory
Follow the instructions to get started with Luke exactly as they are provided in the docs
Use a typescript config
Do not add any plugins
Create your first page: index.md (again, as stated in the docs)
Run deno task serve (as per the docs)
The following error prints in the terminal:
error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
Every time
What is the expected behavior?
Should be able to initialize a new lime site by following the provided instructions
What do you see instead?
There is an ambiguous npm package error:
error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload
Interestingly, that specific version of that specific npm package does exist and was published 10 days ago (at time of writing).
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body:
Hi, @Sam-Spencer 
This is a bug from Deno, related with how they cache the NPM packages. (See https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16901)

There are two possible solutions:

- Purge the Deno's cache folder (run `deno info` to get the cache path)
- Or run `echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run --unstable -A --reload -` (The same command of `deno task lume` but with the `--reload` flag). This will refresh the Deno's cache.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The following  ` echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run --unstable -A --reload -` command does not work for me, then I run the following command, it will be fine for me.

```bash
 deno cache --reload ./_config.ts 
```

Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body:
Workaround.

Have a `_data.ts` file with:

```javascript
export const cacheBuster = `${new Date().getTime()}`;
```

Then, in your template, you can do something like.

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="/style.css?cb={{ cacheBuster }}">
```

It doesn't solve every use-case but it's just right for styles and suchlike.

1527842518.Title: Access rendered content from outer pages
1527842518.Author: valpackett
1527842518.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Inside the page layout, `{{ content }}` is the rendered content, already processed with all the `templateEngine`s.

Within `search.pages` results, there is only entirely unprocessed content, and one has to basically redo the entire pipeline manually. And I can't even get the `njk` filter—maybe due to `{% import %}` stuff in the content—to work in that context.

It would be great if the rendered content was cached and available for access in pages with higher `renderOrder`.
1527842518.PlainText:
Access rendered content from outer pages

Enter your suggestions in details:
Inside the page layout, {{ content }} is the rendered content, already processed with all the templateEngines.
Within search.pages results, there is only entirely unprocessed content, and one has to basically redo the entire pipeline manually. And I can't even get the njk filter—maybe due to {% import %} stuff in the content—to work in that context.
It would be great if the rendered content was cached and available for access in pages with higher renderOrder.

Comments.Body:
Hi 👋 

> And I can't even get the njk filter—maybe due to {% import %} stuff in the content—to work in that context.

I don't understand this. The njk filter should be available always, for example:

```
{{ content | njk }}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I mean, using it just quietly ruins the output in a weird way, even cutting off a lot of things after the usage tag. Templates: ```html {% macro preview(post) -%}

  • {{ post.title }}

    {{ post.date | date('HUMAN_DATE') }} ✦ {{ post.content | md | striptags | reading_time }} min read

    {{ post.desc }} ✦ read more →

    • {%- endmacro %} ``` ```html {% import "blog.njk" as blog %}

    Blog

      {%- for post in search.pages("blog=true", "date=desc") | data %} {{ blog.preview(post) }} {%- endfor %}
    ``` Output: ```html

    • Yet another keyboard post, or, introducing ErgoNICE

      January 9th, 2023 ✦ 20 min read

      I made a custom split mechanical keyboard! Because it's me, this involved things like contributing to a PCB design tool and discovering a ridiculously optimized way to read a keyboard matrix. ✦ read more →

    • FreeBSD and custom firmware on the Google Pixelbook

      October 5th, 2019 ✦ 15 min read

      A search for a new thin-and-light laptop, a journey through the Chromebook firmware trust architecture, some FreeBSD kernel development, and finally, something about actually customizing open source firmware. ✦ read more →

      • ..... ``` Now if we change to `{{ post.content | njk | md | striptags | reading_time }}` or `{{ post.content | njk(post) | md | striptags | reading_time }}` or `{{ post.content | njk }}` or `{{ post.content | njk(post) }}`: ```html

      • Yet another keyboard post, or, introducing ErgoNICE

        January 9th, 2023 ✦

      • FreeBSD and custom firmware on the Google Pixelbook

        October 5th, 2019 ✦

      • ``` The full source repo for the site is [here](https://codeberg.org/valpackett/val.packett.cool).
Comments.Author: valpackett
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ohh, that's so clever! I hadn't thought to use a js layout for that. Thanks!!
Comments.Author: valpackett

Comments.Body:
Yeah, it's not designed for that, but it's a nice way to edit the page data dynamically from the layouts :)

BTW, I just saw your site and it's great. Do you mind if I include it in the [Showcase section](https://lume.land/showcase/)?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: oh please do include it :3

Comments.Body:
Done!
Thanks!

1529279405.Title: Improve choosing plugins/dependencies in init script
1529279405.Author: tristanisham
1529279405.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Hello all,

The most important interaction a new user has with a tool like Lume is its init script. A comprehendible, quick, and instructive experience can mean the difference between a lifelong user and a permanent dismissal. My first time experience using Lume was not the former. 

I’d like to update the initialization process to make creating a new Lume site simple. To do this, it’s like to add:

- A dynamic TUI where users can choose as many plugins as they want by arrowing through a list and toggling any with “space”
- A check to determine if any plug-in dependencies are missing and a prompt to automatically add and order them. 
- Instructions, informing the user to run `deno task serve` to initialize their project. 
- General error message cleanup and the addition of suggestions on how to fix errors when they occur. 

I hope you find these suggestions well, and hopefully can expect a pull request in the coming weeks.
1529279405.PlainText:
Improve choosing plugins/dependencies in init script

Enter your suggestions in details:
Hello all,
The most important interaction a new user has with a tool like Lume is its init script. A comprehendible, quick, and instructive experience can mean the difference between a lifelong user and a permanent dismissal. My first time experience using Lume was not the former.
I’d like to update the initialization process to make creating a new Lume site simple. To do this, it’s like to add:
A dynamic TUI where users can choose as many plugins as they want by arrowing through a list and toggling any with “space”
A check to determine if any plug-in dependencies are missing and a prompt to automatically add and order them.
Instructions, informing the user to run deno task serve to initialize their project.
General error message cleanup and the addition of suggestions on how to fix errors when they occur.
I hope you find these suggestions well, and hopefully can expect a pull request in the coming weeks.

Comments.Body:
Hey!
Thanks for your suggestions. Yes, an improved initialization is always welcome!
I'm ussing cliffy.io to build the command line interface, and it has a prompt functionality (https://cliffy.io/docs@v0.25.7/prompt) which is a bit better to the default prompt provided by Deno, so maybe it would be nice to use it.

Other instructions like `deno task serve` are good ideas. Feel free to create pull request, they are welcome! I just ask you to don't include all things in the same PR. One PR per feature is much better.

Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Awesome! Thanks for the quick response. I'll start working on adding some stuff soon! I appreciate you mentioning Cliffy. I hadn't heard of it before. I'll reply to this with some of the models I have and submit a PR when it's ready.
