1534804022.Title: Pagefind plugin exited with error code 127
1534804022.Author: shishkin
1534804022.PlainText:
Pagefind plugin exited with error code 127

Version
1.15.1
Platform
MacOS on M1/ARM
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Init new Lume project with pagefind plugin
Run deno task lume
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
Every time
What is the expected behavior?
Build works
What do you see instead?
I see an error:
The byte array content above is:
Additional information
Contents of the _bin/pagefind file:

Comments.Body:
Someone reported the same issue some days ago. We think it's a bug of the last version of pagefind in the Apple M1 processors.
Try again configuring the pagefind version to the previous version: `v0.10.5`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Try again configuring the pagefind version to the previous version: v0.10.5.

Thanks @oscarotero. Setting the version to v0.10.5 worked indeed.
Comments.Author: shishkin

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero I think this is an issue on the Lume side — either in dbin and untar, from a skim? Though I'm not quite sure how it would be happening.

I'm seeing the same error as reported in this issue on my M1 machine. Running Pagefind through its `npx` wrapper is working fine for me, and downloading the release url specified in the Lume log (`https://github.com/CloudCannon/pagefind/releases/download/v0.10.6/pagefind-v0.10.6-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz`) extracts fine, and the binary runs correctly.

My `_bin/pagefind` file created by Lume looks identical to the one referenced above:
```
30 mtime=1671398107.623792028
22 GNU.sparse.major=1
22 GNU.sparse.minor=0
28 GNU.sparse.name=pagefind
32 GNU.sparse.realsize=10945478
```

This looks like the tar headers of the file, in some way? I'm not super familiar with tar headers, but the `mtime` lines up with when that archive was created in the GitHub Action, and the `realsize` works out to the correct size of the decompressed Pagefind binary. So it looks like everything is downloading correctly, but the tar headers are being copied over instead of the tar contents.

I have no explanation for why a previous version of Pagefind is working, however. Nothing in the release flow changed between those two versions, and looking at the logs they both ran on the same OS version/build image, so they should have been archived the same. 

Let me know if there's anything I can help debug using my machine
Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
Okay, what I think is the case is that the Deno standard library archive functions don't support sparse tar files — and `v0.10.6` happened to archive as sparse, as that can happen by default on the system tar used in macOS.

I don't know how common it is for tar libraries to handle sparse tars, so I don't know if it's worth opening any issue against Deno for this. It might be one to look further into, though, as any GitHub action building for macOS that uses the system tar may, at any point, create a sparse archive that fails in Deno/Lume. Whether it does so seems to depend on the underlying blocks of the filesystem, so it's a tossup whether or not it will happen.

In any case, I've updated Pagefind's release flow to force GNU tar instead of bsdtar on macOS, which defaults the other way on sparse files, so they will no longer be created. That means version `v0.10.7` and onwards of Pagefind will be working again in Lume.
Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
Many many thanks @bglw for your time digging into this issue and releasing a new patch for Deno/Lume.
I'm not familiarized with how tar files work, but I guess Deno standard library should be able to handle sparse files. I'll post an issue to Deno.

Thanks again!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks @bglw. I can confirm that 0.10.7 works .
Comments.Author: shishkin

1551758565.Title: Passing `Data` to components breaks their behavior
1551758565.Author: naiyerasif
1551758565.Body:
### Version 1.15.1 ### Platform Windows ### What steps will reproduce the bug? Currently, Lume injects the components in the templates so that they are easily accessible by the layouts and templates. For example, I have an `_components/icon.ts` as follows. ```ts import { attributes } from "lume/plugins/attributes.ts" const defaults = { class: ["icon"], role: "img" } export default function (name: string, { title, ...attribs }: { title?: string; [key: string]: unknown }) { const href = `#${name}` const props = attributes({ ...attribs, ...defaults }) if (title) { return ` ` } else { return ` ` } } ``` Lume fails to render the component when I try to use it in a template through `comp` as described by the [docs](https://lume.land/docs/core/components/#create-your-own-components), for example, like this. ```ts export const title = "Home" export const renderOrder = 1 export default function ({ comp }) { const icon = comp.icon("moon", { slot: "dark" }) console.log(icon) return `

${title}

${icon}` } ``` The issue originates because of the ` ...attribs` specified in the component as I sometimes expect dynamic HTML attributes to be passed to the component (for example, `slot` in this case). Unfortunately, Lume also passes the entire `Data` object to the component and the `attributes` plugin fails to handle it. This also leads to unexpected issues. For example, sometimes, I may pass a `title` attribute to the component. But when I export a `title` in the template, that's also being implicitly passed to the component which is definitely not correct. ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Reproducing this does not require anything specific. Here's the repository with the reproduction: https://github.com/naiyerasif/lume-components-issue Launch the application through `deno task serve` and you should encounter the issue described above. ### What is the expected behavior? The components should receive only those arguments which are explicitly defined in their contract. The `Data` object should not be passed to them at all. ### What do you see instead? I get the following error on console. ```sh Error: Error rendering this page - page: /index.tmpl.ts at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/renderer.ts:138:19) at async concurrent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/utils.ts:193:3) at async Renderer.renderPages (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/renderer.ts:126:7) at async Site.#buildPages (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/site.ts:590:5) at async Site.build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/site.ts:518:9) at async build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/cli/build.ts:36:3) at async Command.execute (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1794:7) at async Command.parseCommand (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1639:14) at async (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/cli.ts:158:3) Caused by TypeError: value.replace is not a function at escape (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/plugins/attributes.ts:124:16) at joinAttributes (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/plugins/attributes.ts:117:29) at attributes (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/plugins/attributes.ts:27:10) at default (file:///E:/web/lume-components-issue/src/_components/icon.ts:7:16) | 6 | const href = `#${name}` | 7 | const props = attributes({ ...attribs, ...defaults }) | ~ | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^ | 8 | at ModuleEngine.renderSync (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/plugins/modules.ts:38:9) at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/component_loader.ts:100:20) at Object.render (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/component_loader.ts:98:32) at Proxy. (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.1/core/source.ts:542:60) at default (file:///E:/web/lume-components-issue/src/index.tmpl.ts:5:20) | 4 | export default function ({ comp }) { | 5 | const icon = comp.icon("moon", { slot: "dark" }) | ~ | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^ | 6 | console.log(icon) ``` ### Additional information _No response_
1551758565.PlainText:
Passing Data to components breaks their behavior

Version
1.15.1
Platform
Windows
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Currently, Lume injects the components in the templates so that they are easily accessible by the layouts and templates. For example, I have an _components/icon.ts as follows.
Lume fails to render the component when I try to use it in a template through comp as described by the docs, for example, like this.
The issue originates because of the  ...attribs specified in the component as I sometimes expect dynamic HTML attributes to be passed to the component (for example, slot in this case). Unfortunately, Lume also passes the entire Data object to the component and the attributes plugin fails to handle it.
This also leads to unexpected issues. For example, sometimes, I may pass a title attribute to the component. But when I export a title in the template, that's also being implicitly passed to the component which is definitely not correct.
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
Reproducing this does not require anything specific. Here's the repository with the reproduction: 
Launch the application through deno task serve and you should encounter the issue described above.
What is the expected behavior?
The components should receive only those arguments which are explicitly defined in their contract. The Data object should not be passed to them at all.
What do you see instead?
I get the following error on console.
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body:
As a workaround, I am explicitly importing the components as follows. ```ts import icon from "./_components/icon.ts" export const title = "Home" export const renderOrder = 1 export default function () { return `

${title}

${icon("moon", { slot: "dark" })}` } ```
Comments.Author: naiyerasif

Comments.Body:
Mmm, ok. Interesting use case.

Components are designed to work with any template engine, so they need to receive all properties in the first  argument in an object (this is how JSX works).
And they also needs access to the `comp` object in order to create nested components, and other global data like `search`, etc.

Both requirements are not compatible with the use of spread `{...attribs}` to collect the remaining attributes. I have to think in a way to fix this. Maybe defining these properties as not enumerable. 🤔  

Another workaround is using an `attr` property to store all attributes:

You component:

```js
export default function ({ name, title, attrs }) {
}
```

In your template:

```js
const icon = comp.icon({ name: "moon", attrs: {slot: "dark"} })
```

I'm going to do some tests to see if I can fix it.
Thanks.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero Thanks for taking a stab at this. I tried the dev build and I am still getting the same error. Here's what I changed: https://github.com/naiyerasif/lume-components-issue/commit/bdbb735693d001618d1c1607f2f7fde8fa5e2c98#diff-ca7f5073a339af20e76bf73a56628c71ae873d2fe29dce97e2677a03ad9dc8bc
Comments.Author: naiyerasif
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Yup, that worked. Thanks @oscarotero.
Comments.Author: naiyerasif

Comments.Body: Lume 1.15.2 was released, including this change.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1560808085.Title: Unable to use lume option, `prettUrl: false` with meta and sitemap plugins
1560808085.Author: dnk8n
1560808085.Body:
### Version

v1.29.4

### Platform

x64 linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Unfortunately because my main site is hosted with Deno Fresh (Deno Deploy), my urls come out ugly - https://rethinkcode-z0rjctkawcv0.deno.dev/articles/advanced-transitional-support/index.html

I at least want to allow the sitemap to point to the ugly urls, but can't find a way to do that.

Unless there is a setting I can change in Fresh / Deno Deploy to host static html in a pretty way?

e.g. https://rethinkcode-z0rjctkawcv0.deno.dev/articles/advanced-transitional-support/index.html -> https://rethinkcode-z0rjctkawcv0.deno.dev/articles/advanced-transitional-support

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Hosting content produced by Lume by a third party hosting platform

### What is the expected behavior?

### What do you see instead?

Comments.Body:
Ok, I just saw this: https://github.com/dnk8n/rethinkcode/blob/main/_src/pages.tmpl.js#L36

Pretty URLs option is applied only to generate the URLs of pages that already don't have the `url` variable defined. If you define the urls of a page as `/articles/advanced-transitional-support/` (note the trailing slash), the output file will always contain the `/index.html` at the end.

More info: https://lume.land/docs/creating-pages/urls/#the-url-variable

You can simply change your code to `/articles/${article.slug}.html`.

1578457442.Author: bglw
1578457442.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

👋 

I'm looking to run `.disable('code')` on the default markdown-it instance, but digging through the way its options are exposed I can't see any sanctioned way to do so — only to pass options to the initial constructor, and set the rules. 

For now I'm digging deep to run:
```js
site.formats.get(".md").engines[0].engine.disable("code");
```

But it would be great to have a builtin way to achieve this 🙂

_(Also of note: [This rule is now actually disabled by default in 11ty](https://github.com/11ty/eleventy/issues/2438).)_
1578457442.PlainText:
Expose Markdown-It disable function

Enter your suggestions in details:
👋
I'm looking to run .disable('code') on the default markdown-it instance, but digging through the way its options are exposed I can't see any sanctioned way to do so — only to pass options to the initial constructor, and set the rules.
For now I'm digging deep to run:
But it would be great to have a builtin way to achieve this 🙂
(Also of note: This rule is now actually disabled by default in 11ty.)

Comments.Body:
Hi!
Yeah, it makes sense. I just added a new hook to modify directly the markdownIt instance. So in your `_config.ts` file you can do:

```ts
site.hooks.markdownIt((engine) => engine.disable("code"));
```

It will be available when Lume 1.15.3 is released. Probably it should be disabled by default but I prefer to wait to Lume 2.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Before

Comments.Body:
I had something like this in the past but as the number of plugins was growing, it was more obvious it was a bad idea.

The main problem is Deno will download not only all plugins (even if you don't import them) but also their dependecies. Some are light dependencies but others are more heavy with hundred of subdependencies, like tailwind, imagick, windi_css, etc.

So it's better to import only the modules you need.

1583383524.Author: bglw

Reflect base subdirectory when serving site through lume server

Enter your suggestions in details:
With a configuration along the lines of:
Then templating {{ "/" | url }} in a build will output /documentation/, and when serving will output /.
I would love a mode where Lume's dev server bakes this subdirectory in, thus serving the above site at http://localhost:3000/documentation/ and matching the subdirectory that would be used in production.
Happy to dig in and have a poke at this one myself, but some initial pointers would be appreciated if so 🙂

Comments.Body: I think it's easy to implement, but why do you want this? I want to understand the use case for this behavior.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Mainly consistency. If I have the following template: ```html Home Home ``` When serving in dev mode, both will render as `/` and it won't be obvious that the `| url` filter was missing in the second case. Only when building the final site will they render as `/documentation/` and `/` and cause an issue. In general my desire is to setup dev mode as close to the final build as possible
Comments.Author: bglw
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
>The best way to handle subfolders is by using the [base_path plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/base_path/)

Good tip, I'll look at using that more. The `url` filter still holds true for loading assets though, which I would also like to be consistent between dev/prod.

>It's possible override it in your _config.ts file

That does override the location for the purposes of the `url` filter and such, but the Lume server continues to serve requests without that location taken into account. (i.e. I would like my `index.mdx` file to be at `http://localhost.com:3000/documentation/`, but it is still at `http://localhost.com:3000/`)
Comments.Author: bglw
oscarotero
bglw

Comments.Body:
Yeah, I think Eleventy does what you are requesting, but to me it isn't very practical and portable.

- The output is different depending on whether the site location is in a subpath or not.
- If you want to deploy your site with, say `rsync`, you have to take that into account.
- The index page of the local server always returns a `404`, because you need to go to the subpath (although this could be fixed with a redirect).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
After thinking of this a bit more, I decided to not implement this option, because it's a edge case and would complicate the internal code of Lume to take all cases into account (it would affect to some plugins, urls, etc).

For now, the preprocessor solution works fine. If there are more people requesting this feature, I can change my mind :)

1589893470.Title: Picture plugin
1589893470.Author: oscarotero
1589893470.Body: ### Enter your suggestions in details: It would work combined with the Imagick plugin, to generate different versions of the same image by scanning the HTML and searching for `` elements with the `formats` and/or `sizes` attribute. For example: ```html ``` This would generate the following code: ```html ``` And all versions of `/my-image.jpg`. Related issue: #356
1589893470.PlainText:
Picture plugin

Enter your suggestions in details:
It would work combined with the Imagick plugin, to generate different versions of the same image by scanning the HTML and searching for <picture> elements with the formats and/or sizes attribute. For example:
This would generate the following code:
And all versions of /my-image.jpg.
Related issue: #356

Comments.Body:
I'd love to see a picture plugin.

In terms of how I would like to see this work, rather than having to wrap every image in a site with a `picture` tag, I'd like to have a central place where the configuration of what formats and sizes I want (like already exists with the Imagick plugin). The plugin would then wrap any `img` element (that is not already wrapped in a `picture` element?) with a `picture` element and include the other formats. If that's too greedy, then having an attribute on an `img` tag to signal that it should be processed by the picture plugin (like the inline plugin) would be great too. Your suggestion of having the plugin search for `picture` elements and `formats`/`sizes` attributes could be a great way to override the central configuration of the plugin.

My motivation for suggesting this is that I often author pages in Markdown, and I'd like to not have to change how I'm including images. I'd like the standard way of including images to just work with this new plugin.

~It would also be nice to (perhaps as an option) include a hash of the generated images in the file name, to support long-term caching of these resources. Could that be supported here (or is that already handled by another plugin)?~ I've just seen that this is covered by https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/352.
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
Okay, it makes sense. Maybe it could work in this way: ```html
``` ```html ``` ```html ``` I not sure about the name of the attributes. And I'd like to have only one attribute instead of two. Maybe something like `img="avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x"`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hi @oscarotero. That looks good, but I don't think I was clear with my suggestion. Sorry about that, let me try again.

When I suggested a default configuration, I was imaging something set when registering the plugin, something like:

```javascript
site.use(picture({
  widths: [300, 600],
  formats: ["webp", "jpg"]
}));
```

I've lifted these option names from [Eleventy's image plugin](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/plugins/image/#usage), feel free to pick different options.

Alternatively, the imagick plugin uses shared data. Is there a way we could do the same for a picture plugin, so there is consistency between the two plugins?

With this configuration in place, I'd imagine all images will be affected. Perhaps then your suggestion of `img-formats`/`img-sizes` or `img` attributes would then allow users to override the default configuration for a given image.

I hope this makes sense, do let me know if I've not been clear about anything. And, of course, feel free to ignore all of this if you choose!
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
@cgwrench Yes, I got your suggestion, but don't think it's a good idea to specify this globally in the `_config` file. A website can have different sections, with different configurations for pictures (It can have a blog, a photo gallery, a landing page, etc). So I think this feature should be defined in the templates, in order to have different configurations per page (or even per section in the same page).

If you want to define this behavior for the entire site, you can add the attribute in a layout shared to all pages. For example:

```html
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
...
</head>

<body img="avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x">
    {{ content | safe }}
</body>
</html>
```

To define this value using shared data, you can create a variable in a _data file and use it in the templates. For example:

```yml
# _data.yml

picture_config: "avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x"
```

```html
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
...
</head>

<body img="{{ picture_config }}">
    {{ content | safe }}
</body>
</html>
```

Does it works for you?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That does work for me, thanks!
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
Work in progress here: https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/tree/main/picture

You can see an example here:
https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/blob/main/picture/demo/index.njk

The current syntax is:

- `webp jpg 300` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px width
- `webp jpg 300@1,2` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px width for 1x and 2x resolutions
- `webp jpg 300@1,2  600@1,2` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px and 600px width for 1x and 2x resolutions
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: It will be available in the coming v1.18.0. I'm closing this
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1606820484.Title: The date plugin should support specifying a timezone
1606820484.Author: andreubotella
1606820484.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Currently, the date plugin uses the system's default timezone. This can result in a difference between local testing and deployment, if the build for deployment uses CI (Github Actions uses the UTC timezone, for example, [although this is configurable](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/set-timezone)).

The timezone to use should be able to be set in the plugin's options, but it would also be nice to have a format that will display a date as with `Date.prototype.toISOString`. This is equivalent to `date("ATOM")` in a UTC timezone, but since `toISOString` is a standard, it would be useful to also make it available regardless of the timezone set in the plugin options.
1606820484.PlainText:
The date plugin should support specifying a timezone

Enter your suggestions in details:
Currently, the date plugin uses the system's default timezone. This can result in a difference between local testing and deployment, if the build for deployment uses CI (Github Actions uses the UTC timezone, for example, although this is configurable).
The timezone to use should be able to be set in the plugin's options, but it would also be nice to have a format that will display a date as with Date.prototype.toISOString. This is equivalent to date("ATOM") in a UTC timezone, but since toISOString is a standard, it would be useful to also make it available regardless of the timezone set in the plugin options.

Comments.Body:
The only way I know to set a timezone that works consistently everywhere (not only with `date` plugin but also anywhere you do a `new Date()`, is by setting the `TZ` environment variable.

This is what I use in the task for tests: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/deno.json#L4

So I recommend to edit your lume task to include this environment variable with your timezone of choice.

It might be configurable in the `date` plugin options (there's a package to convert dates to different timezones https://date-fns.org/v2.29.3/docs/Time-Zones) but not sure if it's the best way, because it would work only at plugin level, to format a date. The dates defined in frontmatters or filenames (like `2022-01-01_hello.md`) would be created with the system's timezone, and this could affect to order the pages by date, or to select pages from a specific date.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
In my (WIP) blog I'm using `"git created"` by default, and overriding that when needed with ISO timestamps, so I don't run into this issue when parsing dates. But yeah, it is an issue in general. Whenever the temporal proposal ships, it should allow parsing dates with a timezone option, but I'm not sure if it would allow the variety of date formats that `new Date()` supports.

I framed this issue specifically about date formatting because of that, but if you think any such options should also apply to date parsing, that's fine. I can define a custom `date` filter, so this is not a blocker for me.
Comments.Author: andreubotella

Comments.Body:
Yeah, I think temporal should make things more easy.

If you find a simple way to have this in the `date` plugin, please let me know. There are some issues in date-fns related with this https://github.com/date-fns/date-fns/issues/489 but looks like there's not way to pass a timezone for formatting.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
binyamin

Comments.Body:
@binyamin dayjs looks nice but the available formats are different from date-fns: [dayjs](https://day.js.org/docs/en/display/format) vs [date-fns](https://date-fns.org/v2.30.0/docs/format), so it would break things.

I'd rather to wait until I can switch to something more standard like the Temporal API or even the Intl API that doesn't require any external dependency and it's already supported by Deno (but it's buggy in my native language, Galician, so I cannot use it for now).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Temporal API is stable in Deno 1.40 now: https://deno.com/blog/v1.40 :)
Comments.Author: brookback

Comments.Body:
It's not stable yet, it requires the `--unstable-temporal` flag.
But Lume 2 comes with the temporal polyfill as a dependency, so maybe it can be used to modify the timezone of a specific date.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah, I misread. Sorry.
Comments.Author: brookback

1610365009.Title: Finer control over URLs
1610365009.Author: thegedge
1610365009.PlainText:
Finer control over URLs

Enter your suggestions in details:
New to Deno/Lume, coming from Node/Next.js, and loving Lume so far! It's the closest to what I've been looking for in an SSG, so thanks so much for all the effort you all have put into building :)
Given that I'm coming from Next.js, I want to avoid changing all of my URLs, but struggling to get Lume to do exactly what I want via exporting url from _data.ts. Here's what my folder structure looks like:g
index.tsx
blog/index.tsx
blog/2022-01-01-my-blog-post.mdx
The output files, respectively:
index.html
blog.html
blog/2022-01-01-my-blog-post.html
and the URLs, respectively:
/
/blog
/blog/2022-01-01-my-blog-post
So the few points I'll surface:
Dates are not stripped from the URL.
Dates and titles are separated by a hyphen, not an underscore.
URLs don't need a trailing slash.
This one really only impacts dev mode, because production will know how to serve /subdir/file.html as /subdir/file. I've been using netlify dev + lume build --watch for now.
/any/sub/directory/index.tsx becomes /any/sub/directory
I want something like page.outputPath to be dir/file.html but page.data.url to be dir/file
Any thoughts on making some/all of these more configurable? It was hard to get right, but I was able to get it working by doing two things. First, this in my root _data.ts
I think it would be helpful if Lume would use the default URL if the url function returned undefined / null / some placeholder value.
Second, this is in my _config.ts:
I was super happy that all the things were there to get this working, but it was trickier than I anticipated. If Lume would like to stick with the current defaults, a migration guide for Next.js would be really great!
Let me know what ideas we'd be open to. I'm more than happy to send in some PRs to implement those.

Comments.Body:
Hi, @thegedge 

Nice comments! To clarify:

> Dates are not stripped from the URL.

As I can see, your files are named like `yyyy-mm-dd-slug` so that's not detected as a date, because Lume uses a underscore to separate dates and slugs (`yyyy-mm-dd_slug`). [See code here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/filesystem.ts#L41).

In theory, the output files should keep the dates because they are not interpreted as dates so Lume doesn't remove it, and I assume that's what you want, right?



> I think it would be helpful if Lume would use the default URL if the url function returned undefined / null / some placeholder value.

This is how it works already. [See the code ](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/page_preparer.ts#L63). But it only detect `undefined`, not `null`. 



The use of `modifyUrls` plugin to remove the `.html` extensions is the right way to do that. Previously there was the `no-html-extension` option but it was very hacky [and was removed in Lume 1.13.0](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#removed-3). The Lume local server should support this kind of URLs ([Here's the code](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/server.ts#L143-L145)) out of the box, so you don't need `netlify dev` for that. Let me know if this is not true.

---

PR are very welcome, but I want to understand better the changes that you want to implement.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> This is how it works already. [See the code ](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/page_preparer.ts?rgh-link-date=2023-03-06T11%3A00%3A20Z#L63). But it only detect undefined, not null.

Oh, nice! It didn't look to be working for me, but that's likely my own mistake.

> The Lume local server should support this kind of URLs ([Here's the code](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/server.ts?rgh-link-date=2023-03-06T11%3A00%3A20Z#L143-L145)) out of the box, so you don't need netlify dev for that. Let me know if this is not true.

I think I may have added the `modifyUrl` plugins _after_ switching to `netlify dev`, and neglected to try out `lume serve` again, but you're totally right that this is working.

> In theory, the output files should keep the dates because they are not interpreted as dates so Lume doesn't remove it, and I assume that's what you want, right?

It is, but let me clarify: My files are named `yyyy-mm-dd_slug` but I want my URL to be `yyyy-mm-dd-slug`. Ideally, I could name my files `yyyy-mm-dd-slug` and have the date parsed properly + remain in the URL. Here's what I'd propose:

1. Support parsing the date from files named something like `yyyy-mm-dd-slug.md`. I suspect this won't be easy to generalize, so a lume option to provide a date parsing regex.
2. Add a lume option to keep the date in the slug.

What do you think?

Either way, once I get my site completely migrated, I'd be happy to put together a "Next.js to lume" migration document on https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land :)
Comments.Author: thegedge
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That's significantly simpler than what I had. Much appreciated!

I'm happy to consider that the way forward in this case, so feel free to close unless you think providing an option is useful, just not high priority.
Comments.Author: thegedge

Comments.Body:
Nice!
I close this, because the url function in `_data.ts` is the most flexible solution.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1629746219.Title: Question: how to setup a proxy server (in development mode)
1629746219.Author: max-l
1629746219.Body:
I'm developing a static site that needs to make client side rest call to another site, 
In production, the site will be served by nginx, and it will have the proper CORS header.

In dev mode, I would like the lume server to setup CORS correctly,
and I would also like to intercept calls to the dev server.


The doc shows how to create the lume server: 
 https://lume.land/docs/core/server/

I tried it (the first example), but when started, it only serves static pages,
I need it to do the same as "deno task lume -s", but with more configurability.
1629746219.PlainText:
Question: how to setup a proxy server (in development mode)

I'm developing a static site that needs to make client side rest call to another site,
In production, the site will be served by nginx, and it will have the proper CORS header.
In dev mode, I would like the lume server to setup CORS correctly,
and I would also like to intercept calls to the dev server.
The doc shows how to create the lume server:
I tried it (the first example), but when started, it only serves static pages,
I need it to do the same as "deno task lume -s", but with more configurability.

Comments.Author: oscarotero
max-l

Comments.Body:
I'm not an expert of CORs configuration, but I assume you want the local server (started with `deno task lume -s`) to send the responses with some additional HTTP headers, right?

What's exactly the problem?
- The middleware doesn't add any additional header?
- Or the middleware add some headers but that isn't enought to have CORS configurated propertly?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The headers don't get added (I don't see them in the chrome console).

The logic is quite simple, here's how it's done with other servers:  https://supabase.com/docs/guides/functions/cors

or with nginx:  https://serverfault.com/questions/162429/how-do-i-add-access-control-allow-origin-in-nginx

Being a deno begginer (as well as a lume beginner), I'm strugling to implement this in Lume. I could perhaps try to first implemente it in "plain deno", probably with a middleware, 

Do Lume middleware functions have the same function signature and work the same way, as  deno middleware functions ?

In other words, can a "plain deno server" middleware just be "dropped in" a Lume development server config ? 

That would certainly make things easyer, then the problem of "how to setup CORS in Lume" become "how to setup CORS in Deno", and I ask the question to a wider audience.

Meanwhile (while I figure this out), a nice work around would be to just add a route to the development server, so I can serve certain requests from the lume dev server, and proxy the request.

In general, it would be nice to be able to add custom routes in the dev server.

Thanks a lot for the help BTW, I like the minimalism of Lume (and Deno). I've been using gatsby for years, it is also quite minimalist, but it's become too bloated to my taste.
Comments.Author: max-l
oscarotero

Comments.Body: @max-l I just released a new version of Lume that fixes this bug. So after upgrading to v1.16.0, it should work fine. Let me know if don't.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks !

1635636805.Title: How to use the non-minified dev environment in client side react, to get full error messages ?
1635636805.Author: max-l
1635636805.Body:
I followed this example to have client side react code in a project:  https://github.com/lumeland/react-todo

And I am getting "minified" react errors, that suggest using a non minified build, to get full error and warning messages.

```
Uncaught Error: Minified React error #423; visit https://reactjs.org/docs/error-decoder.html?invariant=423 for the full message or use the non-minified dev environment for full errors and additional helpful warnings.
```

I'm not sure if it's a setting in esbuild, an environement variable, linking to another react build, etc...
1635636805.PlainText:
How to use the non-minified dev environment in client side react, to get full error messages ?

I followed this example to have client side react code in a project:  
And I am getting "minified" react errors, that suggest using a non minified build, to get full error and warning messages.
I'm not sure if it's a setting in esbuild, an environement variable, linking to another react build, etc...

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hmm, this kind of problem is hard to google, the search terms "minify", "dev mode", etc, have zillions of hits that don't address the topic. 

Deno is doing some magic to fetch dependencies with this kind of syntax:
```
import React from "npm:react";
```

The solution is probably to use a development build : 

https://unpkg.com/browse/react@18.2.0/umd/
```
react.development.js	110 kB	application/javascript
react.production.min.js	10.7 kB	application/javascript
react.profiling.min.js	10.7 kB	application/javascript
```

<script src="https://unpkg.com/react@18/umd/react.development.js"></script>

The challenge for me is to figure out how to get deno to load a dev build. I'm sure it's simple, once you know how to.
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
The client side javascript is not generated by Deno but esbuild plugin. There's a loader to resolve all imports. For `npm` modules, it uses esm.sh: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/esbuild.ts#L91-L96

I used `console.log` to debug what URL are loaded and with `jsxDev` option to true, the Reac file is https://esm.sh/react@18.2.0/jsx-dev-runtime (which seems correct).

So I don't know where these warnings come from. Maybe from react-dom?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
https://esm.sh/#docs

Development Mode

> import React from "https://esm.sh/react?dev"
> With the ?dev option, esm.sh builds a module with process.env.NODE_ENV set to "development" or based on the condition development in the exports field of package.json. This is useful for libraries that have different behavior in development and production. For example, React will use a different warning message in development mode.
> 

I tried this : 
```
import { useEffect, useState } from "npm:react?dev"
```

and also this in import_map.json : 
```
{
  "imports": {
    "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.16.0/",
    "react": "https://esm.sh/react?dev"
  }
}
```
And got errors in both cases
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
Oh, I didn't know the `?dev` parameter. That's much better. It must be added not only to react but react-dom. I think this option should be managed by the esbuild plugin. For example:

```
site.use(esbuild({
    extensions: [".jsx"],
    dev: true
  }))
```

I'm going to make some changes. Just a second.

oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@max-l Ok thanks.
I was testing the repo and indeed, it fails. But the problem is not esbuild (that works fine) but the server side rendering (by Deno).

I discovered the problem is this line https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/app/ProteinTable.jsx#L25 (if I remove it, it works fine).

Moving the line to after the `key` attribute also fix the error:

```js
<tr
  key={`${rowProps.key}-expanded-${i}`}
  {...rowProps}
>
```

Don't ask me why :D
Maybe it's a bug of Deno or maybe in JSX the variadic attributes must be placed at the end (like in JS functions).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Interesting !

I'm a bit puzzled that Deno is involved here, because that code never runs on the server (or at least it shouldn't), if esm does the compilation, the only thing Deno should do is to send the code (as inert data) to the client. 

I noticed something interesting: if I remove the offending code, start Lume, and then add the code again, the code reloads and it works correctly.   

It seems as if the offending code prevents something on the server to initialize correctly, because once you remove it, and the initialisation gets done, the offending code has lost it's corrupting effect !
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
The to-do app repository (that you used as a template) pre-renders the code at buildtime in order to be hydrated later on client side.

At buildtime, the App is imported in the `index.tmpl.ts` page and rendered here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/index.tmpl.ts#L6

On the client side, the app is imported and hydrated here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/main.jsx#L6-L11

So if you don't need buildtime pre-rendering, you can remove the code in the index.tmpl.ts file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Interesting, there was much more magic going on than I thought !

I suppose I should use useEffect to switch on/off the prerendering strategicaly ?
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
The prerendering is handled in the Lume template, before any React code is executed.

There's no much magic: just render the root component `App` with `ReactDOMServer.renderToString()` function and place the HTML result in the page (here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/index.tmpl.ts#L20).

The hydration step on the browser side just complete the HTML adding the event listeners (onclick, etc). It only makes sense if the component's data is available at buildtime, so the output HTML is similar to the code generated on the client side. In the to-do example, it didn't make sense because the data is stored in the localStorage on the browser side, so at build time only generates an empty to-do list. But it's just an example to demostrate that this is possible.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Request: include .md source files in output, similar to .js sourcemaps.

Enter your suggestions in details:
I think it's cool how you can publish a website in a way that preserves the human-readable source, and I think it would make sense for Lume to allow a similar feature. I tried this:
but it appears that once Lume has decided a file is being statically copied to the output, there's no way to also render it as html. Is there a workaround for this, or would it require a new config flag?

Comments.Author: oscarotero

1640399491.Title: Incorrect typing for `jsx_preact.ts`
1640399491.Author: indifferentghost
1640399491.PlainText:
Incorrect typing for jsx_preact.ts

Version
v1.16.0
Platform
Linux/Firefox
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Utilize renderAsync it always says it returns a string when we can clearly see that's not true.
What is the expected behavior?
Typescript inference should be enough here.
What do you see instead?
hard typed as a string
Additional Details?

Comments.Body:
Yeah, good catch. `renderSync` must return a string or an object with the `toString()` function defined (which is the case here): https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L310

I'll fix it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Version

Linux

{{ alternates | dump }}

always
What is the expected behavior?
it should dump kv pairs of language codes to pages
What do you see instead?
nothing

Comments.Body: Yes, sorry. The breaking changes of the `multilanguage` plugin introduced in 1.16.0 includes also a change in the `alternates` object. Now it's just an array of the page data objects. So you can do: ```njk {% for alt in alternates %} {{ alt.title }} {% endfor %} ```

1649092396.Title: Search plugin: support logical NOT
1649092396.Author: kwaa
1649092396.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details: ### Discussed in https://github.com/lumeland/lume/discussions/405
Originally posted by **kwaa** March 31, 2023 I use [my own Frontmatter format](https://fff.js.org), where [`flags: ['unlisted']`](https://fff.js.org/concepts/flags.html) means that this post is not included in the list. So how do I accurately search for flags that contain unlisted? [The search plugin's documentation](https://lume.land/plugins/search/) doesn't seem to indicate this, or maybe it doesn't support it yet.
In this discussion I proposed `flags!*=unlisted`, but the logical NOT is more general. Example: ``` category^=A => !category^=A category$=B => !category$=B title*=security => !title*=security level>2 => !level>2 ```
1649092396.PlainText:
Search plugin: support logical NOT

Enter your suggestions in details:
Discussed in 
<div type='discussions-op-text'>
Originally posted by kwaa March 31, 2023
I use my own Frontmatter format, where flags: ['unlisted'] means that this post is not included in the list.
So how do I accurately search for flags that contain unlisted? The search plugin's documentation doesn't seem to indicate this, or maybe it doesn't support it yet.
In this discussion I proposed flags!*=unlisted, but the logical NOT is more general.
Example:

Comments.Body:
Ok, it makes sense.

I just commited a first version. You can test it after updating Lume to the latest development version (`deno task lume upgrade --dev`).

It supports both versions: `category!^=A` and `!category^=A` are equivalents.

The reason is the NOT operator of `=` is `!=`, so it would be a bit confusing to have a different syntax for the other operators. Now you can do `!category=A` as an alternative way to `category!=A`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Ok, it makes sense.
> 
> I just commited a first version. You can test it after updating Lume to the latest development version (`deno task lume upgrade --dev`).
> 
> It supports both versions: `category!^=A` and `!category^=A` are equivalents.
> 
> The reason is the NOT operator of `=` is `!=`, so it would be a bit confusing to have a different syntax for the other operators. Now you can do `!category=A` as an alternative way to `category!=A`.

`!flags*=unlisted` and `flags!*=unlisted` have been tested to work! 🚀
Comments.Author: kwaa

1650993698.Title: includes folders should be ignored automatically
1650993698.Author: oscarotero
1650993698.PlainText:
includes folders should be ignored automatically

Enter your suggestions in details:
Example:
The folder /sass should be ignored automatically without need to do site.ignore("sass").

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero  Documentation for SASS plugin:

"By default, the plugin is configured to look into the _includes directory to find the imported files (you can change this behaviour by editing the includes option)"

I have tried putting files under "_includes" but they are not getting picked up. I did a bit of debugging and the processor when going through pages does not include files under "_include" thus the ".scss" files are not found.

In summary, the SASS plugin includes mechanism seems broken. I am new to Lume so I cannot log a bug with confidence.
Comments.Author: patroytall

Comments.Body:
@patroytall Do you have any `.scss` file outside the `_includes` folder that imports these files? For example, let's say you have the following structure:

```
styles.scss
_includes/colors.scss
```

In your styles.scss file, you can have the following code:

```scss
@import colors.scss
```

and this import should be resolved to `_includes/colors.scss`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: OK, thank you. So yes, to get it working I added a /styles outside the "_includes". I now understand that "_includes" is for imports. Documentation is correct as is.
Comments.Author: patroytall

MDX plugin fails to render

Version

Platform
MacOS 13.3.1
What steps will reproduce the bug?
Clone the repository 
run deno task serve

follow the above steps and it should reproduce every time.
What is the expected behavior?
Site should build correctly, with two pages: / and /emedexis
What do you see instead?
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body: per the error, using `lume/plugins/jsx.ts` instead of `lume/plugins/jsx_preact.ts` works as expected.
Comments.Author: nubunto

Comments.Body:
This is because Deno use React by default to render JSX. You have to [configure Deno to use preact](https://lume.land/plugins/jsx_preact/#configuration). Add the following to your `deno.json` file:

```json
"compilerOptions": {
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "jsxImportSource": "npm:preact"
  }
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yup, this indeed does it. I feel this should be added to the plugin documentation, would it be okay if I did so?
Comments.Author: nubunto

Comments.Body: Sure, It would be great. Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1696723667.PlainText:
lume task --serve does not use server.js as doc says

Version
1.16.0
Platform
ubuntu
What steps will reproduce the bug?
The project's ./server.js  is never  run.
To reproduce, write junk code in this file,
then run "lume --serve" as specified here:   
And the server will start normaly
How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?
every time
What is the expected behavior?
the customize server in ./server.js should run instead of the default one
What do you see instead?
the default server
Additional information
No response

Comments.Body:
Ok, I just noticed that the server must be assigned to lume config : 

```
import {server} from "./server.js"
const site = lume({server})
```

So it's not a bug, 
but a note in the doc would help: https://lume.land/docs/core/server/
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
Fair enough, the documentation is not clear on this.

The `./server.js` file is not used by lume at all, this is just an example of how to create your own server to, for example, Deno deploy. And this file must be executed directly by deno `deno run -A ./server.js`. 

`lume --serve` uses this class internally but with some default middlewares (`reload`, `not_found`, `no_cache` and `logger`) in order to create a proper development experience. If you want to include additional middlewares or configure the server somehow, use the [server options in the _config file](https://lume.land/docs/configuration/config-file/#server-options).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1699174692.Title: Add option for specifying the init directory
1699174692.Author: echoptic
1699174692.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

I was surprised when i saw that i cant specify the init directory by adding the directory name after the init script like this:
`deno run -Ar https://deno.land/x/lume/init.ts <init directory name>`, and was not asked for the init directory when specifying the options in the init script. Maybe theres a way to do this, but i didnt see it
1699174692.PlainText:
Add option for specifying the init directory

Enter your suggestions in details:
I was surprised when i saw that i cant specify the init directory by adding the directory name after the init script like this:
deno run -Ar  <init directory name>, and was not asked for the init directory when specifying the options in the init script. Maybe theres a way to do this, but i didnt see it

Comments.Body:
Yeah, this option is not available and it's a great idea. I guess if the directory doesn't exist, Lume should create it, right?
I like it as a CLI argument (I wouldn't include more questions).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Yeah I think if it doesnt exist it should create it, and if it exists and its not empty then print an error and exit the cli.
Comments.Author: echoptic

Comments.Body: If you change your mind about the question, maybe it could ask at the end something like `This directory is not empty? Do you want to continue?`. Also, I could work on adding this feature if its ok, seems easy and a good first issue.
Comments.Author: echoptic

Comments.Body:
Why the directory must be empty? `lume init` should work like `npm init`, `git init` or any other init script. You can use it to convert your existing site (built with other tool) to Lume. The only thing it does is to generate a couple of new files (`_config.ts` and `deno.json`).

I think you are thinking of `git clone` that requires an empty folder because there can be conflicts. But `init` is not the same.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: You have a point, even if the directory is not empty its not a big deal since only 2 files are created.
Comments.Author: echoptic

1700520071.Author: fserb
1700520071.Body:
### Version

1.17.0

### Platform

mac

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

This is my minimal repro. `_config.js`:

```js
import lume from "lume/mod.ts";

const site = lume();
site.copy([".js"]); // it also works if you do terser, for example, and also with css copy or css postcss.
export default site;
```

create a file `a.js` with a anything on it and run `lume -w`.

If you check, there will be a `_site/a.js` file with the correct content.

Now in a separate terminal, run `touch a.js`. Lume will show: 
```
Changes detected:
- /a.js
```

but now the file disappears from `_site`. If you actually change `a.js` content, then everything works as expected (the file gets updated).



### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

I tried to look into the code and I have no idea how this can be happening, so maybe is some weird OS-specific, thing? I've created a new project from scratch and I'm always able to repro.

### What is the expected behavior?

The file to stay on `_site` when it changed but the content hasn't changed.

### What do you see instead?

The file disappears from `_site` until I re-run lume.

### Additional information

_No response_

If it's any other `.md` it works fine, but if it's called `index.md` (and becomes `index.html`), then the same problem happens.

1740124763.Title: Warnings and a strict mode
1740124763.Author: not-my-profile
1740124763.Body:
With Lume there are currently many opportunities to make small mistakes that will not be reported. For example:

* Mistyping the source path given to `Site.copy` (if the file doesn't exist, nothing will happen).
* Using `Site.copy` multiple times for the same source path with different destinations (only the last call will take effect).
* Making a typo in a variable in a nunjuck template (i.e. referencing undefined data).
* Making a typo when specifying a tag name (either in the metadata or when calling e.g. `search.pages`).

I think it would be nice if such likely usage errors would be detected and reported. The CLI could even have a `--strict` mode that could fail if any such problem was to be detected for usage within CIs. Detecting the mistyped tags would require the ability to optionally declare them with e.g. `lume({tags: ["post"]}, ...)`.

What do you think about this matter?
1740124763.PlainText:
Warnings and a strict mode

With Lume there are currently many opportunities to make small mistakes that will not be reported. For example:
Mistyping the source path given to Site.copy (if the file doesn't exist, nothing will happen).
Using Site.copy multiple times for the same source path with different destinations (only the last call will take effect).
Making a typo in a variable in a nunjuck template (i.e. referencing undefined data).
Making a typo when specifying a tag name (either in the metadata or when calling e.g. search.pages).
I think it would be nice if such likely usage errors would be detected and reported. The CLI could even have a --strict mode that could fail if any such problem was to be detected for usage within CIs. Detecting the mistyped tags would require the ability to optionally declare them with e.g. lume({tags: ["post"]}, ...).
What do you think about this matter?

Comments.Body:
There are some interesting points here. Not sure if a strict mode is a good idea because it would be hard to implement some of the checkings.

For example, there are many ways to select and copy a file:
- Using a extension: `site.copy([".jpg"]`
- Using the file name: `site.copy("image/one.jpg")`
- Using a folder name: `site.copy("image")`

The same file can match to multiple conditions, and this is okay. For example, you may want to copy all jpg files to a specific directory, except a couple of files that have a different destination. The only thing we can do is if the same condition was already used (for example, calling `site.copy("image")` twice), we can show a warning.

> Making a typo in a variable in a nunjuck template (i.e. referencing undefined data).

Nunjucks has already an option for that (`throwOnUndefined`) that by default is false.

> Making a typo when specifying a tag name (either in the metadata or when calling e.g. search.pages).

I don't understand this. It's okay to filter by non existing fields. For example, you may want to get all pages with the category "lume", so you run `search.pages("category=lume")`. There may be pages without the category defined (because they are different types of pages that don't need it). What do you think it should happen here?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I think the best way to address the pitfall of the current `Site.copy` API is to enable the copying of one file to multiple destinations and perform the deprecations suggested in https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/428#issuecomment-1585699760.

> I don't understand this.

I agree that it's okay to filter on non-existing fields. I was talking of some way of validating the metadata defined in frontmatters. I guess this could be achieved with an [event handler](https://lume.land/docs/core/events/).

If you were to define the schema for your data with e.g. JSON schema we could even validate queries. E.g. if you have defined that there only are the tags `foo`, `bar` and `baz` then filtering for a tag `orange` could be detected as an error. But yeah validating data is of course more important than validating queries.
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

Comments.Body: Schema validation is a nice idea for a plugin. It could be a preprocessor that validate all pages before rendering and show warnings when a page has a different scheme.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1742155784.Title: Cannot copy one file to multiple destinations
1742155784.Author: not-my-profile
1742155784.Body:
The `Site.copy` API is currently quite the footgun:

* You cannot copy one file to multiple locations.

   ```
   site.copy("foo", "bar1")
   site.copy("foo", "bar2")
   ```

   `foo` is only copied to `bar2`.

* You cannot copy a directory and one file in the directory elsewhere.

   ```
   site.copy("static")
   site.copy("static/foo", "bar")
   ```

   `static/foo` is not copied to `bar`.

I would consider both of these to be bugs.
1742155784.PlainText:
Cannot copy one file to multiple destinations

The Site.copy API is currently quite the footgun:
You cannot copy one file to multiple locations.
foo is only copied to bar2.
You cannot copy a directory and one file in the directory elsewhere.
static/foo is not copied to bar.
I would consider both of these to be bugs.

Comments.Body:
Ok, I just saw your pull request (many thanks!) but I'd like to continue the conversation here about how is supposed to work and leave the PR for implementation details.

Is there any real use case in which you want to duplicate a file and save it in multiple destinations?

The most common use case is:

- I want to copy all files in the directory`/statics` to the destination root: `site.copy("statics", ".")`.
- But the files in `statics/favicons` should be in the root, so I copy them with `site.copy("statics/favicons", ".")`.

What you're proposing is the destination directory had the favicons in two different places: `/` and `/favicons` (one per each `copy`). I think the correct behavior is to have only once, because one copy overrides the other.

Lume doesn't handle this well, because once the `static` folder is found, it copies all its content to the destination folder without checking other cases inside this folder (like the `favicons` folder exception).

It's perfectly valid that the same file matches different `copy()` conditions. But this shouldn't output multiple copies of the same file, it should choose the most specific condition.

For example, I want to copy all `.jpg` files:

```js
site.copy([".jpg"]);
```

But I want to copy a specific jpg file to a specific destination:

```js
site.copy("/cover/image.jpg", "/img/cover/image.jpg");
```

The second condition is more specific than the first, so the image should be copied once to `img/cover/image.jpg`

To me, the priority is the following (ordered from less specific to most specific):

- Copy by extension is the less specific (`site.copy([".jpg"])`)
- Copy by folder or filename is more specific (`site.copy("/img/cover/image.jpg")`).
  - Subfolders are more specific than parent folders. `/img/cover` is more specific than `/img` and `/img/cover/image.jpg` is more specific than `/img/cover`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Is there any real use case in which you want to duplicate a file and save it in multiple destinations?

You could use Lume to generate multiple sites.

I agree that the "copy by extension" mechanism should have a lower priority than the "copy a specific path" mechanism. However I seriously don't think that we should do anything fancy about the "copy a specific path" mechanism because it breaks user expectations.

When it comes to an API the code should do what the code says literally. If a method is called `copy`, calling it should always attempt to copy. By that logic I'd also rename `copyRemainingFiles` to e.g. `setImplicitCopyDecider` and deprecate `copy(from: string[], ...)` in favor of something like:

```ts
site.setImplicitCopyDecider(
  (path: string, ext: string) => [".jpg", ".gif", ".png"].includes(ext),
);
```

This also makes it much easier to explain the behavior to the user: With the `copy` function you can perform an explicit copy operation. Files that aren't explicitly copied can still be copied by defining an implicit copy decider function.
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

Comments.Body:
> You could use Lume to generate multiple sites.

I don't get this. You cannot build multiple sites from a single `deno task build`. But you want to output different `deno task build` to the same folder with the `emptyDest` option set to `false`.

`copyRemainingFiles` is not the same as `copy(from: string[])`. Copying has priority over loading. For example, Lume loads by default all `.md` files (to parse and generate html pages). If you set `copy([".md"])`, `.md` files won't be loaded and only will be copied because Lume checks if a file must be copied before loading it. `copyRemainingFiles` is used only if the file is not copied nor loaded.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1742562283.Title: Dots in filenames lead to missing `.html` extension
1742562283.Author: not-my-profile
1742562283.Body:
Like any other static-site generator Lume by default processes `foo.md` to `foo/index.html`. However if the filename contains a dot, e.g. `foo.bar.md` then Lume currently writes the HTML to `foo.bar` ... rather than writing to `foo.bar/index.html` as you would expect (case in point Jekyll and Eleventy and I'd assume most other static-site generators behave such by default).

It's worth noting that setting `prettyUrls: false` doesn't change the Lume behavior: it still writes the HTML to `foo.bar` rather than `foo.bar.html`, as you would expect. The relevant code appears to be the `getUrl` function in `core/source.ts`.

While changing the Lume default behavior regarding this would be a breaking change, I'd consider conforming to user expectations and common practice to be enough justification.

As a workaround you can currently [specify a relative URL](https://lume.land/docs/creating-pages/urls/#relative-urls) in the page frontmatter, such as `url: ./foo.bar/`.
1742562283.PlainText:
Dots in filenames lead to missing .html extension

Like any other static-site generator Lume by default processes foo.md to foo/index.html. However if the filename contains a dot, e.g. foo.bar.md then Lume currently writes the HTML to foo.bar ... rather than writing to foo.bar/index.html as you would expect (case in point Jekyll and Eleventy and I'd assume most other static-site generators behave such by default).
It's worth noting that setting prettyUrls: false doesn't change the Lume behavior: it still writes the HTML to foo.bar rather than foo.bar.html, as you would expect. The relevant code appears to be the getUrl function in core/source.ts.
While changing the Lume default behavior regarding this would be a breaking change, I'd consider conforming to user expectations and common practice to be enough justification.
As a workaround you can currently specify a relative URL in the page frontmatter, such as url: ./foo.bar/.

Comments.Body:
This behavior is intentional. In Lume you can create other files than HTML pages. For example, you can create a css file from nunjucks simply by setting the name to `/styles.css.njk`. Lume removes the template extension and, if the remaining name contains an extension, it's keept.

Related: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/discussions/113
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Right that makes sense if the last extension is a template extension such as `.njk` but it doesn't make sense for `.md` as the last extension as far as I can tell. So perhaps we could introduce some distinction?

Conceptually `.md$` maps to `.html` and `.njk$` maps to the empty string.
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

Comments.Body: It's true that `md` is intended for HTML, but I think making Lume so clever can be confusing because similar things works differently. In theory, you can use markdown to build anything. Imagine, for example, the md file uses a layout that converts this html code into a svg file (or even render it to a PNG file). You may want to name this file `filename.png.md`. :)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: For the vary rare case that you actually want an `.md` file to produce a non-HTML file you can just specify the `url` in the YAML front matter. I think it's much more common to have a regular `.md` file with another dot in the filename.
Comments.Author: not-my-profile
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> so you can create this page with `404.html.md`

I believe file extensions should describe the content of the file. I'd consider conflating actual file extensions describing the content of the file with file extensions describing the output format of some conversion process to be an anti-pattern. I'd consider any `.html.md` file to be confusing.

> this introduces some cognitive load in how Lume works and make its behavior less predictable

What is more predictable?

(A)

* `foo.md` -> `foo/index.html`
* `foo.bar.md` -> `foo.bar`
* `foo.bar.baz.md` -> `foo.baz`

or (B)?

* `foo.md` -> `foo/index.html`
* `foo.bar.md` -> `foo.bar/index.html`
* `foo.bar.baz.md` -> `foo.bar.baz/index.html`

A) is the current default behavior of Lume and B) is the default behavior of Jekyll and Eleventy. I find B) to be much more predictable.  (Granted that this is particularly true because in A3 Lume currently omits the `.bar` from the output filename, which is caused by the slug regex.)

> To me, a file shouldn't have a dot in the name, because this is introduces some ambiguity. You can request the url `/foo/bar.png` expecting a png file but it opens the html page `/foo/bar.png/index.html`.

Correct, you cannot assume a file type just from a URL ... this however is absolutely not a problem because web browsers determine the file type via the `Content-Type` HTTP header. Note that even if the default Lume behavior is changed, there is nothing stopping you from still avoiding dots in your filenames, if you prefer it that way.

On the contrary the current Lume default behavior poses an inconvenience to any user that wants to e.g. mention a version number in a file name, something even the Lume website does: https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-1.17.0-release-notes
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

Comments.Body:
Okay, let's mimic the Jekyll / Eleventy behavior but I don't want to introduce a breaking change now. 
I'm going to put this on hold until Lume 2.0 that will introduce many breaking changes.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed in the v2 branch.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

1772949438.Title: Github Actions error: pagefind: 1: Syntax error: word unexpected (expecting ")")
1772949438.Author: ehaughee
1772949438.Body:
### Version

1.17.5

### Platform

ubuntu-latest

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Created a Lume project with the simple blog plugin.

`_config.ts`
```typescript
import lume from "lume/mod.ts";
import blog from "https://deno.land/x/lume_theme_simple_blog@v0.5.0/mod.ts";

const site = lume();

site.use(blog());

export default site;
```

Followed the [Deployment](https://lume.land/docs/advanced/deployment/) docs and created a deno project, the `deno.yml`, and a `serve.ts`.

`deno.yml`
```yml
name: Publish on Deno Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read

    steps:
      - name: Clone repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v2

      - name: Setup Deno environment
        uses: denoland/setup-deno@v1
        with:
          deno-version: v1.x

      - name: Build site
        run: deno task build

      - name: Deploy to Deno Deploy
        uses: denoland/deployctl@v1
        with:
          project: <my deno project name>
          import-map: "./import_map.json"
          entrypoint: serve.ts

```

`serve.ts`
```typescript
import Server from "lume/core/server.ts";

const server = new Server({
  port: 8000,
  root: `${Deno.cwd()}/_site`,
});

server.start();

console.log("Listening on http://localhost:8000");
```

Enabled actions on my Github repository after pushing.  Action ran and failed with the error:
```

Using binary file at /home/runner/work/what-it-b-log/what-it-b-log/_bin/pagefind

Error: Pagefind exited with code 2



/home/runner/work/what-it-b-log/what-it-b-log/_bin/pagefind: 1: Syntax error: word unexpected (expecting ")")

    at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.17.5/plugins/pagefind.ts:192:15)
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:183:11)
    at async Events.dispatchEvent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.17.5/core/events.ts:49:11)
    at async Site.build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.17.5/core/site.ts:538:5)
    at async build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.17.5/cli/build.ts:41:3)
    at async Command.execute (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1794:7)
    at async Command.parseCommand (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1639:14)
    at async (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.17.5/cli.ts:134:3)
Error: Process completed with exit code 1.
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Every retry of the build fails in the exact same way.

### What is the expected behavior?

Deployment succeeds.

### What do you see instead?

Deployment failure with above mentioned error.

### Additional information

_No response_

1782625515.Title: CLI also shows helpful information, when normally `error: No config file found` would be thrown.
1782625515.Author: almereyda
1782625515.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

In trying to wrap my head around using the Lume CLI

- https://github.com/lumeland/cli/issues/1
- #232
- #34

I am trying to get help by the CLI about how it can be used, but it always complains, because I'm not in a project directory.

```
$ [[ $PWD = $HOME ]]; echo $? 
0
$ deno task lume -h
error: No config file found
$ lume -h          
error: No config file found
```

I should be able to get help, also when I'm not in a project directory.
1782625515.PlainText:
CLI also shows helpful information, when normally error: No config file found would be thrown.

Enter your suggestions in details:
In trying to wrap my head around using the Lume CLI
#232
#34
I am trying to get help by the CLI about how it can be used, but it always complains, because I'm not in a project directory.
I should be able to get help, also when I'm not in a project directory.

Comments.Body:
This is caused by the `init.ts` script registering `lume` as a task in `deno.json`, as opposed to creating an executable in some directory on your `$PATH`. E.g. assuming you have `$HOME/.local/bin` on your `$PATH` then you could create `~/.local/bin/lume` with:

```
deno run -A https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.18.1/cli.ts "$@"
```

(and mark it as executable with `chmod +x ~/.local/bin/lume`) which would let you run `lume` in any directory.

The disadvantage of this approach is that then:
* the used lume version is no longer specified in your project directory, which usually is a good idea since it ideally makes your build reproducible
* the barrier is higher for other contributors (but that might not apply for personal projects) ... if you still have the task in `deno.json` they might end up using a different lume version than you, if you use your own executable
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

@oscarotero I've just started using `lume`, and I think I've hit a similar problem with a `node:process` import going through the esbuild plugin.

In my case, it seems to be coming from https://esm.sh/v128/async-validator@4.2.5/denonext/async-validator.mjs which is being pulled through some transitive dependencies.

The output says:
```
Caused by Error: Build failed with 1 error:
deno:https://esm.sh/v128/async-validator@4.2.5/denonext/async-validator.mjs:2:23:
ERROR: [plugin: lumeLoader] No such file or directory (os error 2): readfile 'node:process'
```
Comments.Author: lucab
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero thanks for the hint, indeed I completely overlooked that detail.

Starting from your observation , it seems that the esbuild plugin is requesting the Deno version of the module to esm.sh (see the `denonext` in the URL above).
In turn, that causes esm.sh to add a `deno:process` polyfill to the code. Requesting the `es2015` version instead does inject https://esm.sh/v128/node_process.js as the polyfill for browsers. 
See the difference between the following:
 * `denonext` -> https://esm.sh/v128/async-validator@4.2.5/denonext/async-validator.mjs
 * `es2015` -> https://esm.sh/v128/async-validator@4.2.5/es2015/async-validator.mjs (the import URL is buggy though)
Comments.Author: lucab
oscarotero

1791138842.Title: RFC: Putting code, tests and docs of plugins next to each other
1791138842.Author: not-my-profile
1791138842.Body:
The files making up a plugin are currently scattered across three different locations:

* `plugins/` in the lume repository contains the actual code, e.g. `plugins/katex.ts`
* `tests/` in the lume repository contains the test code, e.g. `tests/katex.test.ts`
* [`plugins/` in the lume.land repository](https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/tree/6b1fddaf01c19d3c9d58c20d38d8a4dd526054b3/plugins) contains the documentation, e.g. `plugins/katex.md`

I think it would make sense to instead put all these files next to each other in this repository, e.g. we could have:

* `plugins/katex/katex.ts`
* `plugins/katex/katex.test.ts`
* `plugins/katex/README.md`

The reasoning being that if you update the code, you likely also should update the tests and the documentation as well and that's easier to do when the files are all next to each other. Putting them next to each other also makes it easier to spot discrepancies. (Especially having the .md files in another repository makes contributing to plugins more difficult since you'd often need two PRs.)
1791138842.PlainText:
RFC: Putting code, tests and docs of plugins next to each other

The files making up a plugin are currently scattered across three different locations:
plugins/ in the lume repository contains the actual code, e.g. plugins/katex.ts
tests/ in the lume repository contains the test code, e.g. tests/katex.test.ts
plugins/ in the lume.land repository contains the documentation, e.g. plugins/katex.md
I think it would make sense to instead put all these files next to each other in this repository, e.g. we could have:
plugins/katex/katex.ts
plugins/katex/katex.test.ts
plugins/katex/README.md
The reasoning being that if you update the code, you likely also should update the tests and the documentation as well and that's easier to do when the files are all next to each other. Putting them next to each other also makes it easier to spot discrepancies. (Especially having the .md files in another repository makes contributing to plugins more difficult since you'd often need two PRs.)

Comments.Body:
Yeah, it makes sense but I can see some drawbacks:

- From users POV, it requires to repeat the plugin name in the imported url. It's clearer to import it from `lume/plugins/katext.ts` thank `lume/plugins/katext/katext.ts`.
- It's a breaking change: all sites built with Lume would break
- Tests are not only TypeScript files (like `katex.test.ts`) but they need assets (stored in `/tests/assets`) and they generate snapshots (stored in `/tests/__snapshots__`).
- Some plugins need other plugins to work. For example, Tailwind requires Postcss, or SourceMap is used by other plugins like Postcss, Esbuild, SASS, etc.
- We must to figure out a way to import the plugins documentation in the lume.land site if they are in different repositories.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks for the proposal, but I think it's better as it is
Comments.Author: oscarotero
